title: Triund Trek
summary: 2D/1N from Bhagsunag, Dharamshala — waterfall trail, Dhauladhar views, and camping under the stars. From ₹999.
overview:
The Triund trek in the Himalayas is a relatively easy and popular trail, offering stunning views of the snow-covered Dhauladhar ranges and Kangra Valley. It is an excellent option for anyone looking for a hassle-free weekend trek in the region. The trail’s end has a steep climb, but the overall route is short.

Triund is one of the most popular treks in India. The vast expanse near Triund Top boasts lush green meadows with deodar, oak, pine, and rhododendron forests — ideal for nature lovers. Whether you prefer a warm summer outing or a snowy winter walk, the trek is open through much of the year, except during the heavy monsoon season.

Join us for an adventure to witness nature at its finest. Book the Triund trek with friends or family this weekend and experience the magic of the Dhauladhars.

itinerary.title: Bhagsunag to Karthani, Climbing Onto Triund's Ridge
itinerary.trailType: stone-paved village lane, oak and rhododendron forest, open ridge meadow
itinerary.description: The Triund trail begins at Bhagsunag, where a modest stone temple dedicated to the snake deity Nag Devta marks the true trailhead just above the roar of the Bhagsu waterfall. From here the path climbs immediately through a belt of horse-chestnut and oak, the ground underfoot a mix of packed mud and half-buried rock that grows steadily rougher as the village rooftops of Bhagsu recede below. Within the first hour, the trail merges with the route from Dharamkot, and the forest opens intermittently to reveal the Kangra valley spreading out toward the plains, a patchwork of tea gardens and terraced fields hazy in the midday light. A string of small tea shacks appears every twenty minutes or so along this stretch, each one a welcome excuse to refill water bottles from stored supplies, since natural streams on this particular face of the ridge are scarce and seasonal. Rhododendron trees, gnarled and lichen-streaked, begin to dominate as the gradient steepens past the halfway mark, their canopy offering intermittent shade on what can otherwise be a sun-exposed climb through the late morning hours. The final push onto the open ridge is the trail's most demanding section, a series of rocky switchbacks that leave the tree line behind entirely and deliver walkers onto the broad grassy shelf known locally as Karthani, sitting just below Triund's main crest. From this shelf, the Dhauladhar's serrated wall rises with startling immediacy, close enough that individual snow gullies and rock ribs are clearly distinguishable even without binoculars. Tents are pitched on the flattest patches of turf, and by late afternoon a bank of cloud often rolls in from the Kangra side, wrapping the campsite in cool mist before clearing again toward evening. Dinner is taken huddled close to a kerosene stove or, when permitted, a small fire, and the guide runs through basic precautions for the pre-dawn summit push: sturdy footwear, headlamps checked, and an early bedtime despite the temptation to stargaze under a sky remarkably free of light pollution.
highlights: Bhagsu waterfall and Nag Devta shrine at the trailhead
Sweeping views of the Kangra valley through the oak forest
Rhododendron-lined switchbacks onto the open ridge
Close-up views of the Dhauladhar's rock and snow wall from Karthani
tips: Fill water bottles at the tea shacks lower down; the ridge itself has no reliable source
Carry a headlamp for the pre-dawn walk to Triund Top
Pack a warm layer, as cloud and wind can roll in fast once you're on the open ridge

itinerary.title: Sunrise at Triund Top and the Descent to Bhagsunag
itinerary.trailType: rocky ridge scramble, rhododendron switchbacks, stone village lane
itinerary.description: Most trekkers are up well before sunrise on this final day, leaving the tents at Karthani in darkness for the short, steep scramble to Triund Top, a rocky knoll just above camp that offers one of the most accessible high-altitude sunrise views in the Dhauladhar. As the light strengthens, the peaks above Indrahar Pass catch the first colour, shifting from grey to pink to a hard, brilliant white within a matter of minutes, while the Kangra valley below stays in shadow far longer, giving the impression of standing at the exact boundary between night and day. After breakfast back at camp, the group packs up and begins the long descent by retracing the ridge trail down through the rhododendron belt, the walking noticeably faster now that gravity is doing most of the work, though knees and toes take the brunt of the effort on the looser, rockier sections nearer the top. The forest closes in again below Karthani, and the same string of tea shacks passed on the way up now serve as convenient rest points, several offering Maggi noodles and sweet tea that taste disproportionately good after a night at altitude. Below the Dharamkot junction, the trail narrows into the stone-paved lane that leads directly into Bhagsu village, passing terraced vegetable patches and the occasional grazing mule tethered outside a farmhouse. The roar of the Bhagsu waterfall grows steadily louder in the last stretch, and many groups pause here to soak tired feet in the cold plunge pool at its base before the final few minutes back to the temple courtyard where the trek began. Weather permitting, the whole descent from Triund Top to Bhagsunag takes a little under five hours at a comfortable pace, though sudden afternoon cloud is common enough in the shoulder seasons that starting the descent promptly after the sunrise viewing is strongly advised.
highlights: Pre-dawn scramble to Triund Top for sunrise over the Dhauladhar
Watching the Kangra valley emerge from shadow at daybreak
Tea-shack stops on the descent through rhododendron forest
Cooling off at the Bhagsu waterfall plunge pool
tips: Leave camp at least 30-40 minutes before actual sunrise to reach Triund Top in time
Descend promptly after sunrise viewing, as afternoon cloud builds quickly in shoulder seasons
Save a change of dry socks for the Bhagsu waterfall stop if you plan to dip your feet

fitness.description: Triund suits beginners with basic fitness. Expect 3–5 hours of walking with a sustained uphill section. You should be comfortable on inclined trails with a light daypack.
tips: Walk 3–4 km daily for two weeks before departure
Practice stair climbs with a light backpack
Hydrate well and avoid alcohol the night before
Break in your trek shoes beforehand
Arrive at Bhagsunag by 10:00 AM on Day 1

map.overview: Start and end at Bhagsunag, Dharamshala (Kangra district). Day 1 climbs ~7 km via Bhagsu waterfall to Karthani camp. Day 2 summits Triund Top (~2,850 m) then descends ~8 km back to Bhagsunag. Reach Dharamshala by air (Gaggal), overnight bus from Delhi/Chandigarh, or train to Pathankot plus a 2–3 hour road transfer.
map.elevationNote: Max altitude ~2,850 m (9,350 ft). Steep final approach to Triund Top.

weather.note: Snow possible

faqs.question: Is the network available in Triund?
faqs.answer: During the trek, there are spots where the cell network is accessible. When you reach the top, the network often disappears. It is best to let your family know that you may not be reachable for some time.

faqs.question: What is the best way to book a Triund Trek online?
faqs.answer: You can book the Triund Trek package directly on this website. The standard camping package starts from ₹999 per person and covers camping, guide support, dinner, and breakfast as listed in the inclusions.

faqs.question: Is camping allowed in Triund?
faqs.answer: Night camping rules can change with forest department guidelines. When permitted, camping is arranged in designated zones to manage crowd and environmental impact. Your trek leader will brief you on the approved overnight location (typically Karthani / Triund zone).

faqs.question: Can Triund trek be done in one day?
faqs.answer: Yes, Triund can be completed as a single-day hike in about 4–6 hours. For sunrise, sunset, and a night under the stars, we recommend the 2 days / 1 night camping package.

faqs.question: Is Triund Trek safe for kids?
faqs.answer: Yes, families often do this trek, but the climb can tire younger children. Be prepared to help or carry kids on steeper stretches. Share your child’s age with us when booking so we can advise accordingly.

faqs.question: Is night stay allowed in Triund?
faqs.answer: Overnight camping in the Triund area is allowed as per current state guidelines when arranged through approved operators and designated camping zones. Availability depends on weather and permits on the day of the trek.

faqs.question: Can a beginner do Triund Trek?
faqs.answer: The route has sustained uphill walking and can feel tiring for absolute first-timers, but with an easy-to-moderate grade and guide support, beginners with basic fitness regularly complete it successfully.

faqs.question: How much does the Triund trek cost?
faqs.answer: Triund Trek with camping generally starts from ₹999 per person for triple sharing. Double sharing options are available at a higher rate. Exact package prices are shown on this page.

faqs.question: Which time is best for Triund Trek?
faqs.answer: The best months are May, June, September, and October. Weather is generally pleasant, though nights can be chilly. Monsoon is scenic but less ideal for overnight camping; winter can bring snow and extra cold.

faqs.question: Is Triund Trek safe for girls?
faqs.answer: Yes. Triund is a popular and well-trafficked trail, commonly done by solo travelers and women trekkers when booking with a certified guided group.

faqs.question: Is Triund trek difficult?
faqs.answer: It is rated easy to moderate. The trek is short but includes a steep final climb. Most fit beginners complete it comfortably with paced breaks.

faqs.question: How long is a Triund Trek?
faqs.answer: The round trip is around 16 km (both ways). A day hike typically takes 4–6 hours. With camping, plan for 2 days / 1 night to enjoy sunset, night sky, and sunrise at or near Triund Top.

faqs.question: What is included in the trek fee?
faqs.answer: Evening tea, dinner and breakfast (veg), experienced certified trekking guide, camping gear (tents, sleeping bags, mats, kitchen utensils), first-aid kit with the team, and pit-style toilet tents. See the full inclusions list on this page.

faqs.question: How do I reach the starting point?
faqs.answer: Reach Dharamshala / McLeod Ganj first. The trek starts from Bhagsunag Taxi Stand. By air: Gaggal Airport. By bus: overnight coaches from Delhi (about 8–10 hours) or Chandigarh. By train: Pathankot, then a 2–3 hour bus or cab to Dharamshala. Arrive by 10:00 AM on Day 1.

faqs.question: What is your cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the tour cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available in those cases. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

title: Kareri Lake Trek
summary: 2D/1N from Kareri Village near Dharamshala — oak forests, Nyund stream, and a high freshwater alpine lake. From ₹1,499.
overview:
Kareri Lake sits at the foothills of the Dhauladhar range in Kangra Valley, about 9 km above Kareri Village. It is a high-altitude freshwater lake fed by meltwater from snowy peaks, with clear shallows where you can often see the lake bed.

The approach winds through silver oak, rhododendron, and Chir pine beside the Nyund stream. Meadows around the lake double as seasonal grazing grounds for Gaddi shepherds before they cross Minkiani and Baleni passes toward Chamba and Bharmour.

From December to March the lake frequently freezes and access may need technical mountaineering gear. In open season it is an easy-to-moderate weekend trek for fit beginners who want quieter trails than the busiest Dharamshala day hikes.

itinerary.title: Ghera to Kareri Along the Nallah
itinerary.trailType: riverside dirt track, pine and deodar forest, terraced hamlet lanes
itinerary.description: The trek to Kareri Lake begins at Ghera, a small roadhead village reached by a winding drive out of Dharamshala through terraced fields of maize and wheat. From the last houses of Ghera, the trail drops briefly to cross a wooden footbridge over a tributary stream before climbing gently along the true left bank of what will become the Kareri Nallah, the glacial-fed torrent that the entire trek follows almost to its source. The path here is a wide dirt track, still used by mules ferrying supplies, and it winds through stands of chir pine interspersed with wild walnut and pomegranate trees growing informally along the field edges of scattered farmsteads. Gaddi shepherd families, recognisable by their distinctive woollen caps and the long staffs they carry, are a common sight moving flocks of sheep and goats between grazing grounds at this stage of the season, and their dogs often announce a group's approach from a considerable distance. As the valley narrows, the forest thickens into deodar, and the trail crosses two smaller side streams on flat stepping stones before the settlement of Kareri itself comes into view, its slate-roofed houses arranged along a shelf above the true right bank of the nallah. Kareri is larger than most trailside villages in this part of the Dhauladhar, with a school, a small temple, and several homes that take in trekkers for the night. The afternoon is left free for a short walk around the village fields, where sweeping views back down the valley toward Dharamshala are framed by the first close look at the Dhauladhar's jagged skyline above. Dinner is a simple home-cooked meal of rice, dal, and a seasonal vegetable, often shared with the host family around a wood-fired hearth, and the evening briefing covers the considerably steeper and longer walk to the lake that awaits the following day.
highlights: Following the Kareri Nallah upstream from the roadhead
Gaddi shepherd flocks moving through the valley
Stepping-stone crossings over side streams
Home-cooked dinner and hearth-side evening in Kareri village
tips: Book a homestay in Kareri in advance during peak weekends, as rooms are limited
Keep a walking stick handy for the stepping-stone stream crossings
Ask your host about recent weather at the lake before setting off next morning

itinerary.title: Kareri to the Sacred Waters of Kareri Lake
itinerary.trailType: forest trail beside the Kareri Nallah, boulder-strewn riverbed, alpine meadow approach
itinerary.description: Leaving Kareri, the trail immediately steepens and commits to a long, sustained climb that gains roughly three thousand feet over the course of the day. The route hugs the Kareri Nallah closely for the first two hours, the water turning a milkier shade of grey as glacial sediment increases with altitude, and the forest transitions from deodar to birch and then to stunted juniper as height is gained. A boulder field roughly midway demands careful, unhurried footwork, the rocks slick in places where spray from the tumbling stream reaches the trail, and this stretch is typically where the group takes its packed lunch, sheltered behind a large outcrop that blocks the wind funnelling down from the pass above. Beyond the boulders, the valley broadens into a series of grassy shelves grazed by ponies and the occasional herd of goats, and the Dhauladhar's high ridgeline, previously glimpsed only in fragments, now dominates the entire skyline ahead, its snow patches persisting well into the trekking season. The final approach to Kareri Lake follows a gentler grassy slope, and the lake itself appears with a suddenness that catches most first-time visitors off guard: a still, dark-green sheet of water cupped directly beneath the Dhauladhar's rock wall, with clouds and peaks reflected on its surface whenever the wind drops. According to local belief, the lake is sacred to the sage Kareshwar Rishi, and a small collection of prayer flags near the shore is maintained by shepherd families and trekking groups alike. Camp is pitched on a flat grassy bench a short distance from the water's edge, chosen to avoid disturbing the immediate shoreline, and the temperature drops sharply the moment the sun dips behind the ridge. Evenings here can bring sudden gusts funnelling off the peaks, so tents are staked down thoroughly, and dinner is taken early, the group retreating to sleeping bags well before the cold fully sets in, with the lake's dark surface visible from the tent flaps under a sky crowded with stars.
highlights: Sustained climb beside the Kareri Nallah through changing forest zones
Boulder-field crossing with the Dhauladhar wall coming into view
Arrival at the sacred, mirror-still Kareri Lake
Camping within sight of the Dhauladhar's snow-streaked ridge
tips: Wear grippy footwear for the slick boulder-field section
Respect the lake's sanctity by keeping a distance from the immediate shoreline
Stake tents down firmly, as evening gusts off the ridge can be sudden and strong

itinerary.title: Kareri Lake Back to Ghera, Trek's End
itinerary.trailType: retraced forest trail, riverside descent, village road
itinerary.description: The return to Ghera is a single long day that retraces the entire route in reverse, and it begins with a last, quiet look at Kareri Lake in the flat early light before the surrounding peaks catch any colour. Descending from the lakeside bench, the trail drops quickly through the grazing shelves, and the same grassy slopes that felt endless on the way up pass by in a fraction of the time now that the walking is downhill. The boulder field partway down demands the same careful footing as the ascent, if not more, since loose stones underfoot are easier to slip on when moving quickly with the momentum of a descent, and the guide typically calls for a short, deliberate pace through this stretch rather than rushing. Below the boulders, the forest thickens back into birch and then deodar, and the Kareri Nallah's roar becomes a constant companion again, its milky glacial colour gradually clearing as elevation is lost and side streams add cleaner water to the flow. Kareri village offers a natural, welcome midpoint for a break, and many groups stop for tea or a light meal at the same homestay used two nights earlier before continuing the final, gentler stretch back toward Ghera. This lower section passes again through the pine and walnut groves and the scattered farmsteads that marked the very start of the trek, and the sense of returning to a lower, warmer world becomes obvious in the thicker air and the reappearing sounds of village life, from barking dogs to the clatter of someone's tin roof in the wind. Reaching Ghera by mid-to-late afternoon, tired legs are rewarded with the trek's formal end point and, usually, a waiting vehicle for the drive back into Dharamshala, giving the group time to reflect on the dramatic shift in scenery packed into just three days, from terraced farmland to a genuinely high, glacier-fed lake.
highlights: A final quiet look at Kareri Lake in the early morning light
Careful descent through the boulder field
A tea stop back in Kareri village
Return to Ghera's terraced farmland by late afternoon
tips: Start early, since this is the longest single day of the trek
Take the boulder-field descent slowly rather than rushing on tired legs
Keep some cash on hand for a tea stop in Kareri village on the way down

faqs.question: Can we visit Kareri Lake in December?
faqs.answer: April to December generally works for organising the trek, covering summer, monsoon, and early winter moods. From late December through March the lake often freezes and may need technical gear — check conditions before you book a winter date.

faqs.question: Which trek is better — Triund or Kareri?
faqs.answer: Triund is busier and closer to McLeod Ganj, with classic Dhauladhar views. Kareri Lake is quieter and suits hikers who want a freshwater lake, forest trails, and fewer crowds. Many travellers do both on separate weekends.

faqs.question: Does it snow at Kareri Lake?
faqs.answer: Yes. At this altitude snow is common mid-December through February. Trails and the lake shore can be snowbound — winter visits need warmer gear and guide judgement on route safety.

faqs.question: Can Kareri Lake be reached by car?
faqs.answer: No. Drive or take a cab to Kareri Village (Kareri Khas) near Dharamshala, then begin the trek on foot. The lake itself is reached only by trail.

faqs.question: Is there network at Kareri Lake?
faqs.answer: Expect little or no coverage on the trail and at the lake. Even BSNL is intermittent. Carry cash — digital payments are unreliable once you leave the village.

faqs.question: How can I reach Kareri from Chandigarh?
faqs.answer: Buses and shared cabs run Chandigarh to Dharamshala (roughly 6–7 hours). From Dharamshala, hire a taxi to Kareri Village and start the trek by 10:00 AM on Day 1.

faqs.question: How do I go to Kareri Lake from Dharamshala?
faqs.answer: Hire a jeep or cab from Dharamshala toward Ghera / Kareri Village (about 20+ km by road depending on route). The trek starts from Kareri Village and follows the Nyund stream uphill to the lake.

faqs.question: Can I do the Kareri Lake trek alone?
faqs.answer: Solo travellers regularly join guided groups. Booking with a certified guide is safer for navigation, weather calls, and camping logistics.

faqs.question: How do you do the Kareri Trek?
faqs.answer: Reach Dharamshala first. Local buses go toward Ghera; from Ghera, Kareri Village is about a 2-hour walk, or take a cab direct to the village. Meet the group by 10:00 AM and trek via Riyoti to the lake camp.

faqs.question: How long is the trek from Kareri Village?
faqs.answer: One way is roughly 9–10 km (about 20 km both ways). Expect 4–5 hours uphill via Riyoti halfway camp, and 3–4 hours on the return.

faqs.question: Can you swim in Kareri Lake?
faqs.answer: It is a shallow freshwater lake. Some people swim in summer when the water is open; in winter the surface often freezes under deep snow on the approach — swimming is not advisable then.

faqs.question: Can we do Kareri Lake trek in one day?
faqs.answer: A same-day out-and-back is tough (roughly 20 km both ways with climb). Most people prefer 2 days / 1 night to enjoy the lakeside camp and avoid a rushed dark descent.

faqs.question: Is Kareri Lake Trek easy?
faqs.answer: It is easy to intermediate for anyone with reasonable fitness. Much of the path is straightforward, but some rocky uphill stretches demand paced walking and sturdy shoes.

faqs.question: What is included in the trek fee?
faqs.answer: Day 1 evening tea and dinner, Day 2 breakfast, certified guide, camping gear (tents, sleeping bags, mats, kitchen utensils), bonfire when available, and pit-style toilet tents. Forest or other permits are not included.

faqs.question: What is your cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the tour cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available in those cases. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

title: Indrahar Pass Trek
summary: Cross the iconic Indrahar Pass (≈4,342 m) on the Dhauladhar divide between Kangra and Chamba — serious altitude, scree, and huge Himalayan views. From ₹8,999.
overview:
Indrahar Pass Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Cross the iconic Indrahar Pass (≈4,342 m) on the Dhauladhar divide between Kangra and Chamba — serious altitude, scree, and huge Himalayan views.

Indrahar is the classic Kangra–Chamba gateway: scree, thin air, and a sudden reveal of Chamba’s layered ranges once you crest the notch. This is not a casual weekend extension of Triund — treat it as a proper high pass.

The itinerary usually starts at Bhagsunag / Dharamkot and ends at Chamba side or return via McLeod, spanning about 32 km across 4 day(s) with a high point near 14,250 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Indrahar Pass corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Indrahar Pass Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Indrahar Pass main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Indrahar Pass camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Indrahar Pass: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in McLeod Ganj a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Indrahar Pass Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Kareri Village Trek, Minkiani Pass Trek, Guna Devi Trek, Snowline Laka Trek.

Child policy for Indrahar Pass Trek: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Indrahar Pass Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

itinerary.title: McLeod Ganj to Triund via Dal Lake and Gallu Devi
itinerary.trailType: forest lane past a hillside lake, oak and rhododendron switchbacks, open ridge meadow
itinerary.description: Setting out from the small square in McLeod Ganj, the trail to Triund climbs past guesthouses and Tibetan prayer-flag-strung rooftops before entering forest just beyond the last chai stall on the outskirts of town. The initial gradient is gentle, and the path merges after twenty minutes or so with the route climbing from Dharamkot, passing close to the small, reed-fringed Dal Lake tucked into a fold of the hillside, a favourite stop for local monks and a scatter of grazing horses. Beyond the lake, the forest deepens into oak and horse-chestnut, and a short detour leads to the whitewashed shrine of Gallu Devi, considered the guardian deity of this stretch of the Dhauladhar and a customary stop for blessings before the climb proper begins. From here the trail steepens noticeably, switchbacking through increasingly open rhododendron cover, and the views back over McLeod Ganj and the wooded slopes around the Dalai Lama's residence at Tsuglagkhang grow more expansive with every turn. A handful of small tea houses punctuate the route at roughly hourly intervals, each a useful marker of progress as much as a place to rest, and their owners often share informal updates on conditions higher up, useful information this early in a trek that will eventually reach a genuine mountain pass. The final hour climbs through a band of stunted, wind-bent trees before opening abruptly onto Triund's broad grassy ridge, where the full sweep of the Dhauladhar appears at close range for the first time, a wall of grey rock and hanging snowfields that will occupy the group's full attention for the next three days. Camp is set up on the ridge's flatter western end, away from the day-tripper crowds that often gather near the main viewpoint, and the evening briefing focuses on acclimatisation and the technical notes for the days ahead, since this is only the first, gentlest stage of a considerably more demanding route.
highlights: Passing Dal Lake and the Gallu Devi shrine on the way up
Views over McLeod Ganj and the Dalai Lama's residence
Tea-house stops with informal updates on trail conditions
First close-range views of the Dhauladhar's snowfields from Triund
tips: Stop at Gallu Devi for a blessing if you're inclined, but keep moving before the midday sun
Ask tea-house owners for the latest word on snow conditions higher up
Camp on the quieter western end of the ridge, away from day-tripper crowds

itinerary.title: Triund to Lahesh Cave, Gateway to the Pass
itinerary.trailType: steep grass slope, loose scree, rock overhang camp
itinerary.description: The short distance from Triund to Lahesh Cave belies how much the terrain changes in just a few hours of walking. Leaving the grassy ridge, the trail crosses a stretch of coarse alpine turf before tackling a long, punishing slope of loose scree and grass tussocks that gains height relentlessly with almost no level ground to recover on. Streams here are seasonal, often reduced to a trickle by late summer, so bottles are topped up wherever water is found rather than assuming the next source will be reliable. Marmots are a near-constant presence on this stretch, their sharp alarm whistles echoing across the slope whenever the group draws close, and their burrows riddle the hillside just off the main trail. As the gradient eases slightly near the top, the view opens dramatically backward over Triund and, beyond it, the entire Kangra valley receding into a blue haze toward the plains, a perspective considerably wider than anything seen the previous day. Lahesh Cave itself is not a true cave but a large, overhanging rock slab that has sheltered shepherds and trekkers for generations, its underside blackened by decades of cooking fires, and it provides just enough cover for a handful of tents to pitch in its lee, protected from the worst of any overnight wind or sudden hail. The setting is stark: bare rock, sparse cushion plants, and, directly ahead, the final rocky approach to Indrahar Pass rising in clear silhouette against the sky. Afternoons here often bring a build-up of cloud around the high peaks, and the guide watches this closely, since it offers the best indication of what conditions might look like for the pass crossing at first light tomorrow. Dinner is eaten early and warmly wrapped, and most trekkers report a noticeably harder time settling into sleep at this altitude, a normal response the guide checks in on before lights-out.
highlights: A short but relentlessly steep climb from Triund
Marmot colonies scattered across the scree slope
Shelter beneath the historic overhanging rock of Lahesh Cave
First direct sightline to the rocky approach of Indrahar Pass
tips: Top up water at every stream you cross; sources above Lahesh Cave are unreliable
Watch the afternoon cloud build-up as an informal weather forecast for the pass day
Expect a harder night's sleep at this altitude and mention any discomfort to your guide

itinerary.title: Crossing Indrahar Pass and Returning to Lahesh Cave
itinerary.trailType: boulder scramble, snow or ice traverse near the pass, exposed rock saddle
itinerary.description: This is the day the entire trek pivots around, and the group leaves Lahesh Cave in darkness, headlamps picking out the boulder trail as it climbs steadily toward the pass. The going is a mix of large, stable rock and looser scree, and in the trek's shoulder seasons a stretch of hard-packed snow or old ice usually has to be crossed just below the saddle, a section where guides fix a handline and insist on microspikes or crampons regardless of how confident anyone feels on foot. Indrahar Pass itself sits at roughly 14,245 feet, a narrow, wind-raked notch between two rock towers, and reaching it delivers one of the most complete panoramas available on any short Himachal trek: the entire Kangra valley and Dhauladhar's southern face behind, and, opening ahead for the first time, the folded, drier ridgelines of the Chamba side dropping toward the Ravi river system. Prayer flags strung across the gap snap loudly in wind that rarely lets up even on calm mornings lower down, and time at the top is necessarily brief, both because of the cold and because descending the snow section safely is easier while the surface is still firm from the overnight freeze. The descent to Lahesh Cave retraces the morning's route, and while gravity helps, the loose scree below the snowline demands just as much concentration as the ascent did, particularly for anyone carrying fatigue from the early start. Back at camp by mid-afternoon, there is a palpable shift in mood, equal parts relief and quiet pride, and the guide typically uses the remaining daylight to check everyone's feet and hands for early signs of cold exposure before the group settles in for a second, calmer night beneath the same rock overhang, this time with the pass already behind them rather than looming ahead.
highlights: Crossing Indrahar Pass at approximately 14,245 ft
First views of the Chamba side and the Ravi river valleys
Snow or ice traverse secured with a fixed handline
Prayer flags snapping in the wind at the pass
tips: Wear or carry microspikes; the snow section near the pass is rarely fully melted
Keep summit time brief and prioritise a safe, controlled descent over lingering for photos
Check fingers and toes for cold exposure once back at camp

itinerary.title: Lahesh Cave to McLeod Ganj, Full Circle Down
itinerary.trailType: scree descent, ridge grassland, forest switchbacks into town
itinerary.description: With the pass safely crossed, the final day is dedicated entirely to descent, and the scale of it becomes clear within the first hour as the trail drops back down the same scree and grass slope climbed two days earlier, now considerably easier without the accumulated fatigue of an unacclimatised body. Triund appears sooner than expected, and the ridge that felt like a serious high-altitude campsite on day one now feels almost like a lowland waypoint by comparison, crowded with day-trippers who arrived by an entirely different, far shorter route and have no sense of what lies beyond the horizon they're photographing. A short break here allows for a last, appreciative look back at the pass, its notch barely visible now against the jagged skyline, before the trail commits to the long forested descent toward McLeod Ganj. Rhododendron and oak reassert themselves quickly, and the temperature climbs noticeably with every few hundred feet lost, jackets coming off in stages as the group descends through the same tea-house stretch used on the first day, though this time most walkers barely pause, eager to close out the trek. Occasional clusters of langurs move through the canopy near the lower forest, indifferent to the passing trekkers, and the trail's final approach into McLeod Ganj arrives via the same Dharamkot junction and Dal Lake path used at the outset, bringing the route full circle. Reaching the town's main square by mid-afternoon, the group is met by the ordinary bustle of cafes, prayer wheels, and souvenir stalls that feels almost disorienting after four days spent almost entirely above the tree line. A closing debrief typically covers the trek's key moments, and most groups agree that the pass crossing, however brief it was at the top, was worth every step of the approach and the long walk back down.
highlights: Retracing the scree descent from Lahesh Cave with far less fatigue
A last look back at Indrahar Pass from Triund
Langur sightings in the lower forest
Returning full circle through Dharamkot to McLeod Ganj
tips: Use trekking poles on the long scree descent to spare your knees
Keep a lighter layer handy, as temperature rises quickly on the way down
Budget time for a closing debrief with your guide before dispersing in McLeod Ganj

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated challenging. The Indrahar Pass Trek demands challenging effort across 4 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 14250 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Indrahar Pass Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 4D / 3N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 32 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 14,250 ft (4,343 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Indrahar Pass Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Bhagsunag / Dharamkot. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Indrahar Pass Trek trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Designated Indrahar Pass camping zones Backup lower camp in bad weather

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Indrahar Pass Trek demands challenging effort across 4 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 14250 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to McLeod Ganj. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Bhagsunag / Dharamkot.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Indrahar Pass Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Indrahar Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in McLeod Ganj. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Indrahar Pass Trek crowded?
faqs.answer: Indrahar Pass sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Indrahar Pass with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

title: Minkiani Pass Trek
summary: A demanding Dhauladhar crossing above Kareri Lake via Minkiani Pass — remote meadows, high camps, and few crowds. From ₹11,999.
overview:
Minkiani Pass Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. A demanding Dhauladhar crossing above Kareri Lake via Minkiani Pass — remote meadows, high camps, and few crowds.

Minkiani sits above the Kareri basin as a lonelier sibling to Indrahar. Expect long meadow days, cold nights, and navigation that rewards patience more than speed.

The itinerary usually starts at Kareri Village and ends at Chamba / return via Kareri, spanning about 40 km across 5 day(s) with a high point near 13,900 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Minkiani Pass corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Minkiani Pass Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Minkiani Pass main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Minkiani Pass camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Minkiani Pass: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dharamshala a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Minkiani Pass Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Guna Devi Trek, Bhagsu Waterfall Trek, Dharamkot Trek, Kareri Lake Trek.

Child policy for Minkiani Pass Trek: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Minkiani Pass Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

Video and extended gallery placeholders are reserved for Website Management CMS uploads.

Fixed departure calendar and live pricing shown in the booking panel remain editable from CMS.

itinerary.title: Ghera to Kareri, First Steps Toward Minkiani
itinerary.trailType: orchard path, deodar forest track, terraced village shelf
itinerary.description: For a trek that will eventually approach one of the Dhauladhar's higher and lesser-visited passes, the opening day is deliberately unhurried, easing the group in with a walk of only moderate length from the roadhead at Ghera. Pack ponies loaded with tents and ration boxes for the coming days set off just ahead of the group, their bells providing an intermittent soundtrack to the first stretch of trail, which follows a broad path above scattered fields of apricot and walnut trees planted generations ago by families still farming the same plots. The Kareri Nallah keeps pace with the trail on the valley floor, its sound a constant low presence even where the path climbs a little distance above the water to skirt a rocky bluff. Wild bee boxes, tended informally by local households, appear fixed to several trailside trees, a small but telling sign of how thoroughly this valley's economy is woven into its forest. As the trail gains a little height, chir pine gives way to deodar, and the first proper views back toward Dharamshala's scattered rooftops open up through gaps in the canopy. Buntings and Himalayan bulbuls flit through the lower branches in numbers, and a resident troop of langurs is often spotted foraging on the forested slope above the trail's midpoint. Kareri village, when it comes into view, sits handsomely on its shelf above the nallah, and the group settles into a homestay for the night, using the relatively short walking day to properly sort gear for the considerably longer and higher stages ahead. The evening briefing here is more detailed than a typical first-night talk, since Minkiani is a genuine pass crossing attempt rather than a simple there-and-back walk, and the guide spends real time confirming that boots, layers, and general fitness are all suited to what follows.
highlights: Pack ponies loaded for the multi-day approach to Minkiani
Walking beneath old apricot and walnut orchards
Birdlife including bulbuls and buntings along the lower trail
A detailed gear and fitness briefing in Kareri ahead of the pass attempt
tips: Use this easier first day to double-check gear rather than resting entirely
Ask your guide for a realistic assessment of your readiness for the pass stage
Buy any last snacks or supplies in Kareri, as options thin out considerably above

itinerary.title: Kareri to Kareri Lake, a Waypoint North
itinerary.trailType: glacially striated rock slabs, birch and juniper belt, alpine terraces
itinerary.description: Today's walk follows the same broad drainage used by generations of shepherds heading to high summer pastures, climbing steadily away from Kareri's terraced fields into a valley that narrows and steepens by the hour. Early on, the trail crosses a wide, sun-warmed slab of exposed granite scoured smooth by an ancient glacier, a good spot to notice the deep scratch marks, or striations, left in the rock thousands of years ago. Higher up, deodar forest gives way to birch, and the birch itself thins into scattered, wind-flagged junipers as the valley opens into a series of broad grassy terraces used seasonally by grazing herds. A cold, clear side-stream, distinct from the main Kareri Nallah, cuts across the trail roughly two hours below the lake, and its water, untouched by the greyish glacial silt further up, is treated as the best refill point of the day. As height is gained, the Dhauladhar's ridgeline swings further into view, and for those attempting the Minkiani crossing, this is the first genuine glimpse of the saddle and rock towers that mark the pass, still a full day's walk beyond the lake. Kareri Lake itself, reached by early afternoon, serves here as a waypoint rather than the trek's final destination, and camp is deliberately pitched a little further from the main shoreline than usual, closer to the trail continuing north toward Minkiani, to save time on tomorrow's harder push. The afternoon is used for a shorter acclimatisation walk along the ridge above camp, a standard practice before any pass attempt, and the guide points out the rough line the route will follow the next day. Clouds typically build over the high peaks by late afternoon, and the group settles in early, aware that the coming two days will be markedly more demanding than anything covered so far.
highlights: Glacially striated granite slabs along the trail
A clear side-stream distinct from the silty main nallah
First sighting of the Minkiani saddle from the approach
An acclimatisation walk above Kareri Lake before the harder days ahead
tips: Refill water at the clear side-stream rather than the silty glacial flow near the lake
Join the optional acclimatisation walk above camp; it genuinely helps with the next two days
Camp is pitched away from the main lakeside crowd, closer to the Minkiani trail junction

itinerary.title: Kareri Lake to Minkiani Base Camp
itinerary.trailType: lateral moraine ridge, alpine scrub, boulder-hop stream crossings
itinerary.description: Beyond Kareri Lake, the character of the trek changes decisively, the trail leaving the lake's grassy bowl to climb a steep lateral moraine that marks the edge of an old glacial advance, now stabilised by generations of lichen and scattered cushion plants. The route into the upper valley follows what locals call the Minkiani nala, a smaller, steeper drainage than the main Kareri valley, and its stream must be boulder-hopped at several points where snowmelt has swollen the flow beyond a simple stride-across crossing. Vegetation thins with every switchback, oak and birch giving way entirely to low juniper mats and eventually bare scree, and the air itself feels noticeably thinner, a fact most trekkers register first in slightly heavier breathing rather than any dramatic symptom. Bharal, the Himalayan blue sheep, are occasionally spotted picking their way across distant scree slopes, their grey-brown coats blending so well with the rock that a guide's pointed finger is often needed before anyone else can see them. As the trail crests a minor shoulder, Minkiani's approach ridge comes into full view for the first time, a broken, castellated skyline quite different in character from the smoother saddle of Indrahar seen from other trails in this range. Base camp is set on a flat, stony bench beside a small tarn that often carries a thin skin of ice by early morning even in summer, and the setting is stark and elemental, with almost no plant life beyond a few resilient alpine grasses clinging to sheltered pockets. The guide uses the afternoon to walk the group through tomorrow's approach in detail, since Minkiani's terrain closer to the pass is considerably rockier and more exposed than anything covered so far, and an early, deliberate dinner sets up an early start the following morning.
highlights: Crossing an old glacial moraine above Kareri Lake
Boulder-hopping the swift Minkiani nala
Possible bharal sightings on distant scree slopes
A stark base camp beside a semi-frozen tarn
tips: Expect boulder-hop crossings; poles help considerably with balance here
Scan distant scree slopes patiently for bharal, as their camouflage is excellent
Layer up for a cold night, since Base Camp sits beside a tarn that ices over even in summer

itinerary.title: Approaching the Minkiani Saddle and Back to Base Camp
itinerary.trailType: steep scree, rock-band scramble, high alpine saddle
itinerary.description: The approach to Minkiani begins in cold, still air well before sunrise, the beam of headlamps picking a route across the frozen edge of the tarn before the trail commits to a long, steady grind up loose scree that tests both lungs and patience in equal measure. A band of broken rock partway up requires a short scramble, hands as well as feet coming into play on holds that are solid but demand full attention given the exposure below, and the guide leads this section carefully, keeping the group close together. Above the rock band, the gradient briefly eases across a stretch of frost-shattered stone before the final approach to Minkiani's high saddle, where the wind, funnelled through the narrow gap between two rock horns, hits with a force that makes standing upright genuinely difficult on a typical morning. From the saddle, the view opens spectacularly toward the wilder, less-trekked terrain beyond, including distant glimpses toward the high basins that eventually lead to Bara Bhangal, a village famous for its isolation on the far side of the range. Depending on the day's snow and ice conditions, the crossing itself may or may not be pushed further, and on many attempts the group treats the saddle as the trek's high point and turnaround, a decision guides make conservatively given how remote and technical the terrain beyond genuinely becomes. The descent back to Base Camp retraces the rock band and scree with considerably more care than speed, since fatigue and thinner air make missteps easier at this stage of the day. Arriving back at the tarn-side camp by mid-afternoon, there is a strong sense of having reached something genuinely remote and rarely visited, distinct in character from the more heavily trekked passes elsewhere in the Dhauladhar, and the evening is spent recovering with hot food and an early retreat to sleeping bags.
highlights: Pre-dawn scramble across a frost-shattered rock band
Reaching Minkiani's wind-scoured high saddle
Distant views toward the remote Bara Bhangal basins
A guide-led, conservative call on how far to push the crossing
tips: Start in the dark to catch firmer scree conditions before the sun softens the surface
Keep three points of contact during the rock-band scramble
Trust your guide's judgement on whether conditions allow going beyond the saddle

itinerary.title: Minkiani Base Camp Down to Ghera, Journey's End
itinerary.trailType: long moraine and forest descent, riverside track, village road
itinerary.description: The last day is long by design, compressing three days of ascent into a single continuous descent back to the roadhead at Ghera, and the group sets off from the tarn-side camp while the morning light is still flat and grey. The moraine and scree of the upper valley pass quickly underfoot, and by the time the trail rejoins the main Kareri valley near the lake, the change in vegetation, from bare rock back to birch and then deodar, feels almost like watching a season shift in fast motion. A brief stop at Kareri Lake allows for a last look at its still water, quieter now than during the earlier ascent since most other groups on the trail are heading up rather than down, and the guide uses the pause to point out the distant saddle of Minkiani one final time, already looking impossibly far and small from this lower vantage point. Below the lake, the trail's familiar landmarks reappear in reverse: the clear side-stream, the glacially striated granite slab, and eventually the terraced orchards ringing Kareri village, where a short tea stop offers a welcome break roughly two-thirds of the way through the day. The final stretch from Kareri down to Ghera follows the Kareri Nallah's lower course, the trail wide and well-graded but long enough that tired legs are tested regardless of the easier gradient, and the sound of the valley's ordinary traffic, distant vehicles and the occasional loudspeaker from a roadside shop, grows steadily louder as the roadhead approaches. Reaching Ghera by late afternoon, the group is met by transport back into Dharamshala, and the mood is typically one of quiet satisfaction rather than exuberance, the particular reward of having pushed into genuinely remote, seldom-visited terrain that few standard Dhauladhar itineraries ever reach.
highlights: A single long descent compressing three days of ascent
A final look back at Kareri Lake and the distant Minkiani saddle
Familiar landmarks passed in reverse on the way down
Return to Ghera after a rarely-visited high route
tips: Start at first light, since this is the trek's longest single walking day
Use the Kareri village tea stop to refuel properly for the final stretch
Confirm your pickup vehicle timing at Ghera before setting off in the morning

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated challenging. The Minkiani Pass Trek demands challenging effort across 5 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 13900 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Minkiani Pass Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 5D / 4N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 40 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 13,900 ft (4,237 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Minkiani Pass Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Kareri Village. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Minkiani Pass Trek trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Designated Minkiani Pass camping zones Backup lower camp in bad weather

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Minkiani Pass Trek demands challenging effort across 5 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 13900 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to Dharamshala. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Kareri Village.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Minkiani Pass Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Minkiani Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Dharamshala. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Minkiani Pass Trek crowded?
faqs.answer: Minkiani Pass sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Minkiani Pass with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

title: Seven Lakes Trek
summary: Explore the Saat Dal (seven lakes) circuit in the high Dhauladhar — multi-lake camping, cold nights, and few commercial groups. From ₹16,999.
overview:
Saat Dal Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Explore the Saat Dal (seven lakes) circuit in the high Dhauladhar — multi-lake camping, cold nights, and few commercial groups.

Saat Dal’s multi-lake circuit rewards those who like repetition with variation — each tarn has its own shoreline mood and camping constraint.

The itinerary usually starts at Bharmour region roadhead and ends at Saat Dal lake cluster, spanning about 55 km across 7 day(s) with a high point near 13,200 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Saat Dal corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Saat Dal Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Saat Dal main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Saat Dal camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Saat Dal: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Chamba hinterland a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Saat Dal Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Nag Dal Trek, Kali Kund Trek, Lahesh Cave Trek, Triund Trek.

Child policy for Saat Dal Trek: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Saat Dal Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.
