itinerary.title: McLeod Ganj to Triund, Start of the Long Traverse
itinerary.trailType: hillside meadow path, rhododendron switchbacks, open ridge
itinerary.description: The seven-lakes traverse is among the longest and most physically demanding routes in this part of the Dhauladhar, and it opens with a comparatively gentle climb from McLeod Ganj through Dharamkot, a hillside hamlet now as well known for its paragliding launch pad and rooftop cafes as for its position on the old trekking route to Triund. Beyond Dharamkot's last guesthouses, the trail narrows into forest, climbing through mixed oak and oak-scrub for the first hour before the terrain opens onto broader hillside meadows dotted with grazing cattle belonging to families in the villages below. Paragliders occasionally drift overhead in the mid-morning thermals, an incongruous but colourful sight against the backdrop of the Dhauladhar's grey rock. As the trail gains height, rhododendron becomes the dominant tree, and in the right window of spring their red blooms carpet entire hillsides, a striking contrast to the otherwise muted palette of bark and stone. Several small tea stalls operate along this stretch through the main trekking season, their owners often carrying loads of supplies up from Dharamkot each morning, and stopping at one or two is as much about supporting the local economy as it is about resting tired legs. The final approach to Triund climbs through a band of stunted, weather-bent trees before opening onto the broad grassy ridge, and for a group beginning a seven-day traverse toward the high lake basins, this first sight of the Dhauladhar's full wall carries particular weight, since the route ahead will eventually pass behind several of the visible ridgelines rather than simply admiring them from below. Camp is set toward the quieter end of the ridge, and the evening briefing outlines the coming week in broad strokes, from the technical short stage to Lahesh Cave through the long, remote traverse of the lake basins and the eventual descent toward Kareri.
highlights: Paragliders launching from Dharamkot's hillside meadows
Rhododendron forest in bloom on the climb to Triund
Tea stalls run by families carrying up daily supplies
A first sight of the full Dhauladhar wall that the week's traverse will cross behind
tips: Pack light for this first stage, but ensure your full kit is ready, since resupply options end after Triund
Time your walk to enjoy the rhododendron bloom if trekking in spring
Study the ridge from Triund carefully during the evening briefing; it helps to visualise the week ahead

itinerary.title: Triund to Lahesh Cave, First Technical Stage
itinerary.trailType: long scree slope, cairned route-finding, rock overhang shelter
itinerary.description: Where a simple pass trek might treat the climb from Triund to Lahesh Cave as the final push before a summit day, on the seven-lakes traverse it is only the first of several demanding stages, and the distinction shapes how the day is paced. The trail leaves Triund's grass behind almost immediately, tackling a long, unbroken slope of scree and tussock grass that rewards a slow, metronomic pace far more than any burst of speed. Small cairns, built up over years by passing trekking groups, mark the vaguer sections of trail where the ground offers no obvious line, and following them carefully saves considerable time over guessing a route through the looser rock. Partway up, a distinctive slab known locally for its resemblance to a sleeping animal serves as an informal rest point, its flat top just wide enough for the group to sit and refill water from a thin trickle emerging from beneath it. Himalayan griffons and lammergeiers are regularly seen riding thermals along this exposed face, circling with an ease that makes the group's laboured breathing at this altitude feel all the more obvious by comparison. Lahesh Cave's rock overhang, when it finally appears, offers the same blackened shelter it has provided travellers for generations, and camp is arranged carefully in its lee, since tomorrow's stage does not return here but instead branches away from the standard Indrahar approach onto the far less trodden traverse line toward the first of the seven lakes. The guide spends extra time this evening on route-finding notes for the coming days, since the path beyond here thins into intermittent shepherd tracks rather than a clearly defined trail, and confirms that everyone's layering system is adequate for the colder, more exposed camps that lie ahead in the lake basins.
highlights: A long scree slope paced deliberately rather than rushed
A distinctive rest-stop slab with a hidden water trickle
Griffons and lammergeiers riding thermals along the ridge
Route-finding briefing for the seldom-trodden traverse beyond Lahesh Cave
tips: Follow the older cairns carefully; the trail beyond Triund is not always obvious
Refill water at the slab's trickle, as it's more reliable than higher sources
Ensure your layering system is dialled in tonight, as camps only get colder from here

itinerary.title: Off-Trail to Nag Dal, the First of Seven Lakes
itinerary.trailType: faint shepherd traverse, sloping rock ridge, descent into a glacial cirque
itinerary.description: Today marks the point where the seven-lakes route properly diverges from the well-known trail to Indrahar Pass, branching instead onto a traverse line that contours below the main ridge rather than climbing directly to the saddle. The path here is faint in places, more a suggestion worn by shepherds moving flocks between seasonal grazing grounds than a maintained trekking trail, and the guide's local knowledge becomes essential for staying on line across several stretches of loose, sloping rock. Himalayan thar are occasionally spotted on the steeper faces above the traverse, their sure-footed movement across near-vertical ground a useful reminder of how differently local wildlife reads terrain that feels precarious underfoot to a trekker. After several hours of steady sideways progress, the trail crests a minor spur and the group gets its first sight of the day's destination: a hidden glacial cirque holding Nag Dal, the first and lowest of the seven lakes, its water a deep, cold green fringed by a narrow band of moss and alpine grass. The descent into the cirque is steep but short, dropping through a jumble of large boulders deposited by an old glacial retreat, and the basin's enclosed, sheltered character comes as a relief after a day spent almost entirely exposed on open ridge. Camp is set on a grassy patch above the lake's northern edge, chosen to catch the last of the afternoon sun before the surrounding walls plunge the basin into early shadow. This is genuinely quiet, seldom-visited terrain, and the absence of any other trekking group, a near-certainty at Triund or Lahesh Cave, is one of the traverse's most distinctive rewards. The evening briefing looks ahead to the following days spent moving between the remaining six lakes, each reportedly a little higher and starker than the last.
highlights: Leaving the main Indrahar trail for a faint shepherd traverse
Himalayan thar spotted on steep rock faces
First sight of Nag Dal, the lowest of the seven lakes
A sheltered, seldom-visited cirque camp
tips: Stay close to your guide on the faint traverse sections; the line is easy to lose
Watch the steep faces above for thar, especially in early morning light
Enjoy the basin's quiet; genuinely few other groups reach this far off the main trail

itinerary.title: Through Do Dal, Teen Dal, and Char Dal to Panch Dal
itinerary.trailType: rock-hop lake shorelines, rubble saddles between basins, alpine scree
itinerary.description: The route today strings together four more of the basin's lakes in succession, each separated from the next by a short rocky saddle that keeps the walking varied and, in its own way, genuinely exciting, since every crest reveals a new body of water rather than more of the same terrain. Do Dal, the second lake, sits barely twenty minutes above Nag Dal and is noticeably colder and clearer, its bed of pale scree visible several metres down through water that barely stirs even in a stiff breeze. A short climb over a rubble saddle leads to Teen Dal, smaller but deeper, its surface streaked with faint blue-green bands where meltwater from a shrinking hanging glacier feeds in from a gully above. Char Dal, the fourth, is the most dramatic of the morning's lakes, hemmed in on three sides by near-vertical rock and fed by a thin waterfall that appears to hang almost motionless from this distance. Snow finches and accentors work the lake edges for insects throughout the walk, seemingly unbothered by the group's presence, and the general absence of any human trace beyond the faint trail itself reinforces just how remote this basin remains even relative to other high-altitude spots in the Dhauladhar. By early afternoon, the trail reaches Panch Dal, the fifth lake, where camp is pitched on a broad, gently sloping bench above the shoreline, chosen for its slightly better shelter from the wind that channels through the narrower basins closer to the ridge. The evening light here, when clear, turns the surrounding rock faces a deep amber before fading to grey, and the stillness of the lake's surface at dusk, undisturbed by any wind, makes for a memorable, almost meditative close to a day spent entirely among water and stone.
highlights: Visiting four lakes in succession across a single day
Char Dal's near-vertical rock walls and thin hanging waterfall
Snow finches and accentors working the lake margins
A calm, amber-lit dusk at the Panch Dal camp
tips: Pace the day around the lakes rather than the distance; there's plenty of time to linger at each
Keep cameras ready, as light changes quickly between the enclosed basins
Expect a colder camp at Panch Dal than the previous night at Nag Dal

itinerary.title: Chhah Dal and Saat Dal, the Highest Lake
itinerary.trailType: high scree traverse, shadowed lake basin, exposed ridge terrace
itinerary.description: The final two lakes of the traverse sit noticeably higher and starker than the five already visited, and today's walk reflects that shift, climbing out of Panch Dal's relatively gentle basin onto a longer stretch of high, exposed scree. Chhah Dal, the sixth lake, is reached after a stiff hour's climb and is smaller than most of its neighbours, tucked into a narrow gash of rock that keeps it in shadow for much of the day, its water taking on an almost black cast as a result. From Chhah Dal, the trail climbs a final, demanding pitch of loose rock to reach Saat Dal, the seventh and highest of the lakes, set in a wide, austere bowl just below the main Dhauladhar crestline. Ice often persists at Saat Dal's margins even in high summer, and the lake's exposed position means wind is a near-constant presence, testing balance on the looser sections of shoreline scree. From a shoulder just above Saat Dal, the ridge opens views toward more distant features of the Dhauladhar's high country, including glimpses in clear weather toward the general direction of the Toral and Lam Dal areas further along the range, underscoring just how extensive this stretch of mountains really is beyond what any single trek covers. Camp for the night is set on a sheltered rock terrace slightly below the exposed ridge crest, chosen deliberately to reduce wind exposure overnight, and it is, by a wide margin, the highest and coldest camp of the entire traverse. The guide runs a careful check on everyone's condition given the sustained altitude of the past three days, and dinner is kept simple and warming, with an early retreat to sleeping bags standard practice at a camp this exposed and this far from any possibility of quick descent.
highlights: Reaching Chhah Dal, the shadowed sixth lake
Saat Dal, the highest of the seven lakes, ringed by persistent ice
Distant views toward the Toral and Lam Dal high country
The traverse's highest and most exposed camp
tips: Move steadily rather than fast on the final scree pitch to Saat Dal; the altitude is felt sharply here
Choose your tent spot on the sheltered terrace, not the exposed ridge crest
Report any altitude symptoms immediately; this is the trek's most remote point

itinerary.title: Descending the Basin to Kareri Lake
itinerary.trailType: long descending traverse, extensive moraine, grassy shelf approach
itinerary.description: Leaving the high ridge camp near Saat Dal, the day begins with a descent back down past Chhah Dal and Panch Dal, the same lakes seen in the opposite direction now taking on a different character in the flatter, earlier light. Rather than retracing the full outward route through Nag Dal, the trail branches onto a long descending traverse that curls around the shoulder of the basin and drops, gradually at first and then more steeply, toward the drainage that feeds Kareri Lake, a different valley system entirely from the one used on the approach through Lahesh Cave. Extensive moraine fields mark much of the upper descent, the loose rock demanding careful, unhurried footing even as legs grow tired from six days of sustained effort. As altitude is lost, grass reappears in patches among the rock, and by the time the trail reaches the grassy shelf above Kareri Lake, the transformation from the stark high basins is almost startling: the lake sits calm and green below, ringed by the kind of alpine turf that felt entirely absent just a few hours earlier. Reaching Kareri Lake's shore in the late afternoon effectively closes the loop of the week's traverse, connecting the remote, seldom-visited lake cluster explored over the previous four days to a far more familiar and accessible point in the wider Dhauladhar trekking network. Camp is pitched near the lake's northern edge, and the mood among the group is markedly different from earlier nights, a mixture of physical exhaustion and genuine satisfaction at having completed one of the range's more demanding multi-day routes. Dinner conversation inevitably turns to comparing the seven lakes, debating which was most striking, and the guide uses the relative comfort of this lower, gentler camp to begin winding the trip's intensity back down ahead of the final descent tomorrow.
highlights: A long descending traverse connecting the lake basins to the Kareri valley
Extensive moraine fields tested by six days of accumulated fatigue
The startling return of green turf around Kareri Lake
Closing the loop of the week's traverse at a familiar landmark
tips: Take moraine sections slowly; tired legs are more prone to ankle rolls here
Enjoy the relative comfort of the Kareri Lake camp after four remote nights
Use the evening to compare notes on all seven lakes while memories are fresh

itinerary.title: Kareri Lake to Ghera, Closing the Loop
itinerary.trailType: forest descent beside the Kareri Nallah, village lanes, roadhead
itinerary.description: The closing day retraces the familiar descent from Kareri Lake down through Kareri village to the roadhead at Ghera, a route that, after six days spent largely off any defined trail, feels almost luxuriously straightforward. The path drops through birch and then deodar forest beside the Kareri Nallah, its glacial-fed water now a known, comfortable presence after a week spent among far starker, higher terrain, and the group makes good time on ground that no longer demands the careful route-finding required throughout the lake traverse. Kareri village appears roughly two-thirds of the way through the walk, and the same homestay families who hosted the group at the very start of the trip often recognise returning trekkers with a warm welcome and offers of tea, a small but genuinely touching bookend to the week. Below Kareri, the terraced fields and orchard trees passed on day one reappear in their familiar order, and the sound of the valley's ordinary life, distant vehicle horns, a radio playing somewhere, grows steadily louder as the roadhead approaches. Physically, this is a demanding final push after six accumulated days of effort, and most trekkers feel it distinctly in tired legs and feet by the last hour, even on comparatively gentle ground. Arriving at Ghera in the later afternoon, transport waits to carry the group back into Dharamshala, and the mood is typically reflective rather than celebratory in any loud sense, the particular quiet satisfaction that comes from having spent a full week largely out of contact with the ordinary world, moving between seven lakes that very few trekkers in the region ever get to see in a single continuous journey.
highlights: A comfortable final descent after six days off the beaten trail
A warm return welcome from Kareri's homestay families
Familiar orchards and terraces marking the trek's close
Reflecting on a rare week spent among all seven high lakes
tips: Take it easy on this last stretch; six days of accumulated fatigue catches up on tired feet
Consider a short stop in Kareri to thank your homestay hosts if time allows
Arrange your onward transport from Ghera a day in advance given the trek's long duration

faqs.question: Is Saat Dal Trek safe for solo travellers?

title: Lam Dal Lake Trek
summary: Pilgrim-meets-alpine trek to sacred Lam Dal in the Dhauladhar–Manimahesh hinterland — high meadows, glacial lakes, and remote camps. From ₹15,999.
overview:
Lam Dal Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Pilgrim-meets-alpine trek to sacred Lam Dal in the Dhauladhar–Manimahesh hinterland — high meadows, glacial lakes, and remote camps.

Lam Dal blends pilgrimage energy with glacial-lake silence. High pastures, cold water, and remote camps define the approach from the Bharmour hinterland.

The itinerary usually starts at Bharmour / Hadsar region and ends at Lam Dal alpine lake, spanning about 48 km across 6 day(s) with a high point near 12,800 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Lam Dal corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Lam Dal Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Lam Dal main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Lam Dal camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Lam Dal: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Chamba / Bharmour side a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Lam Dal Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Saat Dal Trek, Nag Dal Trek, Kali Kund Trek, Snowline Laka Trek.

Child policy for Lam Dal Trek: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Lam Dal Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

itinerary.title: Bir to Rajgundha Through the Chhota Bhangal Forest
itinerary.trailType: old jeep track, oak and rhododendron switchbacks, meadow village approach
itinerary.description: The Lam Dal trek begins in Bir, a town better known internationally for the paragliding site at nearby Billing than for the considerably wilder terrain that opens up just behind it. From the edge of Bir's Tibetan colony, the trail climbs steadily through terraced tea gardens before entering forest, following what is largely an old jeep track used occasionally by supply vehicles serving the villages of Chhota Bhangal further up the valley. Oak dominates the lower forest, giving way gradually to rhododendron and then to blue pine as the gradient steepens through a series of long, looping switchbacks. Streams cross the track at regular intervals, most running clear and fast enough by midday that filtering rather than treating chemically is generally sufficient, and each crossing offers a natural pause to look back over the widening view of the Kangra valley's tea-covered lower slopes. Villages here belong to the Gaddi and Chhota Bhangal shepherd communities, distinct in dialect and dress from those closer to Dharamshala, and small clusters of slate-roofed houses appear at intervals, their fields planted with potato and maize rather than the wheat seen lower down. Rajgundha itself sits on a broad, sloping meadow shelf, one of the last proper villages before the trail commits fully to wilderness, and its guesthouses, simple but comfortable, are run by families who have long supported trekking groups heading toward Lam Dal and the wider Chhota Bhangal high country. The evening offers sweeping views back down toward Bir and the Kangra plains beyond, often lit gold as the sun drops, and the guide uses dinner to outline the considerably wilder stages ahead, since the trail beyond Rajgundha grows quiet and remote very quickly.
highlights: Starting near Bir's famous Billing paragliding site
Old jeep track climbing through oak, rhododendron, and blue pine
Villages of the culturally distinct Chhota Bhangal region
Golden evening views over the Kangra plains from Rajgundha
tips: Fill water bottles at the clear streams along the jeep track rather than waiting for the village
Rajgundha is the last proper resupply point; check your kit thoroughly here
Ask locally about current conditions at Lam Dal before continuing

itinerary.title: Rajgundha to Palachak Thatch
itinerary.trailType: dense fir and spruce forest, grazing meadow, shepherd thatch clearing
itinerary.description: Beyond Rajgundha, the trail loses its jeep-track character almost immediately, narrowing into a genuine forest path that climbs through increasingly dense fir and spruce, the canopy overhead thick enough that direct sunlight becomes a rare, welcome interruption rather than a constant. The forest floor is carpeted with a deep layer of needle litter, and fungi in a startling range of colours appear along the path's damper stretches, particularly after recent rain. Himalayan monal are occasionally heard, and less often seen, calling from deeper in the forest, their sharp, distinctive whistle a highlight for anyone attentive enough to catch it over the sound of boots on the trail. As the forest begins to thin near the day's end, the trail opens onto a scatter of grazing meadows dotted with thatch, the simple stone-and-timber shelters built by Gaddi shepherds who move their flocks up to these pastures for the summer months. Palachak Thatch, the day's camp, is one such clearing, chosen by generations of shepherds for its flat ground and proximity to a reliable spring emerging from beneath a mossy rock face just above the site. The setting feels distinctly wilder than anything encountered on the first day, with the Dhauladhar's higher ridgeline now visible in fragments through gaps in the surrounding forest, hinting at the terrain still to come. Evening temperatures drop sharply once the sun disappears behind the ridge, and the group typically gathers close around the cooking fire, sharing food with any shepherds currently based at the thatch, whose knowledge of the trail conditions ahead to Lam Dal is treated as valuable, up-to-date information rather than mere conversation.
highlights: Dense fir and spruce forest with a thick needle-litter floor
Himalayan monal calls echoing through the trees
Camping at a traditional Gaddi shepherd thatch clearing
A reliable spring emerging from a mossy rock face
tips: Listen for the monal's distinctive whistle in the quieter stretches of forest
Refill water at the spring above camp; it's more reliable than lower streams
Chat with any shepherds at the thatch for the latest word on trail conditions ahead

itinerary.title: Palachak to the Shores of Lam Dal
itinerary.trailType: alpine wildflower meadow, consolidated boulder field, moraine lakeshore
itinerary.description: Leaving Palachak, the trail breaks out of the fir forest within the first hour, and the change in scenery is immediate and dramatic, the group emerging onto a wide alpine meadow carpeted, in season, with anemones, primulas, and clusters of blue Himalayan poppy tucked into sheltered folds of ground. Grazing sounds fade as the meadow gives way to a boulder field, larger and more consolidated than the loose scree of lower stages, and the trail threads a careful line between the biggest rocks rather than attempting to climb over them directly. A shallow, braided stream crosses the boulder field at several points, its water noticeably colder here, drawn directly from snowfields still visible on the slopes above even in the height of summer. As the trail crests a final rise, Lam Dal appears in its full extent, considerably larger than most other high lakes in this part of the Dhauladhar, its long, curving shoreline framed by steep grassy slopes on one side and a wall of bare rock on the other. Locally, the lake carries some religious significance among Chhota Bhangal's shepherd communities, who consider it linked to a resident serpent deity, and a modest cairn near the shore, hung with faded cloth offerings, marks this connection for anyone passing through. Camp is set on a grassy bench a short distance above the water, chosen for both a clear view of the lake and shelter from the wind that tends to funnel along its length in the afternoons. The remaining daylight is typically spent simply absorbing the scale of the place, and the evening, if clear, offers a striking view of the lake's surface turning from blue to slate grey to black as the light fades entirely.
highlights: Alpine meadows in bloom with poppy, primula, and anemone
A consolidated boulder field threading toward the lake basin
First full view of Lam Dal's long, curving shoreline
A shoreline cairn linked to local serpent-deity folklore
tips: Look out for blue Himalayan poppy in sheltered meadow folds if trekking in the right season
Cross the braided boulder-field stream carefully; the cold water numbs feet quickly
Respect the shoreline cairn and any cloth offerings left by local shepherd communities

itinerary.title: A Day Exploring Lam Dal's Basin
itinerary.trailType: lakeside meadow circuit, rocky viewpoint scramble
itinerary.description: With no need to move camp, the day is given over to exploring Lam Dal's surroundings at an easier pace, starting with a loop along the lake's grassy northern shore where the water is shallow enough near the edge to see pale stones on the bottom clearly on a still morning. A short, scrambling climb onto a rocky spur above the eastern shoreline offers the best overall view of the lake's true size, its long body curving out of sight around a rocky headland, a scale genuinely difficult to appreciate from the lower camp. From this vantage point, several smaller, satellite tarns become visible in folds of ground further up the slope, fed by the same snowfields that feed the main lake, and a short optional walk can extend to the nearest of these for anyone with energy to spare. Bar-headed geese are occasionally seen resting on the lake during migration windows, a striking sight at this altitude, and the guide often points out fresh tracks near the shoreline mud left by foraging musk deer, animals rarely seen directly given their shy, forest-edge habits. The middle of the day is typically left unstructured, and many trekkers use it simply to rest, wash a little gear in a side-stream well away from the lake itself, or read in the sun on the meadow above camp. Weather at this altitude can shift quickly, and a short afternoon squall bringing sleet or hail is not unusual even in peak season, though it typically clears within an hour, leaving the lake's surface calm again by early evening. This lighter day serves a practical purpose too, allowing bodies to properly adjust to the altitude before the return journey begins tomorrow.
highlights: A scramble to a rocky spur for the best view of Lam Dal's full length
Sighting satellite tarns fed by the same snowfields
Possible bar-headed geese resting on the lake during migration
A genuine rest day to aid acclimatisation before the descent
tips: Use the rocky spur viewpoint for photographs; the angle captures the lake's scale best
Wash gear only in a side-stream, never directly in the lake
Keep a rain shell handy even on a clear morning, as afternoon squalls appear quickly

itinerary.title: Lam Dal Back to Palachak Thatch
itinerary.trailType: boulder field, fading alpine meadow, fir forest descent
itinerary.description: The descent from Lam Dal begins with a last look across the lake from the camp bench, its surface especially still in the early morning before any wind picks up, and the group retraces the boulder field crossed two days earlier, now moving with the easier confidence that comes from familiar ground rather than a first encounter. The braided stream crossing the boulders runs noticeably lower than on the way up, a small but clear sign of the previous night's colder temperatures having slowed the day's snowmelt, and the crossing itself takes barely any time at all compared to the more cautious approach used on the ascent. Dropping off the boulder field onto the alpine meadow, the wildflowers seen in fuller bloom during the approach have begun to fade slightly, a subtle marker of how quickly the short high-altitude summer moves through its cycle. Fir forest reclaims the trail within the hour, and the transition back into shade and closer, more enclosed terrain feels almost immediate after two days spent in the lake basin's wide-open exposure. Birdsong, largely absent at Lam Dal's altitude, returns gradually as the forest thickens, and a pair of Himalayan woodpeckers is spotted working a dead trunk just off the trail, undisturbed by the passing group. Palachak Thatch, reached again by mid-afternoon, feels considerably more familiar this time, and any shepherds still stationed there often greet the group with genuine curiosity about conditions at the lake, since word of the basin's snow and weather travels slowly between the scattered summer camps. The evening is a quieter, more relaxed affair than the outward journey's equivalent night, the hardest and highest stage of the trek now safely behind the group rather than still ahead.
highlights: A calmer, faster boulder-field crossing on the way down
Fading wildflowers marking the pace of the short alpine summer
Himalayan woodpeckers spotted in the returning forest
A relaxed second night at the familiar Palachak Thatch
tips: Cross the boulder field in the cooler morning hours when the stream runs lower
Share any updates on lake conditions with shepherds you meet; the exchange of information runs both ways
Enjoy a more relaxed pace today, since the trek's hardest stage is now complete

itinerary.title: Palachak to Bir, Trek's End
itinerary.trailType: long forest descent, old jeep track, tea-garden approach into town
itinerary.description: The final day compresses the outward journey's first two stages into one long, continuous descent, and the group leaves Palachak while the forest is still cool and shaded from the low morning sun. The fir and spruce belt passes quickly underfoot heading downhill, and Rajgundha appears roughly midway through the day, offering a natural break point for tea and a chance to thank the guesthouse families who hosted the group at the very start of the trip. Below Rajgundha, the trail rejoins the old jeep track, and the walking becomes noticeably easier on the broader, more consistent gradient, though the accumulated distance of the past five days is felt clearly in tired feet by the later hours. Paragliders drift into view again as the trail nears Bir, a visual bookend to the trek's opening day, their colourful canopies circling above Billing in numbers that build through the afternoon as thermals strengthen with the day's heat. The terraced tea gardens on Bir's outskirts, passed almost without notice on the way up nearly a week ago, now register properly, their neat rows a startling contrast to the wild, high basin the group has just spent days exploring around Lam Dal. Reaching Bir's Tibetan colony by late afternoon effectively closes the loop of the trek, and the ordinary comforts of a proper cafe, hot shower, and solid mobile signal feel disproportionately luxurious after six days spent largely in forest and high meadow. Most groups use the final evening in Bir to unwind properly, comparing memories of the lake, the thatch camps, and the long stretches of quiet forest that defined a route considerably less crowded than many of the Dhauladhar's better-known trails.
highlights: A long final descent compressing two outward stages into one
A thank-you stop with the Rajgundha guesthouse families
Paragliders circling Billing as the trek's closing bookend
Returning to Bir's tea gardens and Tibetan colony comforts
tips: Start early to give yourself a comfortable buffer on this long final day
Stop in Rajgundha to properly thank your hosts from the first night
Book a night in Bir to recover before travelling onward, rather than rushing straight out

itinerary.title: Ghera to Kareri, Gateway to Toral
itinerary.trailType: cantilever footbridge, chir pine and wild rose scrub, deodar forest
itinerary.description: Toral Pass has long served as a working trade link between the Kangra and Chamba sides of the Dhauladhar, and the trek that crosses it begins, fittingly, with a straightforward walk from the roadhead at Ghera that gives little hint of the scale of what lies four days ahead. A cantilever footbridge, its timber deck patched and repatched over the years, carries the trail across the Kareri Nallah almost immediately, and from there the path climbs gradually through a mixed belt of chir pine and wild rose scrub, the roses' pale pink flowers a common sight along sunnier, more open stretches of trail through late spring. Mule caravans are a frequent sight on this lower stretch, often carrying cement, steel rod, or sacks of grain up to Kareri and beyond for ongoing village construction projects, a reminder that this trail remains a working supply route as much as a trekking corridor. As the valley narrows, deodar forest thickens around the path, and a resident pair of lammergeiers is often seen working the thermals above the ridge, their distinctive diamond-shaped tails visible even at considerable height. Kareri village, reached by early afternoon, sits on its usual shelf above the nallah, and for a group about to attempt a genuine cross-range pass into Chamba, the stop here carries a slightly different weight than for those simply visiting the lake, since it marks the last point with any real connection to the outside world before the trail commits fully to the mountains. The evening is spent on a thorough gear check specific to a multi-day pass crossing, confirming rope, warm layers, and food supplies are sufficient for the considerably more remote and technical days that follow.
highlights: Crossing a patched timber cantilever bridge over the Kareri Nallah
Wild rose scrub blooming along the sunnier trail stretches
Mule caravans supplying ongoing construction in the villages ahead
A thorough gear check in Kareri before committing to the Toral crossing
tips: Give way to loaded mule caravans on the narrower stretches of trail
Look up regularly for lammergeiers riding the ridge thermals
Use the Kareri stop for a genuinely thorough gear check; this is your last easy chance

itinerary.title: Kareri to Kareri Lake, Acclimatising for the Crossing
itinerary.trailType: narrowing gorge trail, hidden waterfall, boulder field approach
itinerary.description: For a group aiming to cross Toral into Chamba, today's climb to Kareri Lake is treated primarily as an acclimatisation stage rather than a destination in its own right, though the terrain itself demands full attention regardless of how it's framed. The trail follows the Kareri Nallah upstream through a steadily narrowing gorge, and a modest waterfall roughly two hours above the village, easy to miss behind a screen of overhanging birch, offers one of the better spots on the entire route to refill water bottles from a source untouched by higher glacial silt. Koklass pheasant are occasionally startled from streamside cover here, their explosive, clattering flight a small shock each time it happens, and their calls carry a fair distance across the narrow valley in the still morning air. Fog frequently drifts up from the lower valley through the late morning hours on this stretch, thick enough at times to reduce visibility to a few dozen metres before burning off entirely by early afternoon, and the guide keeps the group close together during these socked-in stretches as a matter of course. Beyond the boulder field that marks the approach to the lake, Kareri Lake itself appears as it always does, still and dark beneath the Dhauladhar's wall, though today's camp is pitched with a specific eye toward tomorrow's onward push rather than the usual leisurely lakeside stay, positioned closer to the trail branch heading toward Toral. The evening briefing focuses heavily on the two demanding days immediately ahead, since the terrain beyond the lake toward Toral grows considerably more remote and technical than anything encountered so far on this particular route.
highlights: A hidden waterfall offering the best clean water source on the route
Koklass pheasant startled from streamside cover
Drifting morning fog along the narrowing gorge
A lakeside camp positioned for tomorrow's push toward Toral
tips: Refill water at the hidden waterfall rather than waiting for the lake
Stay close together during foggy stretches; visibility can drop suddenly
This camp is positioned for tomorrow's Toral approach, not for a relaxed lake stay

itinerary.title: Kareri Lake to Toral Base Camp
itinerary.trailType: distinct nala drainage, abandoned shepherd enclosure, high scree approach
itinerary.description: Leaving Kareri Lake, the trail climbs away from the main basin along a route distinct from both the Indrahar and Minkiani approaches, following instead a drainage locally referred to as the Toral nala that cuts a steeper, more direct line toward the range's crest. The lower stretch crosses a series of grassy shelves grazed lightly by sheep through the summer, and a stone shepherd enclosure, roofless and long abandoned, marks roughly the halfway point of the climb, its weathered walls a small monument to generations of seasonal use. Above the enclosure, vegetation drops away rapidly, and the trail commits to a long, sustained scree slope that tests both stamina and patience, the loose surface shifting underfoot with almost every step despite a generally manageable gradient. Snow patches, often hardened into old, granular ice by repeated freeze-thaw cycles, appear in shaded gullies well before the base camp itself, and crossing a couple of the smaller ones offers useful practice for the more serious snow expected near the pass tomorrow. Toral Base Camp sits on a rocky, exposed shelf with a direct, unobstructed view of the pass itself, a broad notch flanked by two prominent rock towers quite distinct in profile from Indrahar's narrower gap seen elsewhere in the range. The setting is stark and largely lifeless beyond scattered lichen and the occasional hardy cushion plant, and temperatures drop well below freezing overnight even in peak summer. The guide uses the afternoon for a detailed walkthrough of the crossing itself, including rope protocols for the steeper section just below the pass, and an early, deliberate dinner sets up the pre-dawn departure that tomorrow's crossing requires.
highlights: Climbing the distinct Toral nala drainage away from the main Kareri basin
Passing an abandoned stone shepherd enclosure
Practice crossings on hardened snow patches before the main event
A stark base camp with a direct view of Toral's twin rock towers
tips: Use the practice snow crossings to test your footing before the real thing tomorrow
Expect sub-freezing overnight temperatures even in summer at this camp
Attend the full rope-protocol briefing; the section below the pass is genuinely technical

itinerary.title: Crossing Toral Pass Into Chamba
itinerary.trailType: fixed-rope snow wall, boulder-strewn pass gap, long forest descent
itinerary.description: The crossing of Toral Pass is the longest and most demanding single day of the trek, and it begins hours before sunrise, the group climbing by headlamp up the scree slope familiar from the previous evening's reconnaissance glance. A short but genuinely steep band of hardened snow guards the final approach to the pass in most seasons, and the group ascends it clipped into a fixed rope, kicking careful steps into the surface under the guide's close direction. Toral Pass itself, at close to 14,600 feet, is a narrow, boulder-strewn gap between the two towers visible from base camp, and the wind through it is typically fierce enough to make even brief photographs a two-person job, one holding a phone steady while the other blocks the worst of the gusts. The view from the top swings dramatically between two very different worlds: the Kangra side's forested folds and distant plains behind, and ahead, the drier, more rugged Chamba hills rolling away toward the Ravi river system, their slopes noticeably barer of forest even from this distance. The descent begins immediately and does not let up for hours, dropping first through steep scree and then, as altitude falls away, into increasingly dense forest of blue pine and, lower still, deodar, the vegetation change on this Chamba-facing side visibly different in composition from anything seen on the Kangra approach. Griffon vultures circle high overhead for much of the descent, taking advantage of the same updrafts that make the pass itself so windy. Kuarsi Thach, a broad forest clearing used by Gaddi shepherds from the Chamba side, offers a welcome, sheltered camp after a day that has tested every member of the group, and the relief of having the pass safely behind, rather than still ahead, is evident in the noticeably lighter mood at dinner despite the accumulated exhaustion.
highlights: A fixed-rope ascent of the snow wall guarding Toral Pass
Crossing at nearly 14,600 ft between two distinct mountain worlds
Dropping through forest that changes character on the drier Chamba side
Griffon vultures riding updrafts along the long descent
tips: Clip into the fixed rope on the snow wall regardless of how confident you feel
Hold your camera steady with two people at the windy pass-top
Budget a full nine to ten hours for this day and start well before dawn

itinerary.title: Kuarsi Thach Down to Kuarsi Village
itinerary.trailType: shepherd forest track, stone devta shrines, Chamba-style village lanes
itinerary.description: With the technical crossing now well behind the group, today's walk down to Kuarsi village is comparatively relaxed, though the forest itself remains a highlight in its own right, deodar and blue pine giving way gradually to walnut and apple trees as the trail loses height and the Chamba valley's drier, more open character asserts itself. The path follows a shepherd track worn over generations by Gaddi families moving between Kuarsi Thach's summer pastures and their permanent homes lower down, and small stone shrines to local devtas appear at several points along the way, distinct in style from the temples seen on the Kangra side of the pass. Terraced fields of maize and potato announce the approach to Kuarsi itself, a sizeable village built in the distinctive Chamba hill style, with slate roofs weighted by heavy stones and timber balconies carved with simple geometric patterns quite different from the architecture seen in Kareri or elsewhere on the Kangra side. A modest but locally important temple sits near the village centre, and the group is often invited to look inside, an opportunity the Kangra side's more restricted customs elsewhere in the region rarely extend. Homestay families here are well used to hosting trekkers completing the Toral crossing from the Kangra side, and the evening meal typically features Chamba specialities distinct from the food served on the trek's earlier days, including a thicker style of maize bread cooked over an open hearth. Sitting in a proper house again after two nights of high camping feels disproportionately comfortable, and the evening is spent swapping stories about the crossing with curious village residents, several of whom have made the reverse journey themselves at some point in their lives.
highlights: Forest transitioning to walnut and apple orchards on the descent
Distinctive Chamba-style stone shrines along the shepherd track
Kuarsi's heavy slate roofs and carved timber balconies
A home-cooked Chamba meal after two nights of high camping
tips: Try the local maize bread if offered; it's distinct from anything served earlier on the trek
Ask politely before photographing the village temple's interior
Use this easier day to properly dry out any gear still damp from the pass crossing

itinerary.title: Kuarsi to Bharmour, Journey's End
itinerary.trailType: village road, widening terraced farmland, motorable approach into town
itinerary.description: The final day out of Kuarsi follows a gentler, mostly descending village road toward Bharmour, the historic former capital of the Chamba hill kingdoms and the trek's official end point. The path passes through a succession of small hamlets and terraced farmland, the Chamba valley widening steadily with every passing mile, and glimpses of the Ravi river appear intermittently through gaps in the terracing well before the sound of it becomes a constant presence. Local farmers, busy with seasonal work in the fields, are a common sight along this stretch, and their curiosity about a group arriving on foot from over Toral Pass, rather than by the usual road route, often leads to friendly, if brief, roadside conversations. As the trail nears Bharmour, the ancient Chaurasi temple complex, a cluster of stone shikhara-style shrines dating back centuries and dedicated to a group of eighty-four sages, comes into view, and most groups make time for at least a short visit before the trek formally concludes. Bharmour's setting, ringed by peaks that include the sacred Manimahesh massif visible on clear days, makes for a fitting final panorama after a week spent almost entirely among mountains considerably less visited than the well-known pilgrimage routes further along the Chamba valley. Reaching the town by early afternoon, the group typically has time for a proper meal and a chance to reflect properly on the trek's arc, from the working trade trail out of Ghera to the technical crossing of Toral and the very different cultural world encountered on the descent into Chamba. Onward transport toward Chamba town or back over the Dulchi Pass road to Kangra is arranged from here, closing out a route that few standard itineraries in the region attempt in full.
highlights: Walking out through Chamba's widening terraced farmland
A stop at the ancient Chaurasi temple complex in Bharmour
Distant views of the sacred Manimahesh massif
Completing a rare full crossing from Kangra to Chamba over Toral Pass
tips: Set aside time for the Chaurasi temple complex before onward travel
Arrange your transport out of Bharmour a day in advance, especially in peak season
Carry your permit or ID, as this route crosses from Kangra into Chamba district

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated challenging. The Toral Pass Trek demands challenging effort across 6 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 14500 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Toral Pass Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 6D / 5N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 52 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 14,500 ft (4,419 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Toral Pass Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Chamba / Kangra remote approaches. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Toral Pass Trek trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Designated Toral Pass camping zones Backup lower camp in bad weather

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Toral Pass Trek demands challenging effort across 6 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 14500 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to Dhauladhar. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Chamba / Kangra remote approaches.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Toral Pass Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Toral Pass Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Dhauladhar. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Toral Pass Trek crowded?
faqs.answer: Toral Pass sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Toral Pass with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

title: Bagala Nag Trek
summary: Quiet temple trail in the Dharamshala foothills — village paths, seasonal streams, and few tourist crowds. From ₹799.
overview:
Bagalu Nag Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Quiet temple trail in the Dharamshala foothills — village paths, seasonal streams, and few tourist crowds.

Bagalu Nag keeps things local: quiet temple approaches, seasonal streams, and almost no commercial trek traffic.

The itinerary usually starts at Local foothill trailhead and ends at Bagalu Nag temple area, spanning about 6 km across 1 day(s) with a high point near 5,800 ft. Graded easy, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Bagalu Nag corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Bagalu Nag Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Bagalu Nag main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Not applicable — day trek.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: Charge devices in town before starting.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Bagalu Nag: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dharamshala foothills a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Bagalu Nag Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Kareri to Triund Traverse, Toral Pass Trek, Waru Pass Trek, Triund Trek.

Child policy for Bagalu Nag Trek: 8+ with guardians. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Bagalu Nag Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

itinerary.title: Bhagsu to the Forest Shrine of Bagalu Nag
itinerary.trailType: forest path, stream-side climb, temple clearing
itinerary.description: Tucked into a fold of forest above Bhagsu village, the shrine of Bagalu Nag is one of the lesser-known devotional hikes in the Dharamshala area, and the walk to it makes for a satisfying half-day outing away from the busier Triund trail. The path begins at the upper edge of Bhagsu, slipping behind the last row of guesthouses onto a narrow track that follows a feeder stream of the Bhagsu khad upstream through a corridor of horse-chestnut and walnut trees. Local families occasionally use this same path en route to graze goats on the small clearings scattered through the forest, and their presence, along with a scattering of prayer flags tied to branches at odd intervals, is usually the only sign of other people on what is otherwise a quiet, shaded walk. The stream crossings, three in total, are simple stone-hop affairs in normal conditions, though the flow can swell noticeably after heavy rain, and caution is warranted on the slicker rocks near each crossing. As the gradient steepens toward the temple, the forest opens briefly onto a rocky bluff with a clear view back down over Bhagsu's rooftops and, beyond them, the wider sweep of the Kangra valley. Bagalu Nag itself is a modest stone platform shrine dedicated to a local serpent deity believed by villagers to guard the water sources of the valley, and it sits in a small natural clearing ringed by old deodar trees, quiet and rarely crowded even on weekends. Visitors commonly leave small offerings of milk or flowers at the shrine, following local custom, and the return walk retraces the same forest path, typically completed well before the afternoon cloud that often builds over the ridge by mid-afternoon in the warmer months.
highlights: A quieter alternative to the busier Triund trail
Stone-hop crossings of a Bhagsu khad feeder stream
A rocky bluff view over Bhagsu and the Kangra valley
The forest-clearing shrine of Bagalu Nag, dedicated to a local serpent deity
tips: Start by late morning to be back well before the typical afternoon cloud build-up
Watch your footing on the stream crossings, especially after rain
Carry a small offering of flowers or milk if you'd like to follow local custom at the shrine

title: Galu Devi Trek
summary: Popular short climb from Dharamkot to Galu Devi — prayer flags, café culture, and the classic start toward Triund. From ₹699.
overview:
Galu Devi Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Popular short climb from Dharamkot to Galu Devi — prayer flags, café culture, and the classic start toward Triund.

Galu Devi is Dharamkot’s classic prayer-flag climb and the cultural doorstep of the Triund trail system.

The itinerary usually starts at Dharamkot and ends at Galu Devi Temple, spanning about 4 km across 1 day(s) with a high point near 7,000 ft. Graded easy, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Galu Devi corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Galu Devi Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Safety on Galu Devi: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dharamkot a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Galu Devi Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

itinerary.title: Dharamkot to the Galu Devi Temple
itinerary.trailType: forest ridge trail, stone temple steps
itinerary.description: Galu Devi is often walked past quickly by trekkers focused on reaching Triund, but taken as a destination in its own right, the short climb from Dharamkot offers a relaxed introduction to the lower Dhauladhar's forest and folklore. The trail leaves Dharamkot's cluster of cafes and guesthouses along a well-trodden path shared with day-trippers and pilgrims alike, climbing gently through oak forest interspersed with the occasional stand of rhododendron, its blooms a highlight for anyone walking through in the early summer window. A handful of small shops selling tea, biscuits, and basic trekking supplies line the lower stretch, catering to the steady flow of visitors this trail sees compared to quieter routes elsewhere in the range. As the path gains height, gaps in the forest canopy begin offering framed views down over Dharamkot's rooftops and, further beyond, the wider Kangra valley fading into haze toward the plains. Galu Devi's small whitewashed temple sits on a modest shelf just below the point where this trail joins the main route to Triund, its simple architecture in keeping with the many roadside and forest shrines scattered across this part of Himachal. Local guides typically share the temple's association as guardian of travellers heading further up into the mountains, a role reflected in the custom of stopping briefly for a blessing before continuing on to higher, more demanding trails. Even for those not continuing further, the temple clearing makes a pleasant spot to rest, with space to sit in the shade and take in the forest sounds, before the group retraces the same path back down to Dharamkot, typically finishing in time for a late lunch back among the village's rooftop cafes.
highlights: A relaxed forest walk shared with local pilgrims
Rhododendron blooms along the lower trail in early summer
Framed valley views through gaps in the oak canopy
The whitewashed Galu Devi temple, traditionally a blessing stop for onward trekkers
tips: Combine this easy walk with a relaxed lunch back in Dharamkot's cafes afterward
Visit in the early morning for the quietest experience on this popular trail
Carry small change if you'd like to make an offering at the temple

itinerary.title: The Cave Shrine Behind Aghanjar Mahadev's Waterfall
itinerary.trailType: forest path, stream-side scramble, cave shrine approach
itinerary.description: Aghanjar Mahadev is one of the more atmospheric short hikes near Dharamshala, built around a natural cave shrine tucked directly behind a modest waterfall rather than a conventional temple structure. The walk typically begins near Naddi, following a forest track that climbs steadily through deodar and oak before dropping slightly to meet the stream that eventually forms the waterfall at the trek's destination. The trail alongside the stream grows progressively rockier, and several short scrambles over wet, moss-covered boulders demand careful footing, particularly in the cooler months when a thin film of ice can form on shaded rock surfaces overnight. Ferns and moss thrive in the damp microclimate created by the stream's constant spray, giving this stretch of forest a noticeably different, greener character than the drier ridge trails found elsewhere around Dharamshala. As the waterfall comes into earshot before it comes into view, the trail narrows further, threading between large boulders before opening onto a small rock amphitheatre where the water drops several metres into a shallow pool. Behind the fall, accessible by a short, damp scramble, a natural cave houses a simple Shiva lingam, said locally to have formed naturally rather than been carved, and devotees regularly make the walk specifically for this feature, believing the site carries particular spiritual significance tied to its unusual, water-shrouded setting. The spray inside the cave keeps the air notably cool even on warm days, and the sound of the falling water fills the small chamber with a constant, resonant background noise. After time spent at the shrine and pool, the group retraces the stream-side path back to the trailhead, the descent generally quicker but requiring the same careful attention on the wet boulder sections passed on the way up.
highlights: A natural cave shrine set directly behind a forest waterfall
Moss and fern-lined stream scrambles through a distinctly green microclimate
A believed naturally formed Shiva lingam inside the cave
The constant resonant sound of falling water inside the shrine chamber
tips: Wear shoes with good grip; the boulders near the waterfall stay wet and slippery
Carry a dry layer, as spray inside the cave can dampen clothing quickly
Visit outside the coldest winter months, when icy rock makes the scramble considerably riskier

itinerary.title: Above Chamunda Devi, a Knoll With Dhauladhar Views
itinerary.trailType: riverside path, forested knoll, temple courtyard steps
itinerary.description: The Chamunda Devi trek is a gentle counterpoint to the high-altitude routes further up the valley, built around a short forested walk above one of Kangra's most visited temples. Starting from the temple complex on the banks of the Baner river, the trail crosses a suspension footbridge before climbing a stepped path into a stand of chir pine that shields the route from direct sun for most of its length. Sadhus and pilgrims are a common sight on the lower stretch, some resting under the pines after visiting the temple, and the atmosphere carries a distinctly devotional character quite different from the more purely scenic trails elsewhere in the region. The path climbs a low, rounded knoll overlooking the temple and the river valley, gaining just enough height to open a genuinely wide panorama: the Dhauladhar's full wall rising abruptly from the Kangra plain, snow visible on its upper reaches for much of the year even when the valley floor is warm and dry. From this vantage, the temple's distinctive golden dome and the terraced fields surrounding it are laid out clearly below, and the Baner river can be traced as a silver thread winding through the valley toward its confluence further downstream. A small clearing near the top of the knoll makes a natural spot to stop for a packed lunch, and langurs are frequently spotted moving through the pines nearby, generally indifferent to visitors. The descent follows the same stepped path back to the temple, and many visitors combine the walk with time spent at the temple itself, taking in the steady stream of devotees and the general bustle of one of the district's most active pilgrimage sites before heading back across the footbridge to the roadside.
highlights: A suspension footbridge crossing over the Baner river
Sadhus and pilgrims resting along the forested lower trail
Panoramic views of the full Dhauladhar wall from a low knoll
The temple's golden dome and terraced surroundings seen from above
tips: Combine the walk with a visit to the temple itself before or after
Visit on a weekday if you prefer a quieter experience, as weekends draw large pilgrim crowds
Carry water, as the lower pine forest offers little shade relief from valley heat in summer

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated easy. The Chamunda Trek demands easy effort across 1 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 3500 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Chamunda Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 1D (day trek). Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 4 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 3,500 ft (1,067 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Chamunda Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Chamunda Devi Temple area. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Chamunda Trek trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: Not applicable — day trek

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Chamunda Trek demands easy effort across 1 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 3500 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to Kangra. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Chamunda Devi Temple area.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Chamunda Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Chamunda Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Kangra. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Chamunda Trek crowded?
faqs.answer: Popular sections near towns can be busy on weekends; early starts help.

faqs.question: Can I combine Chamunda with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

title: Kunal Pathri Trek
summary: Sacred hill walk to Kunal Pathri Temple — pine forests, local pilgrims, and a serene half-day outing from Dharamshala. From ₹799.
overview:
Kunal Pathri Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Sacred hill walk to Kunal Pathri Temple — pine forests, local pilgrims, and a serene half-day outing from Dharamshala.

Kunal Pathri’s temple hill is pine-scented and locally loved — a half-day cultural climb when you want calm over conquest.

The itinerary usually starts at Kunal Pathri Temple approach and ends at Kunal Pathri, spanning about 5 km across 1 day(s) with a high point near 6,500 ft. Graded easy, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Kunal Pathri corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Kunal Pathri Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Kunal Pathri main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Not applicable — day trek.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: Charge devices in town before starting.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Kunal Pathri: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dharamshala a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Kunal Pathri Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Chamunda Trek, Aghanjar Mahadev Trek, Galu Devi Trek, Snowline Laka Trek.

Child policy for Kunal Pathri Trek: 8+ with guardians. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Kunal Pathri Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

Map coordinates placeholder: 32.3500°N, 76.3750°E (CMS-editable Google Map embed).

Video and extended gallery placeholders are reserved for Website Management CMS uploads.

Fixed departure calendar and live pricing shown in the booking panel remain editable from CMS.
