itinerary.title: Dharamshala to the Rock-Cleft Shrine of Kunal Pathri
itinerary.trailType: forest ridge path, rock-cut cave approach
itinerary.description: Kunal Pathri offers one of the easiest and most accessible short hikes in the immediate vicinity of Dharamshala town, making it a popular choice for visitors without the time or inclination for a multi-day trek. The path begins near the town's upper bazaar area and climbs through a pleasant belt of deodar forest, the trail wide and well maintained given the volume of local visitors it receives, particularly on weekends and during festival periods. Squirrels and a variety of small forest birds are commonly seen darting through the lower canopy, and the relatively gentle gradient makes this a genuinely comfortable walk even for those unused to hill terrain. As the trail nears its destination, it narrows to approach a distinctive rock formation split by a natural fissure, within which sits the small Kunal Pathri shrine, a site local legend connects to the era of the Pandavas and, in some tellings, to a resting place associated with Sita during her forest exile. Whatever the precise legend favoured by a given local guide, the shrine's setting inside the rock cleft gives it a genuinely distinctive character compared to more conventional temple buildings elsewhere in the district. From the small platform just outside the shrine, views open out over Dharamshala's lower town and, on clear days, toward the plains beyond, a satisfying reward for a walk that demands relatively little physical effort. The site rarely feels crowded even when other nearby attractions are busy, and many visitors linger for a while in the cool shade of the rock cleft before making the short, easy descent back through the same deodar forest to town, often timing the return to coincide with lunch at one of Dharamshala's many cafes.
highlights: An easy, well-maintained forest walk close to Dharamshala town
A shrine set within a natural rock fissure linked to Pandava-era legend
Views over lower Dharamshala and the plains from the shrine platform
A quiet alternative to busier nearby attractions
tips: A good choice for a low-effort acclimatisation walk before a longer trek
Visit in the late afternoon for softer light over the valley views
Wear comfortable footwear even though the gradient is gentle; the path can be uneven in places

title: Snowline Trek
summary: 3D/2N from Bhagsunag — Triund camp, then climb to Laka Glacier / Snowline with Indrahar Pass views. From ₹2,500.
overview:
Snowline Laka (also called Snowline or Laka Got) sits in the Dhauladhar range above Triund near McLeod Ganj. If Triund is on your list, this extension takes you closer to the snowline and the approach toward Indrahar Pass.

The trail is steeper beyond Triund and rewards you with glacier scenery, possible snow walks in season, and occasional wildlife sightings such as mountain goats. Camping nights at Triund and Laka make it a fuller weekend than a single Triund overnight.

Rated easy to moderate, the 3 days / 2 nights itinerary starts at Bhagsunag at 10:00 AM and typically finishes back there around 2:00 PM on Day 3.

quickInfo.groupSize: Join a group | Weekend departures
quickInfo.fitnessLevel: Easy to moderate — Triund + Snowline extension

highlights: Extend classic Triund into Laka Glacier / Snowline
Bhagsu waterfall stop on the climb
Camp two nights — Triund Top and Laka
Close views toward Indrahar Pass
Steeper snowline trail that challenges your stamina
Guided camping with veg meals and toilet tents
Ideal long-weekend upgrade from a Triund overnight

itinerary.title: McLeod Ganj to Triund via the Bhagsu Waterfall Route
itinerary.trailType: waterfall approach, misty oak forest, rhododendron switchbacks
itinerary.description: The walk up to Triund for this snowline-focused trek starts from McLeod Ganj's main chowk and drops briefly toward Bhagsu before rejoining the standard climb, a slightly longer opening than the direct Dharamkot route but one that takes in the roar of the Bhagsu waterfall before the real climbing begins. From the waterfall, the trail switches back sharply into oak forest, the canopy here noticeably denser and damper than on the more open Dharamkot side, and in the monsoon shoulder months a scattering of small leeches can appear on the wetter stretches, easily managed with salt or a quick check of boots at rest stops. As the trail climbs, mist frequently drifts through the trees even on otherwise clear mornings, muffling sound and giving the forest an unusually still, closed-in quality that many find more atmospheric than scenic. Higher up, the oak gives way to rhododendron, and a series of increasingly open switchbacks deliver expanding views back over McLeod Ganj's scattered rooftops and the wooded ridge sheltering Bhagsu below. The final approach to Triund crosses a band of wind-stunted trees before the ridge opens fully, and for this particular trek, reaching Triund carries a specific purpose beyond the view: from here, the group gets its first proper look at the snowline itself, the visible boundary partway up the Dhauladhar's flank where persistent snow begins, its exact position shifting noticeably by season and providing a useful preview of tomorrow's target elevation. Camp is set on the ridge's eastern shoulder, chosen for the clearest possible sightline to that snow boundary, and the evening briefing focuses on judging current snowline conditions and what gear will be needed to walk comfortably along its edge the next day.
highlights: Starting near the Bhagsu waterfall before the climb proper begins
Damp, mist-filled oak forest distinct from the drier Dharamkot route
Expanding switchback views over McLeod Ganj and Bhagsu
A first sighting of the seasonal snowline from Triund's ridge
tips: Check boots and socks for leeches on damp monsoon-shoulder stretches
Carry a light waterproof layer, as mist can turn to fine drizzle without much warning
Use the Triund ridge view to gauge how far the snowline sits before tomorrow's walk

itinerary.title: Triund to the Snowline Camp
itinerary.trailType: grass and scree slope, seasonal snowfield edge
itinerary.description: Today's walk is short in distance but distinct in character from the other stages leading out of Triund, since the goal here is not a pass or a cave shelter but the snowline itself, and the pace reflects that more exploratory purpose. The trail climbs the same initial scree and tussock slope used by routes heading toward Lahesh Cave, but branches away after the first hour onto a more direct, steeper line aimed squarely at the lowest visible patches of old snow clinging to shaded gullies on the ridge's flank. Underfoot, the ground shifts noticeably as the party gains height, packed earth giving way to increasingly loose, frost-shattered rock, and the first real patches of snow appear earlier in the day than most visitors expect, often lingering in pockets well below where the bulk of the snowfield begins. Reaching the true snowline, where a continuous white blanket finally covers the ground rather than isolated patches, is a genuinely satisfying milestone, and the guide typically allows time here for the simple pleasure of walking on snow at altitude, testing microspikes on a gentle slope, or, weather and group energy permitting, a bit of informal glissading down a short, safe stretch. Camp for the night is pitched on a rocky bench just below the main snowfield, chosen specifically to avoid the risk of overnight snowmelt runoff pooling under tents, and the setting is bright and stark, framed by the Dhauladhar's upper rock towers rising directly behind camp. Temperatures fall sharply the moment the sun disappears, and condensation on tent walls freezes into a fine frost by morning, a small but memorable detail of camping this close to a genuine, if seasonal, snowline.
highlights: A direct climb aimed at the visible snowline rather than a pass or cave
Early snow patches appearing well below the main snowfield
Time to test microspikes or try a short, safe glissade
A bright, frost-rimed camp just below the snow's edge
tips: Test microspikes on a gentle slope before relying on them anywhere steeper
Only attempt a glissade on a clearly safe, guide-approved stretch
Expect tent walls to frost over by morning at this camp

itinerary.title: Snowline Back Down to McLeod Ganj
itinerary.trailType: scree and grass descent, forest switchbacks, town approach
itinerary.description: The final day traces a dramatic transition in miniature, dropping from the frost-hardened ground around the snowline camp to the warm, crowded streets of McLeod Ganj in the span of a single walk. The descent begins across the same loose, frost-shattered rock climbed the previous day, now considerably easier to negotiate downhill despite the persistent looseness underfoot, and the snow patches passed at close range on the way up shrink to distant white smudges within the first hour. Triund's ridge, reached by mid-morning, offers a natural pause, and the contrast between the stark snowline camp just left behind and the ridge's comparatively lush grass is often remarked on as one of the trek's more striking small details. From Triund, the trail drops into the familiar rhododendron and oak forest, and the tea houses passed on the way up two days earlier reappear as welcome rest points, several vendors recognising returning trekkers and asking, with genuine curiosity, how far up the snow actually reached this particular week. Lower in the forest, troops of langurs are a common sight moving unhurriedly through the canopy, entirely unbothered by the passing group, and the trail's final stretch through Bhagsu brings the roar of the waterfall back into range before the last short climb into McLeod Ganj itself. Arriving at the town's main chowk by early afternoon, the group is met by the ordinary bustle of prayer wheels, cafes, and street vendors, a striking contrast to the silence of the snowline camp barely six hours' walk behind them. Most groups use the remaining daylight to rest properly, the short but genuinely varied trek having packed a surprising range of terrain and temperature into just three days on foot.
highlights: A single-day transition from frost-hardened snowline camp to town streets
A contrast pause at Triund between stark snow and lush ridge grass
Familiar tea-house vendors curious about this season's snowline
Langur troops in the lower forest on the final descent
tips: Layer down gradually as you descend; the temperature swing across the day is significant
Chat with tea-house vendors about the snowline; their seasonal knowledge is genuinely useful
Plan a rest afternoon in McLeod Ganj after this varied, temperature-swinging trek

inclusions: Welcome / evening tea, 2 dinners, 1 lunch, and 2 breakfasts (veg only)
Camping gear — tents, sleeping bags, sleeping mats, kitchen utensils
First-aid medical kit with the team
exclusions: Any cost arising due to natural calamities
Transport to / from Dharamshala or Bhagsunag
Any other service not mentioned in inclusions

fitness.description: Plan for three walking days including the Triund climb plus a steeper push to Laka. Basic fitness and prior day-hike experience help on Day 2’s rocky snowline section.
tips: Complete a long weekend walk (10–12 km) before departure
Pack warm layers for Triund and Laka nights
Carry trekking poles for descent on Day 3
Break in sturdy shoes for rocky snowline terrain
Arrive at Bhagsunag by 10:00 AM on Day 1

map.overview: Start and end at Bhagsu Nag, Dharamshala. Day 1 climbs via Bhagsu waterfall and Leta to Triund Top. Day 2 continues ~6 km to Laka Glacier / Snowline (~3,200 m). Day 3 descends ~12 km to Bhagsunag. Reach Dharamshala by air (Gaggal), overnight bus from Delhi/Chandigarh, or train to Pathankot plus road transfer.
map.elevationNote: Max altitude ~3,200 m (10,499 ft) at Laka. Steeper grade beyond Triund.

weather.note: Deep snow possible at Laka

faqs.question: Is Snowline Laka Trek difficult?
faqs.answer: It is rated easy to moderate. Day 1 follows the classic Triund trail; Day 2 adds a steeper, rockier climb to Laka / Snowline (~3,200 m). Beginners with basic fitness manage it when paced with a guide.

faqs.question: Will there be snow on the Snowline / Laka trail?
faqs.answer: Snow cover increases in winter and early spring. From late autumn through March you are more likely to walk on snow patches or continuous snow near Laka Glacier. Summer may show only patches depending on the season.

faqs.question: How is this trek connected to Triund?
faqs.answer: Day 1 camps at Triund Top after the Bhagsunag–waterfall–Leta climb. Day 2 continues ~6 km beyond Triund to Laka Glacier / Snowline with views toward Indrahar Pass, then Day 3 descends all the way to Bhagsunag.

faqs.question: Where does the Snowline Laka Trek start?
faqs.answer: Meet at Bhagsunag (Bhagsu Nag), Dharamshala, by 10:00 AM on Day 1 near the temple / taxi stand area. The route goes via Bhagsu waterfall toward Triund, then onward to Laka.

faqs.question: How long is the Snowline Laka Trek?
faqs.answer: Plan for 3 days / 2 nights. Total walking is around 24 km including Triund and the Laka extension. Day 3 typically ends back at Bhagsunag around 2:00 PM.

faqs.question: What is included in the package?
faqs.answer: Welcome / evening tea, 2 dinners, 1 lunch, 2 breakfasts (veg), certified guide, camping gear, first-aid kit with the team, and pit-style toilet tents.

faqs.question: What is your cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the tour cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available in those cases. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

title: Laka Glacier Trek
summary: Continue past Snowline to Laka Got — glacial cirque, Lahesh Cave approach, and Indrahar Pass walls on a 3D/2N Dhauladhar classic. From ₹2,899.
overview:
Laka Glacier Trek is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Continue past Snowline to Laka Got — glacial cirque, Lahesh Cave approach, and Indrahar Pass walls on a 3D/2N Dhauladhar classic.

Laka Got feels like the antechamber of Indrahar — a cold cirque where summer still holds snowfields and the wind funnels straight off the pass. Unlike busy Triund, the final approach is quiet, rocky, and unmistakably alpine.

The itinerary usually starts at Bhagsunag and ends at Bhagsunag, spanning about 22 km across 3 day(s) with a high point near 11,480 ft. Graded moderate, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Laka Glacier corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Laka Glacier Trek: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Laka Glacier main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Laka Glacier camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Laka Glacier: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in McLeod Ganj a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Laka Glacier Trek: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Indrahar Pass Trek, Kareri Village Trek, Minkiani Pass Trek, Kareri Lake Trek.

Child policy for Laka Glacier Trek: 12+ with fitness. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Laka Glacier Trek departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

Things to carry beyond the packing list: Layered clothing; Rain shell; Headlamp; Personal medicines; Reusable water bottle.

Things not to carry: Single-use plastics beyond reusable bottles; Heavy jeans or cotton-heavy clothing for multi-day wet weather; Unnecessary valuables and excess jewellery; Alcohol and recreational drugs.

itinerary.title: Dharamkot to Triund, En Route to Laka Got
itinerary.trailType: cafe-lined forest track, oak and horse-chestnut canopy, open ridge meadow
itinerary.description: This trek's approach to Triund follows the standard Dharamkot route, but the framing is different from a typical overnight visit, since the goal two days from now is a close view of one of the Dhauladhar's accessible glacial patches at Laka Got rather than the ridge itself. The trail climbs out of Dharamkot past a cluster of rooftop cafes that mark the last real comforts before the forest, and the walking settles into a steady rhythm through oak and horse-chestnut, the path wide and obvious thanks to heavy daily foot traffic on this particular stretch. A resident troop of rhesus macaques is often encountered around the midpoint, considerably bolder than the langurs found higher up, and food should be kept firmly out of sight and reach through this section. As height is gained, the forest opens into intermittent meadow, and distant thunderheads are a common late-morning sight building over the plains to the south, a useful early warning system for planning the day's pace, since afternoon storms on this side of the range can arrive with real speed. The final stretch onto Triund's ridge climbs through stunted, weather-flagged trees, and the ridge itself, when reached, offers the first distant look toward Laka Got's higher ground, a subtly different skyline feature from the more commonly photographed Indrahar approach visible from the same viewpoint. Camp is set with an eye toward an early departure the next morning, since reaching Laka Got and its glacier views in good light depends on covering the short but steep stage before any midday cloud has a chance to build. The evening briefing accordingly emphasises an early start over any other single piece of advice for the following day.
highlights: Climbing through Dharamkot's rooftop cafes before the forest begins
Bold rhesus macaques encountered along the mid-route forest
Distant thunderheads over the plains as a natural weather gauge
A first distant glimpse of the Laka Got skyline from Triund
tips: Keep all food sealed and out of sight around the macaque troop's territory
Watch the southern skyline for building thunderheads as an informal forecast
Get an early night, since tomorrow's stage rewards an early start with clearer views

itinerary.title: Triund to Laka Got's Glacier Views
itinerary.trailType: grass ridge, contouring scree bowl, glacier-view rock shelf
itinerary.description: The walk from Triund to Laka Got shares its first hour with the trail toward Lahesh Cave, climbing the same long scree and tussock slope, before splitting onto a gentler, more contouring line that curves around the shoulder of the ridge rather than climbing it head-on. This alternative line trades some of the direct elevation gain of the pass trail for wider, more sustained views across the face of the range, and a small tarn passed roughly halfway makes a natural rest stop, its water fed by exactly the kind of high snow patch the day's route is ultimately aimed at. As the trail rounds the final shoulder, Laka Got opens into view: a broad rock and grass shelf sitting almost directly beneath a substantial patch of permanent snow and old glacial ice wedged into a north-facing bowl in the ridge above, one of the more accessible close-up glacier views available on any short Dhauladhar trek. The ice itself, streaked with rock debris and meltwater channels, feeds a thin stream that crosses the shelf before dropping toward the valley, and the sound of occasional small rockfall or ice-cracking from the bowl above is a genuine, slightly unsettling reminder that the feature, while stable in the short term, is a living, shifting piece of terrain rather than a static backdrop. Camp is pitched a cautious distance back from the bowl's base, on flatter ground safely out of any obvious fall line, and the late afternoon light on the ice, shifting from white to blue-grey as the sun swings around, is one of the trek's most photographed moments. Evening temperatures drop quickly given the proximity to so much exposed ice and snow, and the group typically retreats to sleeping bags earlier than on a standard Triund-area camp.
highlights: A contouring alternative line offering wide views across the range's face
Close-up sight of the permanent snow and glacial ice bowl at Laka Got
A thin meltwater stream crossing directly through the rock shelf camp
Shifting blue-grey light on the ice through the late afternoon
tips: Pitch and sit well back from the bowl's base, clear of any obvious rockfall line
Bring an extra layer for the noticeably colder evening this close to the ice
Photograph the ice in the late afternoon light for the most dramatic colour

itinerary.title: Laka Got Back Down to McLeod Ganj
itinerary.trailType: contour trail, scree slope, forest descent into town
itinerary.description: Leaving Laka Got, the trail retraces the contouring line back toward the main Triund ridge trail, the glacier bowl shrinking steadily behind the group and, for many, already looking almost unreal in scale compared to how close and immediate it felt at camp only an hour before. Rejoining the standard trail near the point where the two routes split the previous day, the walk down to Triund passes quickly, legs already warmed up from the initial descent, and the ridge itself is reached in time for a final, lingering look back at the ice bowl, now visible as only a small pale patch against the much larger backdrop of the Dhauladhar's grey rock. From Triund, the group commits to the long forest descent shared by most routes off this stretch of ridge, dropping through rhododendron and then oak as the gradient eases in stages. Local day-hikers heading up in the opposite direction are common on this lower stretch, many asking eagerly about conditions at Laka Got, since the glacier viewpoint has a smaller but genuinely enthusiastic following among repeat visitors to the area. The trail's final approach into Dharamkot and then McLeod Ganj follows familiar ground, cafes and guesthouse signboards reappearing as the forest gives way to the built edges of town, and the distance covered today, considerably longer than either preceding day thanks to the added detour to and from Laka Got, is felt clearly in most people's legs by the final stretch. Arriving back in McLeod Ganj by mid-to-late afternoon, the trek closes on a note most participants describe as centred less on any single ridge or pass and more on the specific, memorable sight of standing beside genuine glacial ice within a three-day window from town.
highlights: A final lingering look back at the ice bowl from Triund's ridge
Meeting curious day-hikers heading up toward Laka Got
The trek's longest single descent, covering the added detour distance
Returning to McLeod Ganj having stood beside genuine glacial ice
tips: Pace this longer final day steadily rather than rushing the early contour section
Share honest conditions with hikers heading up if asked; it helps their planning
Treat your legs to a proper rest in McLeod Ganj after the trek's longest day

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: This route is rated moderate. The Laka Glacier Trek demands moderate effort across 3 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 11480 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Laka Glacier Trek take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 3D / 2N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 22 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 11,480 ft (3,499 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Laka Glacier Trek start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Bhagsunag. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Laka Glacier Trek trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: Designated Laka Glacier camping zones Backup lower camp in bad weather

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Laka Glacier Trek demands moderate effort across 3 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 11480 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to McLeod Ganj. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Bhagsunag.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Laka Glacier Trek safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Laka Glacier Trek?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in McLeod Ganj. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Laka Glacier Trek crowded?
faqs.answer: Laka Glacier sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Laka Glacier with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.

title: Thatharana Trek
summary: 3D/2N Thatharana Trek from Dharamshala, Himachal Pradesh — guided Himalayan experience with India Holiday Destinations.
overview: Thatharana Trek is a carefully curated Himalayan trek based around Dharamshala in Himachal Pradesh. Rated moderate with a 3D/2N itinerary, this journey balances scenic walking, safe camping or homestays, and memorable mountain views. India Holiday Destinations handles permits, experienced local guides, meals on trek, and logistics so you can focus on the trail.
highlights: Scenic Dharamshala trailhead access
3D/2N guided itinerary
moderate difficulty with local support
Meals and camping/homestay as per plan
Experienced mountain guides

itinerary.title: Naddi Into the Quiet Forest Below Thatharana
itinerary.trailType: deodar forest track, ridge spur, forest-edge camp
itinerary.description: Thatharana remains one of the quieter, less-documented walks above Dharamshala, and the approach begins from Naddi, climbing through a dense belt of deodar forest along a ridge spur rarely used by the larger groups heading toward Triund on the opposite side of town. The track underfoot is a mix of packed earth and exposed root, well shaded for almost the entire distance, and the relative quiet of the forest here, without the steady stream of day-trippers found on more popular trails, is one of the walk's immediate and notable qualities. A scattering of old, moss-covered boundary stones mark what were once grazing rights divisions between neighbouring villages, a small detail that local guides are often happy to explain in more detail than most visitors expect from what looks like an unremarkable forest walk. Woodpeckers and nuthatches are frequently heard working the deodar trunks, their tapping carrying clearly through the still air, and a resident pair of Himalayan griffons is sometimes seen circling over a rocky outcrop passed roughly two-thirds of the way up. As the ridge spur narrows, the forest begins to open in patches, offering early, partial views back over Dharamshala's scattered rooftops and, further out, the haze of the Kangra plains. Camp for the first night is set at the edge of the forest, just below the true Thatharana meadow that tomorrow's walk will properly explore, chosen for the shelter the surrounding trees provide against any overnight wind. The evening is notably peaceful, and with genuinely few other groups likely to be anywhere nearby, the sense of having found an uncrowded corner of the Dhauladhar foothills is one of this short trek's clearest appeals.
highlights: A quieter ridge-spur route rarely used by larger Triund-bound groups
Old moss-covered boundary stones marking historic grazing divisions
Woodpeckers and nuthatches working the deodar trunks
An uncrowded forest-edge camp below the Thatharana meadow
tips: Bring binoculars if you enjoy birdwatching; this quieter forest rewards patient observers
Ask your guide about the old boundary stones for a bit of local history
Expect very few other trekking groups on this route, a genuine rarity near Dharamshala

itinerary.title: Onto the Open Meadow of Thatharana
itinerary.trailType: open meadow trail, gentle grassy ridge, forest-edge exit
itinerary.description: Leaving the forest-edge camp, today's short climb finally delivers the group onto the true Thatharana meadow, a broad, gently rolling expanse of grass that opens with a suddenness typical of Dhauladhar foothill treks, the dense deodar canopy giving way within a matter of minutes to unobstructed sky. Wildflowers cover much of the meadow in season, and grazing is still practised here by a small number of local shepherd families, whose flocks can often be seen moving slowly across the far slopes, tended by dogs whose barking is frequently the loudest sound heard all day. Walking further onto the meadow's higher ground reveals a genuinely wide panorama, the Dhauladhar's rock and snow wall rising to the north in close, almost overwhelming detail given how little elevation has actually been gained relative to higher trekking routes in the same range, and to the south, the Kangra valley spreads out in full, its patchwork of tea gardens and scattered towns visible without the haze that often obscures the view from lower vantage points. A shallow depression near the meadow's centre holds a small seasonal pool, fed by snowmelt run-off, and it is common to see local cattle watering here through the warmer months. Camp for the second night is pitched on the meadow's flattest section, well away from any grazing paths, and the openness of the site makes for a spectacular, largely unobstructed sunset, the light stretching long shadows across the grass before the temperature drops with the sun's disappearance behind the ridge. With no onward climb required tomorrow beyond the return descent, the evening is unusually relaxed, and many groups spend a good stretch of it simply sitting out under a sky that, this far from any town, fills with stars almost immediately after dusk.
highlights: Emerging suddenly from forest onto the open Thatharana meadow
Wide, close-range views of the Dhauladhar's rock and snow wall
A shallow seasonal pool used by grazing cattle
A spectacular, unobstructed sunset from the meadow campsite
tips: Pitch well clear of visible grazing paths to avoid disturbed sleep from passing herds
Stay for the sunset before retreating to your tent; the open meadow offers a rare unobstructed view
Bring a wide-angle lens or simply enjoy the sky without a camera; light pollution here is essentially zero

itinerary.title: Thatharana Down to Dharamshala
itinerary.trailType: meadow descent, ridge spur forest, town approach road
itinerary.description: The return leg drops off the Thatharana meadow the way the group came, retracing the ridge spur down through deodar forest, though the descent follows a slightly different lower branch that exits toward Dharamshala's main town rather than looping back to Naddi, giving the trek a small but satisfying change of scenery on its final stretch. The moss-covered boundary stones and the same patches of forest-floor fern seen on the way up reappear in reverse, and the birdlife, woodpeckers especially, remains a steady presence through the quieter middle section of the descent. As the trail loses height, deodar gradually gives way to chir pine, and the forest floor grows drier and dustier underfoot, a small but noticeable shift reflecting the lower, warmer elevation being approached. Partial views of Dharamshala's scattered lower town reappear through gaps in the pine, considerably closer and more detailed now than the hazier glimpses caught from the meadow the previous day, and the trail eventually meets a narrow village road that leads the final distance into town past small roadside shops and a scatter of houses with neatly tended kitchen gardens. Reaching Dharamshala's main bazaar area by early afternoon brings a distinct sense of having covered real ground in a short window, the transition from an almost entirely empty high meadow to a bustling small-town street happening within the space of a single morning's walk. For a trek that saw so few other visitors throughout its three days, the return to town's ordinary noise and activity feels like a genuinely abrupt shift, and most groups agree afterward that Thatharana's appeal lies precisely in that contrast, a short, easily arranged trek that nonetheless delivers a real sense of solitude rarely found on the region's better-known routes.
highlights: A varied descent route exiting toward Dharamshala rather than back to Naddi
A gradual forest shift from deodar to drier chir pine
Closer, clearer views of Dharamshala's lower town on the way down
An abrupt, memorable contrast between the meadow's solitude and the bustling bazaar
tips: Confirm in advance which trailhead your transport will meet you at, since this descent ends in Dharamshala town rather than Naddi
Enjoy the drier pine forest lower down, distinct in feel from the previous days' deodar
Reflect on the trek's rare solitude; few routes this close to Dharamshala offer as quiet an experience

fitness.description: Prepare with regular walks and stair practice before Thatharana Trek.
tips: Walk 4-5 km daily before the trek
Hydrate well
Break in your shoes

title: Hampta Pass Trek
summary: Cross from lush Kullu meadows into the stark Spiti desert — one of India's most dramatic landscape switches in five days.
overview: Hampta Pass is a study in contrast. You begin among wildflowers and river valleys near Manali, climb toward a rocky alpine gateway, and descend into the moonscape silence of Spiti. Over five carefully paced days you’ll experience meadow camps, glacial streams, high wind-swept saddles, and the otherworldly beauty of Chandratal (seasonal option). Our expedition-style planning emphasizes acclimatization cues, weather discretion, and small-group attention.

quickInfo.groupSize: 6–14 trekkers

highlights: Dramatic Spiti desert crossover
River crossings and glacial streams
Lush Hampta valley meadows
High mountain pass experience
Optional Chandratal Lake visit
Stunning campsites
Photography heaven
Experienced high-altitude guidance

itinerary.title: Manali Drive to Jobra and Acclimatisation Walk to Chikka
itinerary.trailType: birch forest path, riverside trail along the Rani Nallah
itinerary.description: The Hampta crossing begins with a bumpy but scenic drive out of Manali, the road climbing past Prini and the apple orchards of Sethan before the tarmac finally gives out at Jobra, a scatter of Gaddi shepherd huts that marks the true trailhead. From here the group shoulders daypacks and drops onto a soft forest path running parallel to the Rani Nallah, its glacial water churning white over grey boulders just below the trail. Birch and maple crowd close on both sides, their leaves rustling in a wind that already carries a noticeably sharper edge than the valley floor, and the first hour is as much about settling into rhythm as covering ground. Wild roses and clumps of nettle line the damper stretches near the stream, while higher up the corridor opens briefly onto boulder fields where the group pauses to look back at the receding green folds of the Kullu valley. Chikka, the day's destination, is a broad grassy shelf pinched between the rushing Rani Nallah and a steep forested slope, used for generations as a shepherd halt before the climb toward Balu ka Ghera begins in earnest. Tents go up on flattened ground near the water's edge, and the short distance covered today is entirely deliberate, giving bodies a gentle first taste of altitude before the real gain begins tomorrow. Guides use the afternoon to check gear, redistribute loads if needed, and walk through the week's plan in detail, including how weather on Hampta's exposed pass can shift within hours. As dusk falls, the temperature drops fast enough that down jackets appear at the fire circle, and the sound of the nallah becomes a constant backdrop to a dinner eaten under a sky already noticeably clearer and starrier than in Manali. It is a short day by distance, but an essential one for settling nerves and legs alike before Hampta's more demanding stretches begin.
highlights: Drive from Manali through Sethan's orchard belt
First walk beside the glacial Rani Nallah
Gentle acclimatisation pacing on day one
Shepherd halt campsite at Chikka
tips: Keep the first day's pace deliberately slow regardless of how fresh you feel
Carry a rain shell even in clear weather, as this corridor can turn misty fast
Fill water bottles at the nallah before pitching camp, since flow is cleanest upstream of the site

itinerary.title: Chikka to Balu ka Ghera through Alpine Meadow
itinerary.trailType: boulder-strewn riverbank, moraine slope, open alpine meadow
itinerary.description: Leaving Chikka, the trail immediately demands more attention, weaving across a boulder field where the Rani Nallah has scattered rock debris over centuries, and trekkers pick a careful line from stone to stone with poles doing much of the balancing work. Beyond the boulders, the path climbs onto steadily narrowing moraine slopes, the vegetation thinning from birch scrub to low juniper and finally to bare alpine turf as the valley walls close in on either side. Waterfalls drop from hanging side valleys at intervals, some crossed on makeshift log bridges and others requiring a direct wade through icy, ankle-deep water that jolts everyone fully awake regardless of the hour. As the gradient eases in the afternoon, the valley opens into the wide, undulating meadow known as Balu ka Ghera, ringed by rock walls and fed by multiple snowmelt streams that braid across the grass in silver threads. The name, loosely translating to a sandy enclosure, reflects the pale, granular soil visible in patches between tufts of grass, distinct from the darker earth seen lower down the valley. This is one of the most photographed campsites on the entire route, with views back down the valley toward Chikka on one side and, ahead, a first glimpse of the rocky notch that marks Hampta Pass itself. Marmots are frequently spotted darting between rocks near camp, and the cold at this altitude arrives early, well before actual sunset. Guides use the extended evening light to point out the pass's exact line on the ridge above and to brief the group thoroughly on tomorrow's demanding crossing, including pacing strategy and what to expect from the steep final climb. Dinner is eaten quickly tonight, appetite often blunted slightly by the altitude gain, and most trekkers retreat to their tents earlier than the previous night, aware that an early start is essential for the pass crossing ahead.
highlights: Boulder-hopping along the Rani Nallah
Multiple glacial stream and waterfall crossings
Wide open meadow camp at Balu ka Ghera
First clear sighting of the Hampta Pass notch
tips: Waterproof your boots or carry sandals for the colder stream crossings
Eat something even if appetite dips, tomorrow is the toughest day of the trek
Zip tents fully at night, as wind at this campsite picks up noticeably after dark

itinerary.title: Balu ka Ghera over Hampta Pass to Shea Goru
itinerary.trailType: steep scree ascent, glacier-edged pass, boulder descent into Lahaul
itinerary.description: This is the day the entire trek pivots around, and the group departs Balu ka Ghera in cold, early light to make the most of firm ground before the sun softens any remaining snow patches on the ascent. The climb toward the pass steepens quickly, following a rough line of scree and loose rock that zigzags up the headwall, with occasional stretches of old, hardened snow that require careful, deliberate footing. Breathing gets noticeably harder as the group gains the final few hundred metres, and short rest stops become more frequent, though the sense of anticipation keeps most legs moving despite the thinning air. Cresting Hampta Pass at just over 14,000 feet delivers one of the most abrupt landscape transitions in the Indian Himalaya: behind lies the green, moisture-fed Kullu valley, while ahead the Lahaul side unfolds in shades of brown, grey, and ochre, utterly stripped of the vegetation seen just metres away on the other side of the ridge. Prayer flags strung across a cairn mark the pass itself, and on a clear day the view extends toward the rugged Spiti-facing ranges and down into the valley the group will descend through for the remainder of the trek. The way down is steep and demands full concentration, dropping through loose scree and boulder fields where trekking poles prove essential, the terrain growing progressively drier and starker with every switchback lost. Shea Goru, the night's camp, sits in a stark, wide valley bed beside a milky glacial stream, entirely without the greenery of the Kullu side, its silence broken mostly by wind moving across bare rock. Reaching camp after a genuinely long day brings real relief, and dinner conversation is dominated by replays of the crossing itself, each trekker comparing notes on the exact moment the landscape flipped from meadow to desert underfoot.
highlights: Crossing Hampta Pass at approximately 14,100 ft
Dramatic shift from Kullu greenery to Lahaul's arid terrain
Steep scree descent into the Chandra valley catchment
Stark glacial camp at Shea Goru
tips: Start in the dark to reach the pass before the snow softens
Sunglasses are essential, as glare off any remaining snow can be intense
Pace the descent carefully, loose scree causes more falls than the climb itself

itinerary.title: Shea Goru Descent to Chatru
itinerary.trailType: arid riverbed track, sandy moraine, Lahaul valley trail
itinerary.description: With the pass now behind the group, today's walk trades yesterday's tension for a long, steady descent through a landscape that feels almost lunar in its bareness, the Chandra valley's tributary stream carving a pale channel through slopes of sand, scree, and wind-sculpted rock. The trail follows this stream loosely, crossing it a few times on stepping stones as the valley widens, and the utter absence of trees or continuous grass cover here stands in sharp contrast to every previous day's greenery. Wind is a near-constant companion through this stretch, funnelled down the open valley with enough force that buffs and hoods stay on for most of the walk, and conversation tends to fade into a steady, meditative rhythm of footsteps instead. Small clusters of blue poppy and other hardy alpine flowers occasionally punctuate the otherwise stark ground in the height of summer, a surprising splash of colour against the browns and greys dominating the scenery. As the trail loses height, the valley broadens further and the first signs of the Manali-Kaza highway traffic become visible in the far distance, a strange, welcome reminder of the wider world after two days of comparative wilderness. Chatru itself is a bare, windswept flat beside the Chandra river, historically used as a halting point by road workers and now a favourite overnight stop for trekkers completing the Hampta crossing, often shared with groups doing the reverse route or overlanding bikers on the Manali-Leh road. The Chandra river here runs a distinct milky turquoise, fed by glaciers upstream, and camp is pitched a safe distance back from its banks. Evening temperatures drop sharply once the sun dips behind the ridge, and the exposed, open character of this camp makes for a very different night's sleep compared to the sheltered meadows on the Kullu side of the pass.
highlights: Long descent through the arid Chandra valley catchment
Wildflowers surviving in an otherwise stark, dry landscape
First distant views of the Manali-Kaza highway
Windswept riverside camp at Chatru beside the milky Chandra river
tips: Keep a windproof jacket handy all day, this valley is rarely still
Watch your footing on the sandy moraine sections, which shift underfoot
Secure loose items in your tent overnight, as gusts here can be strong

itinerary.title: Chatru to Chandratal (Seasonal) and Drive Back to Manali
itinerary.trailType: motorable mountain road, gravel lakeside path
itinerary.description: The final day shifts gears entirely, trading trekking boots for a long but spectacular drive, though when seasonal road conditions allow, most groups build in a stop at Chandratal, the crescent-shaped lake that has become almost synonymous with this region of Himachal. The road out of Chatru climbs steadily through more of Lahaul's stark, folded terrain before a short side track branches off toward the lake, and the first sight of Chandratal's improbably still, blue-green water set against a bowl of bare mountains rarely fails to draw an audible reaction from the group. A gentle walk around a section of the lake's grassy fore-shore reveals its full, elongated crescent shape, the feature that gives it the name Moon Lake, and on calm days the surrounding peaks reflect on the water with striking clarity. Local legend holds that fairies visit the lake at night to dance on its shores, a story shepherds and traders have told for generations along this old trade route between Lahaul and Spiti. After time spent at the lake, weather and road conditions permitting, the group re-boards vehicles for the long drive back over Rohtang Pass toward Manali, the route retracing much of the historic trade corridor that once linked Kullu with Ladakh via Lahaul. The drive itself is scenic in its own right, passing glacier tongues, roadside waterfalls, and the dramatic hairpins below Rohtang before descending back into the familiar green of the Kullu valley by evening. Arriving in Manali marks the formal end of the trek, and the drive gives everyone a final, unhurried chance to look back at the pass crossed two days earlier, now a distant notch on the skyline, and to reflect on a route that manages to compress an astonishing range of Himalayan landscapes into just five days.
highlights: Seasonal visit to the crescent-shaped Chandratal lake
Local fairy-dance legend associated with Moon Lake
Scenic drive back over Rohtang Pass
Trek conclusion in Manali by evening
tips: Chandratal access depends entirely on road and weather conditions, so keep expectations flexible
Carry warm layers for the drive, as Rohtang stays cold even in peak summer
Keep travel documents handy, as this stretch sometimes has checkpoints

inclusions: First-aid, oxygen readiness on critical days

fitness.description: Expect long summit-day hours and altitude near 14,000 ft. Prior weekend treks and cardio preparation strongly recommended.
tips: Jog / brisk walk 4–5 days a week for 3–4 weeks
Do one practice hike with 6–8 kg backpack
Strength train legs and core twice weekly
Learn basic breath pacing for climbs

map.overview: Kullu meadows → Hampta Pass → Spiti side camps → Chatru corridor.
map.elevationNote: Peak gain on Day 3 at approx. 14,100 ft.

title: Bhrigu Lake Trek
summary: A high alpine lake trail above Manali with meadow camps, dramatic ridgelines, and crystal-blue glacial water.
overview: Bhrigu Lake is Manali’s alpine jewel — a compact itinerary with oversized scenery. Within a few days you trade orchards and valley roads for flower meadows, ridgelines, and a sacred high lake cradled by peaks. Ideal for trekkers seeking altitude experience without a long expedition calendar, Bhrigu rewards preparation with quiet morning light and mirror-still water when winds sleep.

quickInfo.groupSize: 6–12 trekkers

highlights: Sacred alpine lake at ~14,000 ft
Flower meadows in season
Beautiful ridgeline campsites
Himalayan panorama views
Compact high-altitude experience
Photography-friendly light
Close to Manali access
Clear night skies

itinerary.title: Manali to Gulaba and Trek to Rola Kholi Camp
itinerary.trailType: pine forest track, open pasture, gentle ridge climb
itinerary.description: The Bhrigu Lake trek starts with a short drive from Manali along the Rohtang road to Gulaba, a checkpoint and grazing ground just beyond the tree line where the road begins its climb toward the pass proper. From the roadside, the trail cuts away from the highway almost immediately, entering a stand of chir pine before opening onto rolling pasture dotted with grazing sheep and the temporary huts of Gaddi shepherd families who spend the summer here with their flocks. The gradient is forgiving by Himalayan standards, climbing in long, easy stretches rather than sharp switchbacks, which makes this an appealing route for trekkers newer to high-altitude walking. As height is gained, views open steadily back toward the Rohtang road snaking up the opposite slope, and the Beas valley recedes into a green corridor far below. Wildflowers, particularly potentilla and clusters of wild geranium, colour the meadow edges through the summer months, and marmots occasionally break cover near rockier patches close to camp. Rola Kholi itself is a grassy shelf tucked below a low ridge, chosen for its access to a reliable spring and its sheltered position relative to the wind that picks up higher on the route toward Bhrigu. Tents are pitched with clear views toward the Pir Panjal's snow-streaked crest, and the relatively gentle effort required to reach this first camp leaves most trekkers with energy to spare for an evening stroll along the ridge above the site. Guides use the stop to explain the mythology tied to Bhrigu Lake, said to be named for the sage Bhrigu who is believed to have meditated at its shores, and to outline the following day's longer, higher push toward the lake itself. Dinner is taken early, the mountain air turning sharply cold once the sun dips behind the western ridge, and the first true taste of alpine cold sets the tone for the days ahead.
highlights: Gentle ridge walk from the Gulaba roadhead
Gaddi shepherd pastures and grazing meadows
Early views of the Pir Panjal's snow crest
Sheltered first camp at Rola Kholi with a reliable spring
tips: This is an easier first day, use it to test your pace rather than rushing
Carry a personal water bottle refill, the Rola Kholi spring is the last reliable source before higher camps
Sunscreen matters even on overcast days at this altitude

itinerary.title: Rola Kholi to Bhrigu Lake and Back to Camp
itinerary.trailType: open ridge climb, alpine meadow, high-altitude lake basin
itinerary.description: Today's climb leaves the last low scrub behind within the first hour, the trail rising steadily along an increasingly bare ridge that offers uninterrupted views in almost every direction as height accumulates. The going is a mix of grassy slope and looser rocky ground, and the thinning air makes the final approach noticeably more tiring than the distance alone would suggest, with short pauses becoming more frequent as the group nears 14,000 feet. Bhrigu Lake itself appears with little warning, a still, glacial pool set in a wide, otherwise treeless bowl below the ridge, its water taking on an almost metallic grey-blue sheen under overcast skies and a brighter turquoise when the sun breaks through. According to local belief, the lake never fully freezes even through the harshest winters, an oddity attributed to its association with the sage Bhrigu, and its waters are considered sacred by communities across the Kullu valley, some of whom make an annual pilgrimage here during the Fagli festival. The setting is starkly beautiful, ringed by rounded, grass-covered peaks rather than jagged rock, giving the basin an open, exposed feel very different from more enclosed glacial lakes elsewhere in Himachal. Trekkers typically spend a good half hour at the water's edge, and on genuinely clear days the panorama extends toward the Dhauladhar range to the south and the deeper folds of the Kullu valley to the west. The return to Rola Kholi retraces the same ridge, considerably quicker downhill though still demanding careful footing on the looser sections, and reaching camp again by mid-afternoon leaves time to rest and dry out any gear before the evening chill sets in. Dinner conversation tends to circle back repeatedly to the lake's changing colours and the strange, slightly eerie stillness of its water.
highlights: Reaching Bhrigu Lake at roughly 14,100 ft
The lake's unusual year-round unfrozen water
Wide, open grass-ridge terrain uncommon on other Himachal treks
Panoramic views toward the Dhauladhar range
tips: Start early, as afternoon cloud cover often rolls in and obscures the lake by midday
Wear layers you can shed quickly, temperature swings fast between exertion and rest stops
Do not disturb offerings left at the lake's edge by local pilgrims

itinerary.title: Rola Kholi to Khanul Camp via the Ridge Traverse
itinerary.trailType: grassy ridge traverse, scattered boulder field, descending pasture
itinerary.description: Rather than retracing the exact outbound route on the final approach toward Vashisht, the trek shifts today onto a parallel ridge line, traversing a stretch of high pasture that most standard out-and-back itineraries skip entirely. The walking is a gentler blend of grass and scattered boulder, with the trail hugging the contour rather than climbing hard, and the views compensate generously for the modest technical demands, taking in a wide sweep of the Beas valley on one side and glimpses of yesterday's lake basin on the other. Shepherd cairns mark the route at intervals, a practical navigation aid for Gaddi herders moving flocks between grazing grounds through the short summer season, and their significance is often explained by guides familiar with the seasonal migration patterns still practised in this part of Kullu. As the trail begins to lose height in the afternoon, birch and juniper scrub reappear for the first time since Rola Kholi, softening the stark alpine character of the previous two days and signalling the gradual return toward the treeline. Khanul camp sits on a broad, sheltered pasture below this returning vegetation belt, chosen for its position roughly midway along the descent toward Vashisht and for the small stream that runs along its lower edge. The site offers one of the trek's better sunset vantage points, with the Pir Panjal's western ridges catching the day's last light in shifting shades of pink and gold. Evenings here tend to be more relaxed than the previous two nights, altitude-related fatigue easing noticeably now that the group has dropped below 13,000 feet, and conversation often turns toward comparing notes on the trek's varied terrain, from Gulaba's gentle pastures to the stark grandeur of Bhrigu itself. A final briefing on the next day's descent into Vashisht closes out the evening around the fire.
highlights: Ridge traverse skipping the standard return route
Shepherd cairns marking traditional grazing paths
Return of birch and juniper scrub on the lower slopes
Sunset views over the Pir Panjal's western ridges from Khanul
tips: Use trekking poles on the boulder sections, footing here is less even than it looks
This camp tends to be milder than Rola Kholi, but still layer up after sunset
Charge devices fully tonight if relying on solar power banks, tomorrow is the exit day

itinerary.title: Khanul Descent to Vashisht and Trek Conclusion
itinerary.trailType: forested descent, terraced village fields, stone village lanes
itinerary.description: The last day is almost entirely downhill, and it delivers one of the more dramatic vegetation changes on the whole route, the trail dropping from Khanul's open pasture into thickening birch, then oak, and finally deodar forest as the village of Vashisht draws nearer with every switchback. The path is well trodden by local herders and villagers, and stone-lined sections appear increasingly often the lower the trail descends, hinting at the settled, terraced farmland waiting below. Apple orchards begin to punctuate the forest edge in the last hour or two, their neat rows a clear sign of returning civilisation after three days of comparative wilderness, and the sound of the Beas river, distant but growing steadily louder, becomes a constant undertone. Vashisht itself is a village with genuine antiquity, its stone Rama and Shiva temples dating back centuries and its natural hot sulphur springs drawing visitors from well beyond Himachal, and there is a particular satisfaction in easing tired legs into the warm communal baths shortly after finishing the descent. Narrow lanes lined with small shops selling shawls, tea, and mountain snacks give the village a lived-in, slightly bohemian character quite different from the pastoral solitude of the previous days. Guides typically use the walk's final stretch to recap the trek's arc, from Gulaba's gentle meadows to Bhrigu's sacred, unfrozen lake and the quieter ridge traverse that followed, framing Vashisht's warm springs as a fitting, well-earned close. From here it is a short onward journey into Manali proper, and the trek formally concludes with that easy, contented tiredness that comes from four days spent almost entirely above the noise of ordinary life.
highlights: Dramatic forest transition from birch to deodar on the descent
Vashisht's ancient stone temples
Natural hot sulphur springs at trek's end
Apple orchards signalling the return to valley life
tips: Save time and energy for the Vashisht hot springs, they are the perfect end to this trek
Keep small change handy for the village's tea stalls and shops
Confirm your Manali transfer or accommodation before starting the final descent

fitness.description: Short itinerary, but altitude is real. Comfortable with 5–6 hours of incline walking.
tips: Do hill repeats twice a week
Arrive in Manali a day early if possible
Hydrate aggressively on Day 1–2
Avoid overpacking your daypack

map.overview: Gulaba → Rola Kholi meadows → Bhrigu Lake → Vashisht descent.

title: Beas Kund Trek
summary: Walk to the glacial source of the Beas River beneath Friendship Peak — ideal for first-time high-altitude trekkers.
overview: Walk to the glacial source of the Beas River beneath Friendship Peak — ideal for first-time high-altitude trekkers.
highlights: Scenic Manali trailhead access
3D/2N guided itinerary
easy difficulty with local support
Meals and camping/homestay as per plan
Experienced mountain guides

itinerary.title: Manali via Dhundi to Bakarthach Camp
itinerary.trailType: riverside gravel track, birch grove, open moraine flat
itinerary.description: The short drive from Manali to Dhundi runs along the Solang road past ski lifts and paragliding launch points before the tarmac ends at a scatter of tea shacks marking the trailhead. From Dhundi, the trail follows the young Beas river upstream, its water here still narrow and fast, tumbling over rounded stones in a corridor lined with birch and the occasional stand of willow. The path is gentle underfoot, a wide, well-used track shaped by decades of shepherd and mule traffic moving supplies toward the higher grazing grounds, and progress feels easy even for those trekking at altitude for the first time. Snow bridges occasionally span the river in early season, a reminder of how much winter accumulation still lingers this close to the Rohtang corridor even as summer settles over the lower valley. As the trail gains height, the birch thins into scattered juniper and finally bare moraine, and the valley widens into a broad, stony flat where the river braids into several channels across gravel bars. Bakarthach, the day's camp, takes its name from the Gaddi shepherds who have long grazed goats here through the summer months, and their temporary stone shelters are still visible scattered across the flat. Views from camp stretch up-valley toward the rockier terrain that tomorrow's walk to Beas Kund will cross, while behind, the trail back down toward Dhundi and the Solang valley remains visible for much of the afternoon. Being one of the more accessible high-altitude camps near Manali, Bakarthach can see a fair number of day-trekkers passing through, lending the site a sociable atmosphere in contrast to more remote camps elsewhere in the region. Dinner is eaten as the last light fades from the surrounding ridgelines, and the guide outlines the following day's walk to the lake and back.
highlights: Gentle riverside walk beside the infant Beas river
Early-season snow bridges near Dhundi
Gaddi shepherd grazing grounds at Bakarthach
Wide moraine flat camp with valley views
tips: This route sees plenty of day visitors, start early to enjoy quieter trail sections
Watch your footing on the gravel bars near the river, stones shift underfoot
Keep an extra layer accessible, wind picks up on this open flat by late afternoon

itinerary.title: Bakarthach to Beas Kund and Back to Camp
itinerary.trailType: boulder and moraine trail, glacial approach, alpine lake basin
itinerary.description: The walk to Beas Kund climbs through increasingly rugged terrain, the trail picking a line across glacial moraine and scattered boulder fields left behind by the retreating ice that once filled this entire upper valley. Underfoot, loose rock and sand alternate with firmer stretches of packed scree, and the pace naturally slows as the group gains altitude, the thinning air making even moderate gradients feel more demanding than they would lower down. The valley narrows as it climbs, framed by the steep faces of Hanuman Tibba and Friendship Peak, both popular objectives for mountaineering expeditions based out of Manali, and their presence lends the walk a distinctly mountaineering atmosphere rather than a purely pastoral one. Beas Kund itself is a modest but striking glacial lake tucked directly beneath these peaks, considered by Hindu tradition to be the source of the Beas river and named for the sage Vyasa, who is said to have meditated at this very spot. The water sits an intense, cold blue-green, fed directly by snowmelt and small hanging glaciers on the surrounding cliffs, and chunks of old ice occasionally float near its shaded edges even in high summer. Trekkers typically spend a generous stretch of time here, absorbing the scale of the amphitheatre of rock and ice that encircles the lake, before beginning the return journey along the same route. The descent moves considerably faster than the climb, though loose scree sections still demand attention, and reaching Bakarthach again by mid-afternoon leaves ample time to rest before dinner. This out-and-back structure, distinct from routes that continue onward past the lake, gives Beas Kund its particular character as an intense but compact high-altitude objective completed in a single demanding day from camp.
highlights: Reaching the sacred, glacier-fed Beas Kund at over 12,000 ft
Close views of Hanuman Tibba and Friendship Peak
Mythological association with the sage Vyasa and the Beas river's source
Old glacial ice visible near the lake's shaded margins
tips: This is a long, technical day for a two-camp trek, pace yourself on the moraine sections
Sunglasses and sun protection are essential given the exposed, reflective terrain
Carry extra snacks, appetite often dips at this altitude but energy needs stay high

itinerary.title: Bakarthach to Dhundi and Drive to Manali
itinerary.trailType: riverside gravel track, birch grove descent, roadhead trail
itinerary.description: The final morning retraces yesterday's outbound path back down toward Dhundi, though the walk feels markedly different in reverse, with the Beas river now on the opposite side and views opening toward the Solang valley's ski slopes rather than the peaks framing Beas Kund. Descending is quick and easy after two days of harder climbing, the birch groves near the lower stretch offering welcome shade as the morning sun strengthens, and the sound of the river grows steadily louder as the trail loses height. Marmots and occasional Himalayan pika dart between rocks near the trail's edge, more visible now that the group moves at a relaxed pace rather than the concentrated effort of the previous day's lake approach. Dhundi appears with its familiar cluster of tea shacks well before midday, and the transition from mountain quiet to roadside bustle, with taxis, day-trip groups, and the occasional grazing pony, feels abrupt after two nights spent almost entirely alone on the moraine flats above. From Dhundi, a short drive winds back down through Solang, past its cable car towers and zip-line rigs that stand oddly at odds with the wilder terrain just an hour's walk away, before rejoining the main road into Manali. Guides often use this drive to answer final questions about the peaks and features seen from Beas Kund, and to point out landmarks along the Solang valley that connect to other trekking and mountaineering routes in the area. Arriving back in Manali by early afternoon brings the trek to a close, its three days having packed in a genuinely dramatic arc from riverside forest to a sacred, glacier-rimmed lake and back, all within easy striking distance of the town itself.
highlights: Quick, easy descent through birch groves back to Dhundi
Marmot and pika sightings along the lower trail
Contrast between Solang's tourist infrastructure and the wild upper valley
Trek conclusion with an early afternoon return to Manali
tips: Keep your camera ready on the descent, wildlife sightings are more frequent at this relaxed pace
Confirm your Manali drop-off point with the operator before setting off
Grab lunch at Solang if time allows, it is one of the last easy food stops before town

title: Patalsu Peak Trek
summary: 2D/1N Patalsu Peak Trek from Manali, Himachal Pradesh — guided Himalayan experience with India Holiday Destinations.
overview: Patalsu Peak Trek is a carefully curated Himalayan trek based around Manali in Himachal Pradesh. Rated moderate with a 2D/1N itinerary, this journey balances scenic walking, safe camping or homestays, and memorable mountain views. India Holiday Destinations handles permits, experienced local guides, meals on trek, and logistics so you can focus on the trail.
highlights: Scenic Manali trailhead access
2D/1N guided itinerary
moderate difficulty with local support
Meals and camping/homestay as per plan
Experienced mountain guides

itinerary.title: Solang to Patalsu Base Camp
itinerary.trailType: pine forest switchbacks, open ridge meadow, rocky approach slope
itinerary.description: The climb toward Patalsu begins right from the edge of Solang's ski slopes, the trail cutting away from the paragliding fields and rope-tow towers into a quieter forest of pine and horse chestnut that quickly muffles the noise of the valley below. Switchbacks climb steadily through this belt, the gradient firm but manageable, and gaps in the canopy offer intermittent views back over Solang's broad meadow, easily recognisable by the cluster of cafes and adventure-sport rigs dotting its floor. As the forest thins, the trail breaks onto an open ridge shoulder where grass replaces tree cover almost entirely, and the walking becomes noticeably more exposed to both sun and wind, with views expanding rapidly in every direction. This ridge line is used seasonally by shepherds moving flocks up from the Solang side, and stone livestock shelters mark the route at intervals, offering a rough gauge of progress even without a map. Patalsu Base Camp sits on a grassy shelf just below the peak's final rocky approach, chosen for its flat ground and relative shelter from the wind that sweeps more forcefully across the exposed summit ridge above. From camp, the views are already substantial, taking in the Solang valley's full length and a first close look at the folded ridgelines separating Kullu from the Lahaul side of the range. The final stretch of trail up to the summit, visible from camp as a rocky spine above the meadow, is pointed out in detail during the evening briefing, along with realistic timing expectations for tomorrow's early start. Temperatures fall quickly once the sun drops behind the ridge, and most trekkers retreat to their tents soon after dinner, keen to bank as much rest as possible before the demanding summit push ahead.
highlights: Forest switchbacks rising directly from Solang's ski slopes
Open ridge meadow with expanding valley views
Shepherd shelters marking the seasonal grazing route
Base camp views toward the Lahaul-facing ridgelines
tips: Break in your boots before this trek, the summit day involves sustained rocky ground
Fill water bottles at the last reliable stream before the ridge opens up
Sleep early tonight, tomorrow's summit push starts well before sunrise

itinerary.title: Patalsu Peak Summit and Descent to Solang
itinerary.trailType: rocky summit ridge, loose scree, long forested descent
itinerary.description: The summit push begins in darkness, headlamps picking out the trail as it climbs away from camp along an increasingly rocky spine, the gradient steepening noticeably as the final stretch to Patalsu's top comes into view against a lightening sky. Loose rock and scree make careful footing essential through this section, and the thinning air slows pace even on relatively short pitches, the last few hundred feet feeling considerably more strenuous than the map distance suggests. Reaching the summit at just under 14,000 feet rewards the effort with one of the most complete panoramas available on a short trek from Manali: the Solang and Kullu valleys spread out to the south, the Pir Panjal's jagged crest running along the northern horizon, and on exceptionally clear mornings, distant glimpses of peaks toward the Spiti and Lahaul border. Many trekkers linger here longer than planned, reluctant to leave a viewpoint that took real effort to reach, though guides keep a close watch on the time given how long the subsequent descent will take. The way down initially retraces the ascent route back to base camp, but from there the trail diverges, dropping through the ridge meadows and then back into the pine forest via a slightly different line that eventually rejoins the Solang valley floor near its cluster of cafes and adventure-sport operators. This descent is long, covering nearly five and a half thousand feet of elevation loss in a single push, and legs already tired from the early summit effort are tested hard on the lower switchbacks. Arriving back in Solang by afternoon, amid paragliders circling overhead and the general bustle of the valley's tourist infrastructure, provides a strangely fitting, almost jarring contrast to the solitude of the summit just hours earlier, and marks a satisfying, if leg-weary, conclusion to a short but genuinely demanding two-day climb.
highlights: Summiting Patalsu Peak at nearly 14,000 ft
Panoramic views spanning the Pir Panjal and distant Spiti-facing ranges
Long, varied descent through ridge meadow and pine forest
Return to Solang's lively valley floor by afternoon
tips: Start the summit push well before dawn to summit before winds and cloud build up
Trekking poles significantly ease the long descent on tired knees
Carry more water than you think you need, there are no reliable sources above base camp

fitness.description: Prepare with regular walks and stair practice before Patalsu Peak Trek.
tips: Walk 4-5 km daily before the trek
Hydrate well
Break in your shoes
