title: Buran Ghati Trek
summary: 7D/6N Buran Ghati Trek from Janglik, Himachal Pradesh — guided Himalayan experience with India Holiday Destinations.
overview: Buran Ghati Trek is a carefully curated Himalayan trek based around Janglik in Himachal Pradesh. Rated moderate with a 7D/6N itinerary, this journey balances scenic walking, safe camping or homestays, and memorable mountain views. India Holiday Destinations handles permits, experienced local guides, meals on trek, and logistics so you can focus on the trail.
highlights: Scenic Janglik trailhead access
7D/6N guided itinerary
moderate difficulty with local support
Meals and camping/homestay as per plan
Experienced mountain guides

itinerary.title: Rohru to Janglik, Gateway to the Ghati
itinerary.trailType: motorable road drive, short forest approach walk
itinerary.description: The Buran Ghati crossing begins with a drive from Rohru through the apple belt of the upper Pabbar valley, the road climbing steadily past Chirgaon and Kupdi before narrowing into a rough mountain track that ends at Janglik, the last motorable village before the trail proper takes over. Janglik sits on a sunny shelf above the Pabbar's Rupin tributary catchment, its slate-roofed houses and terraced potato fields backed by a wall of forested ridges that hide, for now, any hint of the ice and rock waiting five days ahead. This is deliberately an easy first day, built around arrival, gear checks, and acclimatisation rather than distance, and the short evening walk through the village gives a first taste of the Jaunsari-influenced culture found in this corner of Shimla district, distinct from the Kullu customs on the far side of the pass. Village elders are often willing to talk about the old trade movement across Buran Ghati, historically used by traders and shepherds moving between the Pabbar and Sutlej systems long before it became known as a trekking route, and their accounts of the ice wall lower down the pass, still forming and shifting slightly each season, set a tone of respectful anticipation for what lies ahead. Local guides based in Janglik, many from families that have crossed the ghati for generations, join the group here, and their knowledge of current snow and ice conditions is treated as the final word on whether or how the pass will be attempted this season. Dinner is simple and hearty, and the evening briefing covers the week's itinerary in detail, including the group's fitness expectations and the technical rope and ice-wall system that will be used near the top. Stars over Janglik, undimmed by any significant light pollution, offer a fitting, quiet start to a trek that builds in intensity every single day from here.
highlights: Drive through the Pabbar valley's apple belt
Jaunsari village culture in Janglik
Local guides join with generations of ghati crossing experience
Final gear and acclimatisation checks before the climb
tips: Arrive with all trekking gear already broken in; there is no shop for replacements beyond Janglik
Ask local guides about current ice-wall conditions on Buran Ghati before committing to the full itinerary
Sleep early tonight, since the days ahead only get longer

itinerary.title: Janglik to Dayara through Ridge Forest
itinerary.trailType: pine-oak forest ridge, terraced village fields, open pasture
itinerary.description: Leaving Janglik, the trail climbs immediately into a mixed forest of pine and oak, the gradient easing occasionally onto short stretches of open ridge that offer the first real views back down the Pabbar valley toward Chirgaon. Underfoot, the path is a mix of packed earth and exposed rock, well used by shepherd families who graze sheep and goats on the high pastures above Janglik through the summer months, and their temporary stone shelters appear at intervals along the route. As the forest thins near the day's midpoint, the trail crosses a series of small streams fed by snowmelt still lingering in shaded gullies even in peak season, and these crossings, while straightforward, are a useful early test of balance and footwear grip before the trickier ground higher up. Dayara itself is a broad grazing meadow tucked against a forested slope, used seasonally by Gaddi and local shepherd communities, and it offers the first unobstructed view of the ridgeline that the trek will follow over the coming days toward Chandranahan. Wildflowers carpet sections of the meadow in the early summer window, and the campsite's relative shelter from wind makes it a comfortable, if increasingly cool, place to spend the night. The guide typically uses the evening to explain how the terrain will change dramatically over the next three days, from forest to alpine meadow to the stark glacial basin around Chandranahan lakes, and reinforces the pacing strategy needed to arrive at the pass in good condition. Campfire conversation often turns to the ice chute that gives Buran Ghati its regional fame, with guides sharing firsthand accounts of past crossings, some easy, some considerably more demanding depending on that year's snow accumulation and how the ice wall has formed.
highlights: Pine and oak forest ridge walk
Shepherd meadows and seasonal grazing shelters
First views of the Chandranahan ridgeline
Early wildflower meadows at Dayara
tips: Fill water bottles at the stream crossings, as sources thin out considerably above Dayara
Pace yourself deliberately today to save energy for the steeper days ahead
Keep an eye out for shepherd dogs near grazing camps and give them space

itinerary.title: Dayara to Litham, Into the High Valley
itinerary.trailType: birch and rhododendron forest, riverside meadow trail
itinerary.description: The walk from Dayara to Litham marks the point where the trek properly leaves the forested lower valley behind, the trail climbing through a narrowing corridor of birch and rhododendron before breaking into open, rockier terrain by the early afternoon. The Pabbar's headwater stream stays close to the route for much of the morning, its water turning progressively colder and more turbid with glacial sediment the higher the trail climbs, and several log and stone crossings punctuate the walk, requiring careful footing especially if recent rain has swollen the flow. Rhododendron blooms spectacularly here in late spring, turning entire hillsides shades of red and pink, though even outside bloom season the twisted, lichen-covered branches give the forest a distinctly ancient character. As the trail gains height, views begin opening toward the high ridgeline that separates the Pabbar and Rupin systems, and the scale of the terrain ahead becomes genuinely apparent for the first time, with jagged rock spires visible at the valley's head. Litham itself is a stark, beautiful high camp set on a grassy shelf beside the river, ringed by steep slopes that funnel cold air down from the peaks above as evening approaches, and the temperature drop after sunset here is sharp and immediate compared to the previous two nights. Marmots are commonly spotted darting between boulders near camp, and their sharp warning calls often provide the evening's background soundtrack as the group settles in. The guide typically dedicates part of the evening briefing to acclimatisation signs to watch for over the coming two days, since the jump in altitude from here to Chandranahan and eventually the pass itself is where most physical strain on this trek concentrates, and early recognition of fatigue or headache is treated seriously by the entire team.
highlights: Rhododendron and birch forest corridor
First close views of the ghati's headwall ridge
Litham's dramatic, wind-funneled high camp
Marmot sightings near the campsite
tips: Cross river sections carefully, using poles for stability on slick stones
Report any early altitude symptoms to your guide without delay
Layer up well before dinner, as the temperature drop at Litham is sudden

itinerary.title: Litham to Chandranahan Lakes
itinerary.trailType: moraine and boulder trail, glacial lake basin, high alpine scree
itinerary.description: Today's climb takes the group into genuinely high-altitude terrain, the trail leaving the last scattered vegetation behind within the first hour and continuing over a landscape of bare rock, moraine, and scattered snow patches that persist through much of the trekking season. The route follows the valley's natural drainage upward, tracing a route used for generations by shepherds moving flocks toward the high pastures below Chandranahan, though few animals venture quite this high, and the trail becomes noticeably quieter and more solitary as a result. Views expand dramatically with every few hundred metres of ascent, and by the time the first of the Chandranahan lakes comes into sight, cupped in a stark glacial bowl below the pass's looming rock and ice headwall, the sense of having entered a different, harsher world than the forested valley below is unmistakable. Chandranahan is in fact a cluster of several small lakes at varying levels within the same basin, their waters an intense, almost unnatural turquoise on clear days, fed directly by snowmelt and small hanging glaciers on the surrounding cliffs. The camp here is set on a relatively flat patch of ground near the largest lake, chosen for proximity to water though offering little shelter from the wind that regularly sweeps down off the pass. This is also the last camp before the technical crossing, and the afternoon is used deliberately for rest, gear checks, and a detailed walkthrough of tomorrow's ice wall and rappel section, with the local guides explaining the rope systems and safety protocols that will be used. Altitude here is high enough that appetite often dips and sleep can feel lighter than usual, both normal responses that the guide monitors closely. As dusk falls, the ghati's headwall catches the day's last light in shades of deep orange and violet, a striking, slightly intimidating preview of the following morning's challenge.
highlights: The turquoise Chandranahan lake cluster
Final camp beneath the pass's ice and rock headwall
Detailed briefing on the ice-wall rappel technique
Dramatic evening light over the ghati
tips: Eat well even if appetite is reduced; your body needs fuel for tomorrow's crossing
Attend the full technical briefing and ask questions if any part of the rope system is unclear
Keep your down jacket accessible tonight, as camp temperatures fall well below freezing

itinerary.title: Crossing Buran Ghati Pass via the Ice Wall
itinerary.trailType: glacier approach, ice wall rappel, steep scree descent
itinerary.description: This is the day the entire trek has been building toward, and the group departs Chandranahan in the cold, still dark of pre-dawn to take advantage of firm snow conditions before the sun softens the upper slopes. The climb to the pass itself, at roughly 15,000 feet, moves over a mix of scree, old snowfields, and increasingly steep terrain, the thinning air making every step feel more deliberate than the distance alone would suggest. Reaching the top of Buran Ghati delivers one of the most dramatic viewpoints on this side of the Himachal Himalaya, with the Pabbar valley's forested folds receding behind and the very different, more rugged terrain of the upper Rupin catchment opening ahead. What follows is the trek's signature feature and its most demanding technical section: a near-vertical ice wall on the Kullu-facing side of the pass, formed anew each season by accumulated snow and glacial ice, which trekkers descend using a fixed rope and rappel technique under close guide supervision. The sensation of easing backward over the lip of the ice chute, harness locked into the rope system, is genuinely unlike anything else on a standard Himachal trek, and even those who have done the crossing before describe a rush of adrenaline at this exact moment. Below the ice wall, the terrain relaxes into steep but manageable scree and snow slopes, and the descent continues for several more hours through increasingly softer ground as the trail drops toward Dhunda Thach. The change in scenery on this side is immediate, with hardier alpine vegetation reappearing within an hour or two of leaving the ice behind, a small but welcome sign of returning to gentler terrain after the morning's intensity. Camp at Dhunda Thach, reached late in the afternoon after a genuinely full day, is a relief in every sense, and the evening's conversation inevitably circles back to the ice wall itself, retold and re-examined by every member of the group with the particular satisfaction that comes from having completed one of Himachal's most talked-about pass crossings.
highlights: Crossing Buran Ghati Pass at approximately 15,000 ft
The signature ice wall rappel descent
Dramatic shift from Pabbar to Rupin-side terrain
Return of alpine vegetation below the ice wall
tips: This is a full expedition day; start well hydrated and carry high-energy snacks
Trust the rope system and your guide's instructions fully during the rappel; do not rush the technique
Photograph the ice wall from a safe stance only, after your own descent is complete

itinerary.title: Dhunda Thach to Barua Village
itinerary.trailType: forested gorge descent, shepherd trail, terraced village approach
itinerary.description: The descent from Dhunda Thach continues the dramatic change in landscape that began at the ice wall, the trail dropping through increasingly green terrain as birch, then pine, and finally deodar forest reclaim the slopes on this Kullu-facing side of the range. The gradient is steady rather than gentle, and legs already tired from the previous day's technical crossing are tested again on the long series of switchbacks that carry the trail down toward the Rupin's tributary streams. Shepherd camps become more frequent as altitude is lost, their flocks spread across hillside pastures in numbers that swell noticeably compared to the sparser grazing seen on the Pabbar side, a reflection of the more accessible, gentler terrain here. Barua village, the day's destination, sits on a terraced shelf above the river, its timber-and-stone houses arranged in the characteristic style of upper Kullu hill villages, distinct in several details from the Jaunsari architecture seen at the trek's start in Janglik. Reaching Barua carries real weight after five demanding days, and villagers here, familiar with the occasional trekking group emerging from the ghati, are typically warm and curious, often offering tea or a place to dry out gear that has been damp since the crossing. A homestay or simple guesthouse room replaces the tent for the first time since Janglik, and the basic comforts of a real roof and a hot meal cooked indoors feel disproportionately luxurious after a week of camping. The evening is spent in relaxed reflection, comparing the wildly different characters of the trek's two halves, from Pabbar valley forest to Rupin-side descent, joined by a single unforgettable day above the ice wall.
highlights: Forest regeneration through birch, pine, and deodar on descent
Larger shepherd encampments on the Rupin-facing slopes
Barua village's distinct Kullu-style architecture
First indoor stay since the trek began
tips: Knees take real strain on this long descent; use poles and shorten your stride
Dry out any gear that got wet during yesterday's ice-wall crossing
Accept village hospitality graciously, and consider a small gesture of thanks

itinerary.title: Barua to Kupdi Roadhead and Onward
itinerary.trailType: village road, riverside path, motorable roadhead
itinerary.description: The final day is a shorter, gentler walk that eases the group back toward motorable road, following the river down through a widening valley where terraced fields of maize and beans announce the return of denser settlement after a week spent largely in wilderness. The trail passes through one or two smaller hamlets before reaching Kupdi, where jeeps typically wait to complete the journey back toward Rohru or onward connections deeper into Kullu, depending on the itinerary's planned exit. Along the way, guides often use the relaxed pace to answer lingering questions about the week's route, filling in details on peaks and ridgelines seen from various camps and clarifying exactly which sections of the ice wall varied most from previous seasons' crossings. The drive out, once vehicles are boarded, offers a final look back up the valley toward the high ridgeline the group crossed, its details already softening into a distant, slightly unreal-looking wall of rock and snow. Buran Ghati's reputation among Himachal's serious trekking community rests specifically on that one unforgettable morning at the ice wall, and by the time the group reaches Kupdi, there is a shared, quiet pride at having completed a crossing that combines classic Himalayan trekking with a genuinely technical, adrenaline-charged centrepiece. For many in the group, this trek stands apart from more straightforward pass crossings elsewhere in the region precisely because of that single, vivid morning, and conversations on the drive out tend to circle back to it more than any other single moment from the week.
highlights: Return through terraced farmland and small hamlets
Final views back toward the Buran Ghati headwall
Debrief on the week's ice-wall crossing
Trek conclusion at the Kupdi roadhead
tips: Confirm your onward vehicle arrangement the previous evening in Barua
Keep some cash for tea stops during the drive out
Back up or transfer trek photos as soon as you have signal, especially of the ice wall crossing

summary: 3D/2N Churdhar Peak Trek from Sirmaur, Himachal Pradesh — guided Himalayan experience with India Holiday Destinations.

Rated moderate with a 3D/2N itinerary, this journey balances scenic walking, safe camping or homestays, and memorable mountain views. India Holiday Destinations handles permits, experienced local guides, meals on trek, and logistics so you can focus on the trail.
highlights: Scenic Sirmaur trailhead access
3D/2N guided itinerary
moderate difficulty with local support
Meals and camping/homestay as per plan
Experienced mountain guides

itinerary.title: Nohradhar to Taevi Dhar through Oak-Rhododendron Forest
itinerary.trailType: oak and rhododendron forest switchbacks, mule trail
itinerary.description: The Churdhar climb begins at Nohradhar, a small roadhead town in the Sirmaur hills that serves as the traditional starting point for this pilgrimage-trek to Himachal's highest point in the district. From the very first steps, the trail enters a dense forest of oak and rhododendron, the canopy thick enough to filter sunlight into dappled patches even at midday, and the path itself, worn smooth by generations of pilgrim and shepherd feet, climbs in a series of manageable switchbacks rather than any single punishing gradient. Langurs are a near-constant presence in the upper canopy here, their movement through the branches often the first sign of wildlife before any visual confirmation, and the forest floor is thick with fallen oak leaves that give the ground a soft, springy quality underfoot. As height is gained, oak gradually cedes ground to denser stands of rhododendron, spectacular with bloom in the late spring window, their gnarled, moss-covered trunks lending the forest an almost fairy-tale atmosphere on misty mornings, which are common in this part of Sirmaur even in otherwise clear weather. Taevi Dhar, the day's destination, is a forest clearing used as the standard midway camp for the Churdhar pilgrimage, offering the first real views back down toward the Sirmaur valley and, on clear evenings, distant glimpses of the plains beyond the hills. The camp has a basic forest rest house alongside space for tents, and pilgrims making the same journey, often in small family groups, are commonly encountered here, lending the evening a sociable, devotional atmosphere distinct from purely recreational treks elsewhere in Himachal. Dinner conversation typically turns to the temple awaiting at the summit and the local reverence for Churdhar as one of Sirmaur's most sacred peaks.
highlights: Dense oak and rhododendron forest ascent
Frequent langur sightings through the canopy
Taevi Dhar's forest clearing camp
Shared trail with Sirmauri pilgrims
tips: Start early to make the most of cool morning temperatures under forest cover
Carry a raincover, as this forest belt sees frequent afternoon mist and light rain
Respect pilgrim groups on the trail and let them pass at their own pace where the path narrows

itinerary.title: Taevi Dhar to Churdhar Summit and the Shirgul Temple
itinerary.trailType: alpine ridge, rocky summit approach, oak forest descent
itinerary.description: The second day delivers the trek's central purpose, climbing steadily out of the forest belt and onto Churdhar's increasingly open upper slopes, where the vegetation thins into alpine scrub and finally bare rock as the ridge narrows toward the summit. At just under 12,000 feet, Churdhar stands as the highest point in Sirmaur district, and the final approach rewards the climb with sweeping views that, on a clear day, extend toward the snow-capped ranges of inner Himachal to the north and the hazy expanse of the Shivalik foothills and plains to the south. The summit itself is home to the temple of Shirgul Devta, regarded locally as one of the most powerful deities in this part of the state, and the shrine, though modest in physical scale, draws a steady stream of devotees throughout the trekking season who undertake the climb specifically for darshan rather than recreation. According to local belief, the deity's blessing extends particularly to matters of justice and disputes, and pilgrims often share stories of resolved conflicts attributed to a visit here. After spending time at the temple, absorbing both the panoramic views and the unmistakably devotional atmosphere of the summit, the group begins a descent that trades the steep upper approach for a gentler forested route down to Dev Ghati, a quieter camp lower on the mountain's far flank, chosen to shorten the following day's walk back to Nohradhar. This descent passes through forest noticeably different in character from the ascent route, with more fir mixed among the oak, and the shift in camp location for the night gives trekkers a broader sense of Churdhar's full circumference rather than a simple there-and-back walk. Evening at Dev Ghati is peaceful, with clear skies here often affording an exceptional view of stars away from any settlement light.
highlights: Summit of Churdhar at nearly 12,000 ft, Sirmaur's highest point
Shirgul Devta temple and its local devotional significance
Panoramic views spanning snow peaks to the Shivalik plains
Descent via a different forested route to Dev Ghati
tips: Reach the summit before midafternoon, as cloud cover often builds and can obscure the panoramic views
Observe temple etiquette quietly, since many visitors are here specifically for worship
Carry extra water, as the exposed summit stretch has no reliable source

itinerary.title: Dev Ghati to Nohradhar, Trek Conclusion
itinerary.trailType: forested ridge descent, village trail
itinerary.description: The final day is a comfortable descent through fir and oak forest, the trail dropping steadily as it rejoins the broader Sirmaur ridge system on its way back to Nohradhar. Birdlife is especially active in the cooler morning hours on this stretch, with sightings of Himalayan woodpeckers and the occasional pheasant adding interest to what is otherwise a straightforward walk down. The forest here carries a noticeably different character from the ascent route two days earlier, with older, taller trees suggesting sections that have seen less disturbance over the years, and guides often point out specific oak and rhododendron specimens estimated to be well over a century old. As the trail loses altitude, small clearings offer glimpses of Nohradhar and the terraced fields surrounding it, a welcome visual cue of how close the trek's end has come. Reaching Nohradhar by early afternoon allows time for a relaxed closing lunch at one of the small roadside dhabas that serve trekkers and pilgrims alike, and conversation typically turns to reflections on the deeply devotional character of Churdhar compared to more purely scenic Himachal treks. The Shirgul Devta temple's significance to local communities, and the palpable reverence shown by pilgrims encountered throughout the three days, leaves a distinct impression that sets this short trek apart from routes built solely around scenery. From Nohradhar, onward travel typically continues toward Shimla or Rajgarh, and the drive out offers a final look back at Churdhar's forested slopes, still faintly visible against the Sirmaur skyline. Most who complete this short pilgrimage-trek describe it as a genuinely different experience from Himachal's more purely scenic routes, one that lingers in memory well beyond its modest three-day length.
highlights: Descent through old-growth oak and rhododendron forest
Birdwatching opportunities on the quieter lower trail
Closing lunch in Nohradhar
Reflection on Churdhar's devotional significance to Sirmaur communities
tips: Keep camera ready for birdlife, most active in the early morning descent
Try the local dhaba food in Nohradhar before departing
Book onward transport toward Shimla or Rajgarh a day in advance if possible

fitness.description: Prepare with regular walks and stair practice before Churdhar Peak Trek.
tips: Walk 4-5 km daily before the trek
Hydrate well
Break in your shoes

faqs.answer: It is graded moderate. Our guides pace the group and brief you daily.

title: Shrikhand Mahadev Trek
summary: 5D/4N Shrikhand Mahadev Trek from Kullu, Himachal Pradesh — guided Himalayan experience with India Holiday Destinations.
overview: Shrikhand Mahadev Trek is a carefully curated Himalayan trek based around Kullu in Himachal Pradesh. Rated difficult with a 5D/4N itinerary, this journey balances scenic walking, safe camping or homestays, and memorable mountain views. India Holiday Destinations handles permits, experienced local guides, meals on trek, and logistics so you can focus on the trail.
highlights: Scenic Kullu trailhead access
5D/4N guided itinerary
difficult difficulty with local support
Meals and camping/homestay as per plan
Experienced mountain guides

itinerary.title: Jaon to Thachru through Deodar Forest
itinerary.trailType: dense deodar forest, riverside path, devotee langar camps
itinerary.description: The pilgrimage to Shrikhand Mahadev begins in Jaon, a village on the Kullu-Shimla border reached by road from Rampur or Nirmand, where devotees and trekkers alike register at the base camp office before setting out on one of Himachal's most physically demanding sacred routes. The trail climbs immediately through dense deodar forest, the path underfoot a mix of packed earth and exposed root, and langar stalls set up by devotee trusts appear at surprisingly frequent intervals, offering tea, khichdi, and encouragement to the steady stream of pilgrims making this same journey in devotion to Lord Shiva. The route follows a stream for much of the way, its water cold and fast even this low in the valley, and the forest canopy provides welcome shade through the day's warmer hours. As height is gained, the trail steepens noticeably, and the crowd of pilgrims thins slightly as the more committed continue while day visitors from lower camps turn back. Thachru, the first major camp, sits on a forested shelf beside the stream, its langar tents and makeshift shelters forming a temporary township each pilgrimage season, typically running through July and August depending on weather and administrative permissions. The atmosphere here is intensely communal, with pilgrims from across northern India sharing food, stories, and the particular camaraderie that comes from undertaking a genuinely difficult sacred journey together. Guides usually spend the evening explaining the physical demands of the days ahead, emphasising that Shrikhand Mahadev's reputation as one of India's most arduous pilgrimages is well earned, with steep, exposed terrain and rapid altitude gain distinguishing it clearly from gentler Himachal routes.
highlights: Dense deodar forest with frequent devotee-run langars
Shared trail with pilgrims from across northern India
Thachru's communal pilgrimage camp atmosphere
Briefing on the demanding days ahead
tips: Accept langar food and tea, but consider a donation if you're able
Pace yourself deliberately from day one; this pilgrimage punishes those who rush early
Register properly at the Jaon base camp office before starting

itinerary.title: Thachru to Parkachi Thach via Kali Ghati
itinerary.trailType: exposed ridge crossing, alpine meadow, glacier-fed stream crossings
itinerary.description: The second day increases in both altitude and exposure, the trail climbing out of the forest belt and through Kali Ghati, a steep, narrow section of the route notorious among regular pilgrims for its combination of loose rock and sheer drop, requiring careful, deliberate footwork rather than speed. Beyond Kali Ghati, the terrain opens into alpine meadow, and the character of the walk shifts entirely, with wide views back down the valley and, ahead, the first genuine glimpses of the high, rocky terrain that surrounds the eventual pinnacle. Glacier-fed streams cross the trail at several points, their crossings occasionally requiring pilgrims to link hands or use fixed ropes installed by the administration during peak season, a small but telling indicator of how seriously the route's difficulty is treated by those who manage it. Wildflowers bloom in dense clusters across the meadow sections during the brief summer window, a striking contrast to the barer, rockier ground that begins to dominate as the trail gains further height. Parkachi Thach, the day's destination, sits in a high bowl ringed by increasingly stark peaks, and the drop in temperature compared to Thachru is immediately noticeable, with warm layers needed well before sunset. The camp buzzes with a similar pilgrim energy to the previous night, though the crowd here skews toward those specifically committed to reaching the pinnacle, since Kali Ghati filters out a portion of less prepared visitors. Evening conversation among pilgrims and trekkers alike often centres on personal reasons for undertaking this specific, uniquely challenging pilgrimage, with many describing years of preparation or long-held devotional intent, a depth of motivation that gives Shrikhand Mahadev an atmosphere distinct from almost any other high-altitude route in Himachal.
highlights: Crossing the notoriously exposed Kali Ghati section
Alpine meadows with dense summer wildflower bloom
Multiple glacier-fed stream crossings
Parkachi Thach's high-altitude pilgrim camp
tips: Take Kali Ghati slowly and use any fixed ropes provided; this is not a section to rush
Layer up before dusk at Parkachi Thach, as the temperature drop is sudden and significant
Talk to fellow pilgrims; the shared motivation on this route is part of its unique character

itinerary.title: Parkachi Thach to Bhim Dwar, the High Camp
itinerary.trailType: boulder field, moraine trail, sacred high-altitude lake
itinerary.description: The climb to Bhim Dwar, the final camp before the pinnacle itself, moves through terrain that grows starker and more demanding with every hour, the trail crossing boulder fields and old moraine deposited by the glaciers that still cling to the upper reaches of this range. Vegetation all but disappears by the day's midpoint, replaced by bare rock and, depending on the season, lingering patches of snow that can make certain stretches slow going even for experienced trekkers. Bhim Dwar takes its name from a local legend associating the site with Bhima, one of the Pandava brothers of the Mahabharata, said to have rested here during his own mythical mountain travels, and the name lends an additional layer of storied significance to what is already among the most physically taxing pilgrim camps in the Himalaya. The camp sits beside Nain Sarovar, a small, intensely cold high-altitude lake regarded as sacred in its own right, and many pilgrims perform a brief ritual dip here despite the near-freezing water, in preparation for the final push to the pinnacle above. Altitude at Bhim Dwar is high enough that appetite and sleep are noticeably affected for most in the group, and the guide team monitors everyone closely through the afternoon and evening, since the summit attempt the following day represents the trek's most demanding single stretch. Dinner is eaten early, and most pilgrims retire soon after, aware that the following day begins in pre-dawn darkness in order to reach the pinnacle and return before afternoon weather, which builds with striking regularity in this exposed high basin, closes in.
highlights: Boulder and moraine terrain approaching Bhim Dwar
The mythologically significant Nain Sarovar lake
Ritual cold-water dip tradition among pilgrims
Final high camp before the pinnacle attempt
tips: Only attempt the ritual dip if medically fit for extreme cold water exposure
Eat as much as appetite allows tonight, even if reduced by altitude
Sleep early, as tomorrow's start is in the dark hours before dawn

itinerary.title: Bhim Dwar to the Shrikhand Mahadev Pinnacle and Back
itinerary.trailType: exposed rock scramble, snow patches, sheer pinnacle approach
itinerary.description: This is unquestionably the defining day of the pilgrimage, and the group departs Bhim Dwar in darkness, headlamps picking out a trail that quickly turns to rock scramble and exposed scree as it climbs toward the pinnacle visible, weather permitting, from far below as a distinctive rock spire piercing the skyline at roughly 17,000 feet. The final approach is genuinely severe, involving sustained exposure on narrow rock ledges and sections where hands are needed as much as feet, and the combination of thin air, cold, and technical terrain makes this one of the most demanding single days found on any organised pilgrimage route in India. Snow patches persist on shaded sections of the approach even in peak season, adding a further layer of caution to already careful footwork, and guides keep the group tightly managed, with pacing and rest breaks calculated specifically for this stretch. Reaching the base of the pinnacle itself, where the natural rock formation is worshipped as a manifestation of Shiva's lingam, is an intensely emotional moment for most pilgrims, many of whom have planned this specific journey for years, and the sight of prayer flags and offerings left by countless previous visitors underscores the site's deep spiritual weight. Weather at this altitude and exposure level can change with almost no warning, and the guide team watches cloud formation constantly, ready to turn the group back if conditions deteriorate, since afternoon storms here are common and genuinely dangerous given the terrain. After time spent at the pinnacle for prayer, photographs, and simple contemplation, the long return to Bhim Dwar begins, retracing the same exposed ground with legs and concentration both considerably more taxed than on the way up. Arrival back at camp in the afternoon, often to light rain or building cloud right on schedule, brings a profound sense of both physical and spiritual accomplishment, and the evening is spent quietly, most pilgrims too genuinely exhausted for much beyond a simple meal and early sleep.
highlights: Reaching the sacred Shrikhand Mahadev pinnacle at approximately 17,000 ft
Exposed rock scramble and scree approach
Deeply emotional pilgrim experience at the summit shrine
Careful weather-dependent timing to avoid afternoon storms
tips: This is the hardest day of the trek by far; conserve energy on the approach for the return journey
Turn back immediately if your guide calls off the attempt due to weather
Carry high-energy snacks, as appetite loss at this altitude is common but energy needs remain high

itinerary.title: Bhim Dwar to Jaon, the Long Descent
itinerary.trailType: steep descent through alpine meadow and forest, river crossings, village approach
itinerary.description: With the pinnacle successfully reached, the final day compresses the outward journey's three days of ascent into one long, continuous descent back to Jaon, dropping nearly ten thousand feet in total over boulder fields, alpine meadow, the notorious Kali Ghati section, and finally the deodar forest walked on day one. The pace is necessarily brisk, and while the physical demand is considerable, the psychological weight of the pilgrimage has shifted entirely by this point, replaced by the lighter, satisfied mood common among pilgrims who have completed one of India's most arduous devotional journeys. Familiar landmarks from the ascent appear in reverse order, and the langar stalls near Thachru and lower down are typically still active, offering a final round of tea and simple food to descending pilgrims, exchanged now with the particular warmth reserved for those who have made it to the top and back safely. Knees and legs take considerable strain on this long downhill push, and frequent short breaks help manage the cumulative fatigue, particularly through the loose, technical ground of Kali Ghati, which demands just as much care on the way down as it did on the way up. Reaching Jaon by evening, often after ten or more hours of continuous walking, marks the end of a pilgrimage widely regarded, even among experienced Himalayan trekkers, as one of the most physically and spiritually demanding routes in the region. The evening's mood at the base camp is celebratory but subdued, most pilgrims deeply tired but quietly transformed by an experience that combines raw physical challenge with a depth of devotional significance found in few other treks anywhere in the Himalaya.
highlights: A single long descent retracing the full ascent route
Final langar hospitality on the way down
Careful, deliberate footwork through Kali Ghati in reverse
Completion of one of India's most demanding pilgrimage treks
tips: Use trekking poles throughout to protect your knees on this exceptionally long descent day
Pace your water and snack intake, since the day's duration is considerably longer than earlier stages
Allow a full rest day after this trek before any further strenuous travel

fitness.description: Prepare with regular walks and stair practice before Shrikhand Mahadev Trek.
tips: Walk 4-5 km daily before the trek
Hydrate well
Break in your shoes

title: Friendship Peak Trek
summary: A guided mountaineering expedition near Manali combining glacier travel, ropework, and a stunning summit attempt.
overview: A guided mountaineering expedition near Manali combining glacier travel, ropework, and a stunning summit attempt.
highlights: Scenic Manali trailhead access
7D/6N guided itinerary
difficult difficulty with local support
Meals and camping/homestay as per plan
Experienced mountain guides

itinerary.title: Manali to Bakarthach, the Approach Begins
itinerary.trailType: forest trail, river crossing, alpine meadow camp
itinerary.description: The Friendship Peak expedition begins with a drive from Manali to Dhundi, the last motorable point on the Solang side of the valley, from where the trail sets off through a corridor of birch and fir toward Bakarthach, a broad meadow used as the standard first camp for expeditions heading into the Beas Kund basin. The path follows the Beas river's infant stream closely, crossing it on a simple wooden bridge partway through the walk, and the gradient stays gentle enough to serve as a genuine warm-up rather than a serious physical test, since the expedition's real difficulty lies several days ahead. Bakarthach itself sits on a grassy shelf with views already opening toward the jagged ridgeline that separates the Solang side from the high Beas Kund cirque, and the meadow is grazed seasonally by local shepherds whose flocks are a common sight through the summer trekking window. This is deliberately an easy introduction, and the guide team uses the relatively short walking day to conduct thorough equipment checks, since Friendship Peak, unlike most Himachal treks, involves genuine technical mountaineering gear including crampons, ice axes, and a climbing harness that every member of the expedition must be comfortable using by the time the summit attempt arrives. The evening briefing covers the expedition's full structure in detail, from the coming days at base camp through the skills refresher sessions to the summit push itself, and sets clear expectations that this is a mountaineering objective first and a trek second, with weather and snow conditions ultimately deciding the final outcome regardless of the group's fitness or preparation.
highlights: Gentle warm-up walk through birch and fir forest
First views of the Beas Kund cirque ridgeline
Thorough mountaineering equipment checks
Detailed briefing on the expedition's technical structure
tips: Double-check that all rented mountaineering gear fits properly before leaving Manali
Treat today as recovery and preparation rather than a race to camp
Ask questions now about the technical sections; clarity today prevents confusion higher up

itinerary.title: Bakarthach to Base Camp near Beas Kund
itinerary.trailType: moraine trail, glacial approach, high camp
itinerary.description: The walk to base camp climbs more purposefully than the previous day, following the valley upward past increasingly sparse tree cover and onto open, rocky ground that marks the transition into genuine high-mountain terrain. The Beas Kund basin itself, a glacial cirque considered the mythological source of the Beas river in local tradition, comes into view gradually, ringed by a dramatic wall of peaks that includes Friendship Peak's own summit, visible now as a distinct, snow-streaked pyramid at the head of the valley. Base camp is established on a relatively flat moraine shelf with reliable access to glacial meltwater, chosen for its balance of proximity to the mountain and protection from the worst of the wind that regularly funnels down from the surrounding ridgelines. Reaching this altitude for the first time, most members of the expedition notice a clear shift in physical exertion required for even simple tasks, and the guide team begins closely monitoring acclimatisation from this point forward, since the days ahead depend entirely on the group adjusting properly to the thinning air. Views from camp are genuinely spectacular, taking in not only Friendship Peak but neighbouring summits including glimpses toward Hanuman Tibba's more technical pyramid further along the ridge, and the sense of having arrived at a serious mountaineering objective, rather than simply a scenic high camp, settles in quickly. The afternoon is kept deliberately light, with an early dinner and clear instructions for the following day's rest and skills-focused schedule, since base camp will serve as home for the next two nights while the group prepares properly for the technical climbing ahead.
highlights: Arrival in the dramatic Beas Kund glacial cirque
First full view of Friendship Peak's summit pyramid
Establishing base camp on the moraine shelf
Beginning of close acclimatisation monitoring
tips: Hydrate aggressively from today onward; altitude-related dehydration is a common early issue
Keep gear organised at base camp, since the days ahead move at a faster technical pace
Report even minor headaches or nausea early rather than waiting to see if they pass

itinerary.title: Base Camp Rest and Technical Skills Training
itinerary.trailType: glacier skills training ground, practice slopes
itinerary.description: With base camp established, this day is dedicated entirely to acclimatisation and technical preparation rather than further altitude gain, and the morning begins with a briefing on rope work, ice axe technique, and self-arrest practice conducted on a moderate practice slope within easy walking distance of camp. For many in the group, this is a first genuine introduction to real mountaineering movement, distinct from the trekking skills used on the approach so far, and the instructors, typically certified mountaineering guides rather than trekking leaders alone, run through crampon fitting, roped movement in a line, and the specific hand signals and commands used during the actual summit attempt. Practice continues through much of the day, with repeated drills on the practice slope until the basic movements feel natural rather than deliberate, since fatigue and thin air on the actual summit day leave little room for hesitation or confusion about technique. Between training sessions, rest is emphasised heavily, and the guide team continues close monitoring of how each member is adjusting to the altitude, occasionally adjusting the following days' plan based on how the group as a whole is coping. Weather patterns are also a major topic of discussion today, since the local guide team tracks forecasts closely and the entire remaining schedule, including which day the summit attempt is eventually made, depends heavily on finding a stable weather window in the days ahead. Evening at base camp, now more familiar after a second night, carries a noticeably more serious, focused atmosphere than the earlier days of the expedition, as the reality of the technical climbing ahead becomes concrete rather than abstract, and most members turn in early, aware that the following days will test both fitness and nerve in equal measure.
highlights: Roped movement and self-arrest technique training
Crampon and ice axe skills practice on a nearby slope
Continued altitude monitoring and rest
Weather window planning for the summit attempt
tips: Take the skills training seriously; these exact techniques will be used, tired and cold, on summit day
Use today's rest time properly rather than wandering far from camp
Ask your mountaineering instructor to repeat any technique you're unsure of before moving on

itinerary.title: Base Camp to Advanced Base Camp
itinerary.trailType: glacier crossing, crevasse zone, snow slope
itinerary.description: The move to advanced base camp marks the point where the expedition's character shifts fully from trekking to technical mountaineering, the route crossing an initial stretch of moraine before roping up for a traverse across the lower reaches of the glacier that flows down from Friendship Peak's northern flank. Crevasses, though generally well marked and avoided by the experienced guide team, are a visible and sobering feature of this terrain, and moving as a roped team for the first time on genuinely glaciated ground focuses concentration sharply after the previous day's practice-slope drills. The climb continues steadily upward over increasingly firm snow, and the air grows noticeably thinner with every couloir. Advanced base camp itself sits on a relatively sheltered rock and snow bench at close to 15,000 feet, chosen for partial protection from wind and reasonably direct access to the summit route that lies just above. From here, Friendship Peak's final approach becomes clearly visible for the first time, including the steeper snow and rock sections that will demand the most technical care during the actual summit push. The shorter walking distance today compared to earlier days is intentional, allowing the body time to adjust to yet another significant jump in altitude before the summit attempt itself. Rest is prioritised heavily through the afternoon, and an early, calorie-dense dinner is served in preparation for what will be an extremely early departure the following morning. The mood at advanced base camp is markedly quieter and more focused than lower camps, every member of the expedition acutely aware that tomorrow represents the culmination of the entire trip.
highlights: First roped glacier crossing of the expedition
Advanced base camp at close to 15,000 ft
Clear view of the technical summit approach
Final rest and preparation before the summit push
tips: Move deliberately and stay clipped in during the roped glacier crossing
Eat a substantial, calorie-dense dinner tonight regardless of appetite
Pack your summit kit and lay out clothing tonight for a fast, organised pre-dawn start

itinerary.title: Summit Day: Friendship Peak
itinerary.trailType: fixed rope sections, steep snow/ice slope, technical summit ridge
itinerary.description: The summit attempt begins in the deep cold of pre-dawn darkness, headlamps and crampon points the only points of reference as the roped team sets out across firm, frozen snow that offers considerably better and safer footing than the softer conditions that typically develop after sunrise. The initial stretch climbs at a steady, deliberately unhurried pace across a broad snow slope, the rhythm of ice axe, crampon, and rope discipline settling into something closer to routine after days of practice, even as the altitude makes every movement feel heavier than it should. As the route steepens toward the upper mountain, sections of mixed snow and rock demand more careful, technical movement, and the guide team places additional protection on the more exposed stretches, slowing the pace further but adding an important margin of safety given the consequences of a slip on this terrain. Dawn breaks dramatically over the surrounding peaks as the team gains the final ridge, and the increasingly thin air makes conversation minimal, most energy reserved purely for the physical and mental effort of continued upward movement. The summit of Friendship Peak, at 5,289 metres, delivers a view that ranks among the most spectacular achievable from any trekking-accessible point in Himachal, with the Beas Kund cirque directly below and a vast panorama of the Pir Panjal and Dhauladhar ranges extending in every direction, including striking sightlines toward Hanuman Tibba's steeper pyramid rising just across the ridge. Time at the summit is necessarily brief, given both the cold and the need to begin descending before the snow softens further under the climbing sun, but the sense of achievement among the team, several days and thousands of feet of preparation now culminating in this single moment, is profound and unmistakable. The descent, technically demanding in its own right given softening snow and general fatigue, is managed with the same careful rope discipline used on the ascent, and reaching advanced base camp again by early afternoon brings a wave of relief and quiet, exhausted celebration among a team that has just completed a genuine Himalayan mountaineering objective.
highlights: Summit of Friendship Peak at 5,289 metres
Pre-dawn roped ascent across firm snow
Panoramic views spanning the Pir Panjal and Dhauladhar ranges
Safe, disciplined descent under softening snow conditions
tips: Trust your training from the skills days; summit day is not the time to improvise technique
Dress in your warmest layers for the pre-dawn start; temperatures are brutally cold before sunrise
Descend with the same care and rope discipline as the ascent, even when fatigue sets in

itinerary.title: Descent to Bakarthach and Dhundi
itinerary.trailType: glacier descent, moraine trail, forest return
itinerary.description: With the summit behind the team, today is devoted entirely to descending, retracing the glacier crossing and moraine trail back down through base camp and continuing further to Bakarthach or Dhundi depending on the group's pace and overall energy levels after the previous day's exertion. The descent through the crevassed glacier section is handled with the same rope discipline used on the way up, though the mood is markedly lighter, with the technical seriousness of summit day replaced by a satisfied, talkative energy as team members compare their individual experiences of the climb. As altitude is lost, the air thickens noticeably, and by the time the trail re-enters the birch and fir forest below Bakarthach, most in the group report feeling physically restored simply by the increase in available oxygen, even as legs remain heavily fatigued from two consecutive demanding days. Passing familiar landmarks from the approach in reverse carries a distinct sense of closure, and guides often use the walk to share technical details or stories from previous Friendship Peak expeditions, contextualising the team's own achievement within the broader history of climbing in this valley. Reaching lower ground by late afternoon, whether at Bakarthach or continuing all the way to Dhundi depending on timing and energy, marks the practical end of the expedition's technical phase, and the evening is spent in genuine celebration, with the entire team processing both the physical accomplishment and the specific, vivid memory of standing atop a genuine 5,000-metre Himalayan summit after days of careful, disciplined preparation.
highlights: Final roped descent through the glaciated section
Noticeable relief and energy return at lower altitude
Team reflection and shared summit-day stories
Return to forested terrain below Bakarthach
tips: Stay attentive during the glacier descent even though the summit is behind you; fatigue increases risk
Rehydrate seriously tonight to aid recovery after two demanding high-altitude days
Celebrate, but keep gear organised for tomorrow's final walk out

itinerary.title: Dhundi to Manali, Expedition Conclusion
itinerary.trailType: riverside trail, motorable roadhead
itinerary.description: The final day is a short, easy walk from Dhundi back to the road, followed by a drive into Manali that effectively closes out the expedition, and the contrast between the technical intensity of the preceding days and this relaxed final stretch is stark and genuinely welcome. The walk itself passes quickly, familiar now after six days of travel through this same valley system, and the group typically arrives at the roadhead by mid-morning, well ahead of the drive back into town. Manali's return brings the first hot shower and proper bed in a week, along with the practical tasks of returning rented mountaineering gear and settling any final expedition logistics with the operator. Many teams use the afternoon for a relaxed lunch together, an opportunity to properly debrief on the entire experience, from the early acclimatisation days through the skills training to the summit push itself, often the first chance for genuine reflection now that the physical demands of the expedition are fully behind them. Friendship Peak's particular appeal, as guides frequently point out during this closing conversation, lies precisely in offering a genuine, moderately technical mountaineering objective that remains achievable for reasonably fit trekkers with proper preparation, a stepping stone of real significance for anyone considering more serious Himalayan climbing objectives in the future. From Manali, onward travel typically continues toward Delhi or further into Himachal, and most team members leave already discussing which peak might be next. The sense of camaraderie built over a week of shared effort at altitude tends to outlast the trip itself, with many teams staying in touch long after returning home.

Return to Manali's comforts after a week in the mountains
Full expedition debrief and reflection
Discussion of Friendship Peak's role as a mountaineering stepping stone

Use the closing lunch to get contact details of teammates; shared expedition bonds run deep

title: Hanuman Tibba Base Camp Trek
summary: Expedition-style approach toward Hanuman Ka Tibba (Friendship Peak massif region logistics vary) with high camps, glacier travel briefing, and serious mountain days. From ₹24,999.
overview:
Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp is part of India Holiday Destinations’ Dharamshala and Dhauladhar collection. Expedition-style approach toward Hanuman Ka Tibba (Friendship Peak massif region logistics vary) with high camps, glacier travel briefing, and serious mountain days.

Base-camp logistics for Hanuman Ka Tibba demand expedition discipline — staged camps, glacier briefings, and weather patience before any summit talk begins.

The itinerary usually starts at Bharmour / designated roadhead and ends at Base camp below Hanuman Tibba approaches, spanning about 55 km across 6 day(s) with a high point near 15,500 ft. Graded challenging, it demands respect for Himachal weather, Leave No Trace camping, and honest self-assessment before you book.

Best time to visit: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

Flora along the Hanuman Ka Tibba BC corridor: Regional mix of chir pine, ban oak, rhododendron, deodar, and high-altitude scrub depending on elevation band.

Wildlife notes for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp: Himalayan birds (monal, griffons), langurs in forests, and occasional goral on steep grass — observe from distance.

Photography spots: Hanuman Ka Tibba BC main viewpoint; Valley light at golden hour; Camp ridgeline panoramas; Village / temple cultural frames.

Camping locations: Designated Hanuman Ka Tibba BC camping zones; Backup lower camp in bad weather.

Water sources: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

Network availability: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

Electricity availability: No reliable charging on trail — carry power banks.

ATM availability: Last reliable ATMs in Dharamshala, McLeod Ganj, Palampur, Baijnath, or Bharmour depending on approach.

Medical facilities: Basic trail first aid; nearest substantial care in Dharamshala / regional district hospitals.

Permits required: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

Forest permissions: Follow Himachal Forest Department and local administration rules for camping, fire, and waste.

Safety on Hanuman Ka Tibba BC: Stay on marked trails and follow your trek leader’s briefings. Turn back early if weather deteriorates — Dhauladhar storms build quickly. Never wander alone after dark near cliffs, snow patches, or stream crossings. Always follow your trek leader’s brief for this specific route.

Travel tips: Arrive in Dharamshala / Bharmour approach a night early when mornings start at the trailhead. Download offline maps; do not rely on trail network. Carry cash for village tea shops and emergency transport.

Responsible tourism on Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp: Leave no trace — pack out all non-biodegradable waste. Use designated camping zones and toilet practices advised by your guide. Support local porters, mules, and homestays fairly.

Nearby attractions from this trailhead: McLeod Ganj, Bhagsu Nag, Kangra Fort, Bir Billing, Masroor Temples.

Nearby treks: Hanuman Ka Tibba Expedition, Moon Peak Trek, Dhauladhar Base Camp Trek, Kareri Lake Trek.

Child policy for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp: 16+ experienced preferred. Children are welcome on easier Dhauladhar trails when accompanied by a parent or guardian. Minimum age depends on the route (typically 8+ for short day hikes, 12+ for overnight alpine camps). Share ages at booking for route-specific advice.

Private departures: Private Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp departures can be arranged for families and corporate groups subject to guide availability.

Group discount: Group discount available for 6+ travellers on the same fixed departure.

Booking and cancellation policies for India Holiday Destinations packages apply — see FAQs on this page for the current fee schedule.

itinerary.title: Manali to Dhundi and Solang Approach
itinerary.trailType: motorable road, riverside approach
itinerary.description: The trek to Hanuman Tibba base camp begins with a short drive from Manali to Solang and onward to Dhundi, the last motorable point on this side of the valley, from where the mountains that will dominate the coming days first come properly into view. Even from Dhundi's roadhead, the pyramid-shaped summit of Hanuman Tibba is visible in the distance on clear days, its distinctive profile rising well above the surrounding ridgeline and giving an early sense of scale for what lies ahead, even though this particular trek is designed to reach only its base rather than attempt the technical summit itself. A short walk from the roadhead settles the group into the rhythm of the trail, following the young Beas river through a corridor of birch and fir that offers welcome shade during the warmer parts of the day. This first stretch is intentionally undemanding, allowing time for gear checks and a relaxed group briefing on the days ahead, since the trek's difficulty builds steadily rather than beginning immediately. Local guides, many of whom have supported mountaineering expeditions on Hanuman Tibba's technical routes over the years, share stories of past attempts on the peak, giving useful context for just how serious an objective the summit represents even for experienced climbers, in contrast to the more accessible base camp experience this itinerary offers. Camp for the night is set on a grassy shelf near Dhundi, with the evening's cool mountain air and clear views toward the peaks ahead offering a fitting, calm start to the week.
highlights: First distant views of Hanuman Tibba's pyramid summit
Easy warm-up walk along the young Beas river
Local guide stories from past Hanuman Tibba expeditions
Relaxed first camp near Dhundi
tips: Use today to settle gear and layering systems before the trail steepens
Ask guides about Hanuman Tibba's climbing history; the context enriches the coming days
Keep cameras ready for early, clear views of the peak from Dhundi

itinerary.title: Dhundi to Bakarthach
itinerary.trailType: forest trail, river crossing, meadow camp
itinerary.description: The second day climbs gradually through continuing forest cover, the trail following the valley's natural contour as birch gives way gradually to more open, scrubbier vegetation with increasing altitude. The Beas river remains a close companion throughout the walk, its water growing colder and more heavily laden with glacial sediment the further upstream the trail progresses, and several informal crossings test balance without posing serious difficulty. Bakarthach, a broad grazing meadow used seasonally by local shepherds, offers the day's first wide views back down the valley toward Solang and, ahead, glimpses of the high ridgeline enclosing the Beas Kund basin that the trek will enter the following day. Wildflowers bloom across sections of the meadow through the early summer trekking window, and marmots are a common sight darting between rocks near the campsite, their sharp alarm calls carrying easily across the open ground. This is a comfortable, moderate day designed to continue acclimatisation steadily rather than aggressively, and the guide team uses the relatively early arrival at camp to run through the coming days' plan in detail, including what to expect at Beas Kund and the specific approach to Hanuman Tibba's base camp that follows. Evening temperatures drop noticeably compared to Dhundi, and down layers come out for the first time, a small but clear signal of the altitude gain still to come. Local shepherds camped nearby are often happy to share tea and news of trail conditions higher up, an informal but genuinely useful source of real-time information on snow cover near Beas Kund this particular season.
highlights: Riverside walk with views toward the Beas Kund ridgeline
Shepherd meadows and marmot sightings at Bakarthach
Steady, well-paced acclimatisation
Detailed briefing on the approach to Hanuman Tibba
tips: Refill water at reliable stream sources before Bakarthach, as they become less frequent higher up
Watch for marmots near camp, but keep a respectful distance
Start layering up earlier in the evening from today onward

itinerary.title: Bakarthach to Beas Kund
itinerary.trailType: moraine trail, glacial lake basin
itinerary.description: The walk to Beas Kund climbs more purposefully than the previous two days, the trail leaving the last scattered trees behind and entering the open, rocky terrain of a genuine glacial cirque considered, in local tradition, to be the mythological source of the Beas river. The basin is ringed by a dramatic wall of peaks, and this is the point in the trek where Hanuman Tibba's full scale becomes unmistakably apparent, its steep, technical faces rising directly from the valley floor in a way that clearly separates it from the more approachable trekking peaks found elsewhere in this same basin. Friendship Peak's more moderate pyramid is also visible from here, offering a useful visual comparison that underscores just how much more serious an objective Hanuman Tibba represents for climbers. The small lake at Beas Kund itself, often still partially frozen even in early summer, sits in a stark, boulder-strewn setting that feels considerably wilder than anything walked through on the previous two days, and the thinning air here makes even short uphill stretches noticeably more tiring. Camp is set on a relatively sheltered patch of moraine near the lake, and altitude effects become a more serious consideration from this point onward, with the guide team beginning closer monitoring of the group's acclimatisation. The evening is spent absorbing the scale and drama of the surrounding cirque, with guides pointing out specific technical routes on Hanuman Tibba's face that experienced mountaineering teams have historically attempted, adding real context to the following day's approach toward the peak's base.
highlights: Arrival in the dramatic Beas Kund glacial cirque
Full view of Hanuman Tibba's technical climbing faces
Comparison views toward the more moderate Friendship Peak
Closer acclimatisation monitoring begins
tips: Move at a measured pace today; the altitude gain is significant compared to previous days
Photograph Hanuman Tibba's faces in the clear morning light before afternoon cloud typically builds
Keep warm layers accessible, as Beas Kund's temperature drop after sunset is sharp

itinerary.title: Beas Kund to Hanuman Tibba Base Camp
itinerary.trailType: glacier edge trail, boulder field, high camp beneath the pyramid peak
itinerary.description: The final approach to base camp moves along the edge of the glacier that flows down from Hanuman Tibba's northern flank, the trail crossing increasingly rocky, moraine-strewn ground that demands careful, deliberate footwork rather than speed. Vegetation has entirely disappeared by this point, replaced by a stark landscape of bare rock, ice, and scattered boulder fields, and the thinning air makes the walk feel considerably harder than the modest distance covered would suggest at lower altitude. As the trail rounds the final rocky shoulder, Hanuman Tibba's full pyramid comes into complete, close view for the first time, its steep snow and rock faces rising directly overhead in a way that few other Himachal peaks replicate this close to a standard trekking route. Base camp itself sits on a relatively flat, sheltered bench chosen specifically for its proximity to the mountain while offering some protection from the wind that regularly sweeps down off the higher slopes. Reaching this point, directly beneath one of Himachal's most technically demanding unclimbed-by-casual-trekkers peaks, delivers a genuine sense of scale and achievement even without any technical climbing involved, and many in the group describe this as the most dramatic mountain scenery encountered anywhere on the trek. The afternoon is spent resting and absorbing the setting, with guides sharing further detail on the peak's climbing history and the specific technical challenges, including a notoriously exposed summit ridge, that keep Hanuman Tibba firmly in mountaineering rather than trekking territory. Evening at base camp, silent except for occasional rockfall echoing off the surrounding faces, is genuinely memorable, the cold, clear night sky framing the peak's silhouette in striking detail.
highlights: Arrival at base camp directly beneath Hanuman Tibba's pyramid
Close views of the peak's technical climbing faces and summit ridge
Stark, glacier-edge high-altitude landscape
Detailed context on the peak's mountaineering history
tips: Move carefully across the moraine and boulder sections; loose rock is common underfoot
Layer up well before dinner, as base camp's exposed position brings sharp temperature drops
Take time to simply absorb the setting; this is the trek's visual highlight

itinerary.title: Base Camp Exploration and Return to Beas Kund
itinerary.trailType: high altitude exploration, moraine ridge viewpoint
itinerary.description: With base camp reached, this day is used for exploration at a gentler pace, following the moraine ridge a short distance further to gain slightly different, elevated perspectives on Hanuman Tibba's faces and the broader glacial basin surrounding it. Guides point out features used by mountaineering expeditions on past summit attempts, including specific camps and route lines on the mountain's flank, giving genuine insight into what a technical climb of this peak actually involves compared to the trekking-accessible base camp experience. The exploration walk also offers views toward neighbouring summits, including a different angle on Friendship Peak and, on clear days, glimpses further along the ridge toward other technical peaks in this same mountain group. This lighter day serves a dual purpose, allowing for additional acclimatisation before the descent begins in earnest and giving the group a proper chance to appreciate the scale of the terrain without the pressure of continued altitude gain. By early afternoon, the group begins retracing steps back toward Beas Kund, the descent noticeably easier on the body given the modest altitude loss and increasing oxygen availability. Reaching Beas Kund a second time carries a different feel than the first arrival, familiar now rather than intimidating, and the evening is spent in relaxed conversation reviewing photographs and impressions from the closest possible encounter with Hanuman Tibba available to trekkers rather than technical climbers. Guides often use this final evening at altitude to discuss how the peak's reputation among Himachal's mountaineering community has evolved over the decades, and why it remains a serious, rarely attempted objective even today.
highlights: Elevated viewpoint walk around the base camp moraine
Insight into historical mountaineering routes on Hanuman Tibba
Comparative views of neighbouring technical peaks
Return descent to the familiar Beas Kund camp
tips: Use today's lighter pace to fully absorb the mountain scenery before departing tomorrow
Ask your guide to point out specific climbing routes on the peak's face for added context
Rest well tonight, as tomorrow's descent covers considerable distance

itinerary.title: Beas Kund to Dhundi and Manali
itinerary.trailType: forest descent, riverside trail, roadhead
itinerary.description: The final day retraces the full descent from Beas Kund through Bakarthach and down to Dhundi, the trail now thoroughly familiar and considerably easier to manage than on the way up, given both the altitude loss and the group's improved acclimatisation over the preceding days. Forest cover returns gradually as height is lost, and the increasing warmth and oxygen at lower elevation bring a noticeable lift in energy despite the cumulative fatigue of five days on the trail. The walk offers a final chance to look back up the valley toward Hanuman Tibba's pyramid, its details already softening with distance, a fitting visual close to a trek built entirely around a close encounter with one of Himachal's most formidable peaks. Reaching Dhundi by early afternoon allows time for the short drive back into Manali, where hot showers, proper beds, and a relaxed closing meal mark the practical end of the trek. Guides typically use the drive to answer any final questions about the mountain and its climbing history, and reflections on the week often centre on the stark contrast between this trek's straightforward walking difficulty and the genuinely serious technical challenge that Hanuman Tibba itself represents to any climbing team attempting its summit. From Manali, onward travel is easily arranged, and the memory of standing directly beneath that dramatic pyramid summit tends to remain one of the most vivid parts of the entire Himachal trekking experience for most who complete this route. Many leave already planning a return, whether for a longer trek elsewhere in the valley or, for a smaller number, a future technical attempt on the peak itself.
highlights: Full descent retracing the approach route
Final views back toward Hanuman Tibba's summit
Return to Manali's comforts after five days on the trail
Closing reflection on the mountain's serious climbing reputation
tips: Use trekking poles on the long descent to ease strain on tired knees
Confirm your Manali accommodation or onward travel plans before the final drive down
Keep photographs of the peak's face; the detail is rarely visible this closely from any road

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: This route is rated challenging. The Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp demands challenging effort across 6 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 15500 ft.

faqs.question: How long does the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp take?
faqs.answer: The standard package is 6D / 5N. Actual walking time varies with fitness, weather, and trail conditions.

faqs.question: What is the total trekking distance for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Expect roughly 55 km of walking across the itinerary (out-and-back or point-to-point depending on the route plan).

faqs.question: What is the maximum altitude on Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: The highest point is about 15,500 ft (4,724 m). Ascend steadily, hydrate, and tell your guide if you feel altitude-related symptoms on higher Dhauladhar routes.

faqs.question: Where does the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp start?
faqs.answer: The usual starting point is Bharmour / designated roadhead. Arrive the evening before when the itinerary begins early.

faqs.question: When is the best time for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: April–June and September–November for most stable Dhauladhar weather

faqs.question: Is mobile network available on Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Expect patchy or zero network beyond the last town. BSNL sometimes works in remote valleys.

faqs.question: Do I need permits for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Inner forest / camping permits as required for the specific corridor; included when stated in package.

faqs.question: Is drinking water available on the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp trail?
faqs.answer: Carry adequate water from the last village; treat natural sources before drinking.

faqs.question: Where do we camp or stay on Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Designated Hanuman Ka Tibba BC camping zones Backup lower camp in bad weather

faqs.question: Can beginners attempt the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: It depends on the grade. The Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp demands challenging effort across 6 day(s). Train with stair climbs and loaded walks; respect altitude above 15500 ft. If you are new to the Himalayas, choose a guided departure and prepare with regular walks carrying a daypack.

faqs.question: How do I reach the trailhead for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Fly into Gaggal (DHM) or take a train to Pathankot, then road transfer to Dharamshala / Bharmour approach. Local taxis and shared jeeps cover the final approach to Bharmour / designated roadhead.

faqs.question: What should I pack for Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Carry layered clothing, broken-in trekking shoes, rain shell, headlamp, sunscreen, personal medicines, and a power bank. A detailed packing list is provided on this page and with your booking confirmation.

faqs.question: Is Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp safe for solo travellers?
faqs.answer: Guided group departures are the safest option for solo travellers in the Dhauladhar. Trails can be remote, weather changes fast, and night navigation is risky without local knowledge.

faqs.question: What is the cancellation and refund policy?
faqs.answer: Cancellations 45+ days before departure: 25% fee. 15–30 days: 50% fee. Within 0–15 days: 100% of the trek cost. For weather or government restrictions, we try to offer a viable alternative; cash refund may not be available. Approved refunds are credited to the original payment method in 10–12 working days.

faqs.question: Are meals included on the Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Trek-day meals are included as listed in the package inclusions (typically vegetarian). Confirm dietary needs when you book so the kitchen team can plan.

faqs.question: What medical facilities are near Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp?
faqs.answer: Basic clinics exist in Dharamshala / Bharmour approach. For serious emergencies, evacuation aims for Civil / Zonal Hospital Dharamshala. Carry personal medication and inform us of any conditions before departure.

faqs.question: Is Hanuman Ka Tibba Base Camp crowded?
faqs.answer: Hanuman Ka Tibba BC sees far fewer groups than Triund — expect quieter camps and longer self-sufficiency.

faqs.question: Can I combine Hanuman Ka Tibba BC with Triund?
faqs.answer: Many travellers pair easier Dharamshala walks with Triund or Kareri Lake on separate days. Ask us for a custom multi-trek plan.
